MUI's Material Design aesthetic requires 30+ overrides. Recharts is added separately for charts.

Dexie.js typed repositories align with ADR-001 (data persistence). Typed Table<T> generics provide compile-time safety. liveQuery() hooks integrate directly with React for reactive UI. Zustand selectors lack query capabilities for Insights aggregations. RxDB adds 170KB for unused sync features. sql.js adds a 1.2MB WASM binary with cold-start latency. The repository pattern keeps entity logic encapsulated and testable.

Zustand as an in-memory cache on top of Dexie persistence gives synchronous reads (sub-150ms micro-interactions) with durable storage. Pure Dexie reads are async and require loading states. HTTP cache and React Query presuppose a network layer that doesn't exist. localStorage + useMemo lacks structured queries. Zustand's ~3KB footprint is negligible and the persist middleware provides localStorage fallback.

CashPulse is a personal expense tracker, not a payment processor. It records past expenses, not future payments. The PRD explicitly excludes bank integration, backend APIs, and cloud storage. No payment provider SDK is needed. The 'payment method' field is a user-selected categorization label. All data is stored client-side via Dexie.js/Zustand per ADR-001/005. Note: despite the alternative name referencing localStorage, actual persistence uses Dexie.js (ADR-001) — the key decision is 'no payment provider'.

Inline contextual feedback matches CashPulse's 'zero clutter' design philosophy and the PRD's specific interaction patterns (inline '✓ Saved!' on button, inline 'Confirm delete?' with 3-second auto-revert). Supplemented with Radix Toast (already in dependency tree via shadcn/ui) for the PRD-specified currency change toast and CSV export confirmation. A full Zustand notification store with bell icon over-engineers beyond requirements. Custom toast context reinvents Radix's accessibility features.

Client-side in-memory filtering on the Zustand store delivers sub-5ms filter response times for realistic single-user datasets (1,500-3,000 records). The PRD's search feature is 'could-have' priority. The Zustand store already holds all expense data in memory (ADR-005), making array filtering zero-cost. Fuse.js is the documented upgrade path for fuzzy matching. URL-driven params add routing complexity without UX benefit for a 3-screen app.

implementationPatterns.title: Dexie.js Repository Pattern for All Persistence
implementationPatterns.rule: All entity persistence MUST go through typed Dexie repository modules (e.g., ExpenseRepository, CategoryRepository). Each repository exports async CRUD methods and query helpers. Direct IndexedDB access outside repositories is prohibited. Schema is defined in a single db.ts file with versioned migrations. Use Dexie's liveQuery() for reactive data binding in React components.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-store and gap-orm-strategy decisions. Replaces baseline data-access-drizzle-only since there is no server-side ORM — Dexie.js is the client-side equivalent. Centralizing data access in repositories prevents schema drift and ensures all screens use consistent query patterns.
forbids: Direct IndexedDB API calls outside db.ts
Raw localStorage for entity persistence (use Dexie)
Importing dexie in component files (use repository or hook abstractions)

implementationPatterns.title: Inline Contextual Feedback for Errors and Confirmations
implementationPatterns.rule: All user-facing feedback MUST be rendered inline and contextually: form validation errors appear as caption text below the invalid field, success confirmations appear as inline state changes on the triggering element (e.g., button text changes to '✓ Saved!'), and destructive action confirmations use inline text replacement with auto-revert. Use Radix Toast (@radix-ui/react-toast) ONLY for cross-context notifications (currency change, CSV export). Toast position: bottom-center. Auto-dismiss: 2 seconds. All feedback elements MUST include appropriate ARIA attributes (role='status' for success, role='alert' for errors).
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-notification-channel decision (inline contextual feedback). Replaces baseline api-error-rfc7807 since there is no HTTP API — all errors are client-side validation or UI state errors. The PRD specifies inline patterns: Save button shows '✓ Saved!', Delete shows 'Confirm delete?' with 3-second auto-revert, currency change shows bottom-center toast.
forbids: Global error boundary modals for validation errors
Browser alert() or confirm() dialogs
Floating toasts for form validation — use inline messages

implementationPatterns.title: Tailwind via Design Tokens Only — No Arbitrary Values
implementationPatterns.rule: All styling MUST use Tailwind utility classes mapped to design tokens defined in tailwind.config.js. Arbitrary value syntax (e.g., w-[13px], bg-[#abc]) is PROHIBITED — all values must come from the extended theme config. Custom token extensions: rounded-card (16px), rounded-button (10px), rounded-badge (20px), rounded-input (8px), shadow-card, shadow-elevated. Use CVA (cva()) for all reusable component variants (Button, Card, Badge, Input, Chip). Use tailwind-merge (cn() helper) for class composition. The motion-reduce: variant MUST be applied to all animation/transition classes.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-styling-approach decision (Tailwind + CVA). Refines the baseline component-tailwind-tokens-only pattern with CashPulse-specific token extensions and the CVA requirement. Prevents style divergence across 25 features by enforcing that all values trace back to tailwind.config.js.
forbids: Arbitrary Tailwind values (w-[13px], bg-[#hex])
Inline style attributes for layout/spacing/color
CSS-in-JS runtime libraries (styled-components, Emotion)

implementationPatterns.title: Zustand Client-Side Cache as Single Source of Truth for UI
implementationPatterns.rule: All UI components MUST read data from the Zustand store, never directly from Dexie repositories. On app initialization, hydrate the Zustand store from Dexie. Writes MUST follow the optimistic update pattern: update Zustand first (immediate UI feedback), then persist to Dexie (durable storage). Derived data (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) MUST be computed as Zustand selectors using useShallow to prevent unnecessary re-renders. Each entity slice is a separate Zustand slice composed via the slices pattern.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-caching-strategy decision. Replaces baseline state-server-only since there is no server — the Zustand store is the authoritative UI state. Synchronous Zustand reads guarantee sub-150ms micro-interactions. The optimistic update pattern ensures the Dashboard refreshes instantly after Add Expense without waiting for Dexie writes.
forbids: Direct Dexie reads in React components (use Zustand selectors)
Storing derived/computed data in Dexie (compute in selectors)
Multiple sources of truth for the same entity

implementationPatterns.title: Zod Validation at Data Entry Boundaries
implementationPatterns.rule: All user input MUST be validated using Zod schemas before persisting to the Zustand store or Dexie. Define one Zod schema per entity (e.g., ExpenseSchema, CategorySchema, BudgetSchema). Validation runs on form submission and on data hydration from Dexie (to catch corrupted/migrated data). Validation errors are surfaced as inline field-level messages per the error-handling pattern. Amount validation: z.number().min(0.01).max(99999.99).multipleOf(0.01). Date validation: z.string().refine(d => new Date(d) <= new Date(), 'Future dates not allowed'). Note validation: z.string().max(100).optional().
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from constraint-nfr-009 (data integrity) and the assumption ledger entry on instant validation. Adapts baseline validation-zod-at-boundary from HTTP boundaries to client-side form boundaries. Zod provides TypeScript type inference from schemas, ensuring type safety flows from validation to Dexie persistence. Silent rejection of non-numeric characters is handled at the input component level (inputMode='decimal'), while Zod catches edge cases at the persistence boundary.
forbids: Persisting data without Zod validation
Manual type assertions (as Expense) without validation
Validation logic scattered across components — centralize in schema files

implementationPatterns.title: No Authentication — Single-User Client-Side App
implementationPatterns.rule: CashPulse has NO authentication layer. All data belongs to a single implicit user and is stored locally in the browser. Do NOT add auth middleware, session management, login screens, or user ID fields on entities. The UserSettings entity (entity-004) stores preferences (currency, default budget) without any user identity. If multi-user support is ever added, this decision must be revisited via a new ADR.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-auth-strategy (resolved as 'none' by scope-exclusion) and constraint-scope-1 (user authentication out of scope). Overrides baseline auth-middleware-required since there is no server and no multi-user requirement. Adding auth infrastructure would violate scope exclusions and add unnecessary complexity.
forbids: User ID fields on any entity
Auth middleware or guards
Login/signup screens or flows
JWT/session token handling

implementationPatterns.title: Console Logging in Development Only
implementationPatterns.rule: Use console.warn() and console.error() for development-time debugging of data layer operations (Dexie errors, Zod validation failures, Zustand hydration issues). All console statements MUST be stripped or gated behind a DEV flag in production builds. Do NOT add a structured logging library (Pino, Winston) — there is no server to aggregate logs. For production error tracking, consider adding Sentry as a future enhancement (not in initial scope).
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from the frontend-only architecture. Overrides baseline logging-structured-pino since there is no server-side runtime. Pino is a Node.js library inappropriate for a browser-only app. Console logging is sufficient for a single-user desktop app during development. Production error tracking (Sentry) is a documented upgrade path but not in initial scope.
forbids: console.log in production builds
Server-side logging libraries (Pino, Winston)
Unguarded console statements in component files

implementationPatterns.title: Consistent Focus Ring on All Interactive Elements
implementationPatterns.rule: Every interactive element (buttons, links, inputs, chips, tabs, chart data points) MUST have a visible focus ring: 2px blue-600 outline with 2px offset. Implement via Tailwind classes: focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-primary focus-visible:ring-offset-2. Minimum click target: 36×36px (min-h-9 min-w-9). All color-coded statuses MUST include a text label (never color alone). Charts MUST include role='img' with descriptive aria-label. Keyboard navigation: Tab, Enter, Space, Escape on all interactive elements.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from constraint-nfr-003 (hard WCAG 2.1 AA constraint). This pattern ensures consistent accessibility implementation across all 7 screens and 25 features. The focus-visible pseudo-class (vs focus) prevents focus rings on mouse clicks while maintaining keyboard accessibility. Radix UI primitives (via shadcn/ui) handle focus management for complex widgets (Dialog, Popover, Select).
forbids: Interactive elements without focus-visible ring
Click targets smaller than 36×36px
Color as the sole status indicator

implementationPatterns.title: Respect prefers-reduced-motion on All Animations
implementationPatterns.rule: Every CSS transition and animation class MUST be paired with a motion-reduce: variant that disables or reduces the animation. Use Tailwind's motion-reduce:transition-none for transitions and motion-reduce:animate-none for keyframe animations. Chart entry animations (600ms staggered) MUST render instantly when prefers-reduced-motion is set. Screen slide transitions (350ms) MUST be replaced with instant cuts. Number counter animations (400ms) MUST show final values immediately. Check window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)') in JavaScript for programmatic animation control (e.g., Recharts animation prop).
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from constraint-nfr-004 (soft accessibility constraint). The PRD specifies specific reduced-motion behaviors: instant chart renders, no bounce/cascade effects, instant screen cuts. This pattern ensures consistent implementation across all animation touchpoints.
forbids: Animation classes without motion-reduce: counterpart
Hardcoded animation durations without reduced-motion check

implementationPatterns.title: UUID v4 for All Entity IDs
implementationPatterns.rule: All entity IDs MUST be generated using crypto.randomUUID() (native browser API). Do NOT use auto-incrementing integers (IndexedDB default) or timestamp-based IDs. IDs are generated client-side before persistence. The Zod schema for each entity validates id as z.string().uuid(). This ensures IDs are globally unique, non-sequential, and safe for potential future sync scenarios.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-store and gap-orm-strategy decisions. crypto.randomUUID() is available in all target evergreen browsers (constraint-nfr-006) and requires zero dependencies. Auto-incrementing IDs from IndexedDB would create conflicts if data is ever exported/imported or synced.
forbids: Auto-incrementing integer IDs
Timestamp-based IDs (Date.now())
Third-party UUID libraries (use native crypto.randomUUID())

stackConfig.frontend: React 18 with TypeScript, Vite build tool, React Router for navigation
stackConfig.backend: None — client-side only application (no server, no API)
stackConfig.database: IndexedDB via Dexie.js (client-side), Zustand in-memory cache with optimistic updates
stackConfig.styling: Tailwind CSS v3 + CVA (Class Variance Authority) + tailwind-merge
stackConfig.componentLibrary: shadcn/ui (Radix UI primitives + Tailwind), Recharts for data visualization

additions.description: Returns the budget summary card data for a given month (feat-001). Query param: month (YYYY-MM, required). Response: { month: string, totalSpent: number, budgetLimit: number, remainingAmount: number, status: 'ON_TRACK'|'HEADS_UP'|'OVER_BUDGET', spendPercentage: number, currency: string, transactionCount: number }. 400 if month format invalid. Maps to MonthSummary + Budget entities.

Returns per-category spending breakdown for a given month (feat-002, feat-009). Query param: month (YYYY-MM, required). Response: { month: string, items: Array<{ categoryId: string, categoryName: string, color: string, icon: string, totalAmount: number, percentage: number, transactionCount: number, budgetLimit?: number }> }. Maps to CategorySpending joined with Category.

Returns a paginated, filterable list of expenses (feat-003, feat-010, feat-024). Query params: month (YYYY-MM, optional), categoryId (uuid, optional), search (string, optional — merchant/note/amount), sortBy ('date'|'amount', default 'date'), sortOrder ('asc'|'desc', default 'desc'), limit (integer, default 20), offset (integer, default 0). Response: { total: number, items: Array<{ id: string, amount: number, description: string, categoryId: string, categoryName: string, categoryColor: string, categoryIcon: string, date: string, paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string, createdAt: string, updatedAt: string }> }. 400 on invalid params.

Returns a single expense by ID (feat-004). Response: { id: string, amount: number, description: string, categoryId: string, categoryName: string, categoryColor: string, categoryIcon: string, date: string, paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string, createdAt: string, updatedAt: string }. 404 if not found.

Creates a new expense (feat-005). Request body: { id: string (client-generated uuid), amount: number (>0), description: string (max 255), categoryId: string (uuid), date: string (ISO date, no future dates), paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string (max 100) }. Response 201: { id: string, amount: number, description: string, categoryId: string, date: string, paymentMethod: string, note?: string, createdAt: string, updatedAt: string }. 400 on validation failure (future date, amount <= 0, missing required fields). Triggers recomputation of MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion.

Updates an existing expense (feat-005 edit flow). Request body: { amount?: number (>0), description?: string (max 255), categoryId?: string (uuid), date?: string (ISO date, no future dates), paymentMethod?: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string (max 100) }. Response 200: updated Expense object with all fields. 400 on validation failure. 404 if not found. Sets updatedAt to now.

Deletes an expense by ID (feat-004 delete with confirmation). Response 200: { success: true, deletedId: string }. 404 if not found. Triggers recomputation of MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending.

Returns top 3-4 quick-add suggestion chips ranked by frequency then lastUsedAt (feat-006). Query param: limit (integer, default 4, max 4). Response: { items: Array<{ id: string, description: string, amount: number, categoryId: string, categoryName: string, categoryColor: string, categoryIcon: string, paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', frequency: number, lastUsedAt: string }> }. Returns empty array when no frequent expenses exist (feat-017 empty state).

Returns the period summary stats card (feat-007). Query params: month (YYYY-MM, required). Response: { month: string, totalSpent: number, dailyAverage: number, transactionCount: number, trends: { totalSpentChange: number, totalSpentChangePercent: number, dailyAverageChange: number, dailyAverageChangePercent: number, transactionCountChange: number, transactionCountChangePercent: number }, currency: string }. Trend values compare to previous month. 400 on invalid month.

Returns daily spending data for the bar/line chart (feat-008). Query param: month (YYYY-MM, required). Response: { month: string, dailyAverage: number, items: Array<{ date: string, totalAmount: number, transactionCount: number, isAboveDoubleAverage: boolean }> }. isAboveDoubleAverage drives red bar color. Returns empty items array for months with no data (feat-017). 400 on invalid month.

Returns the top 5 biggest individual expenses for a month (feat-010). Query param: month (YYYY-MM, required). Response: { month: string, items: Array<{ rank: number, id: string, description: string, categoryId: string, categoryName: string, categoryColor: string, date: string, amount: number }> }. Sorted by amount descending, max 5 items. 400 on invalid month.

Returns side-by-side month comparison data (feat-011). Query param: month (YYYY-MM, required — treated as 'current' month). Response: { currentMonth: { month: string, totalSpent: number }, previousMonth: { month: string, totalSpent: number }, difference: number, differenceDirection: 'less'|'more'|'same', currency: string }. 400 on invalid month.

Returns all categories sorted by sortOrder (feat-002, feat-003, feat-005, feat-014, feat-015). Response: { items: Array<{ id: string, name: string, color: string, icon: string, isDefault: boolean, sortOrder: number, createdAt: string, budgetLimit?: number }> }.

Creates a new user-defined category (feat-015 inline category creation). Request body: { id: string (client-generated uuid), name: string (max 50, unique), color: string (hex, one of 6 palette values), icon: string (max 50) }. Response 201: { id: string, name: string, color: string, icon: string, isDefault: false, sortOrder: number, createdAt: string }. 400 on validation failure or duplicate name.

Updates a category (feat-014 category manager). Request body: { name?: string (max 50, unique), color?: string (hex), icon?: string (max 50), sortOrder?: number, budgetLimit?: number (feat-025) }. Response 200: updated Category object. 400 on validation failure. 404 if not found. 403 if attempting to modify isDefault field.

Deletes a user-created category (feat-014 trash icon). Response 200: { success: true, deletedId: string }. 403 if category isDefault=true (system categories cannot be deleted). 404 if not found. 400 if category has associated expenses (must reassign first).
Returns the singleton UserSettings record (feat-014). Response: { id: string, currency: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY', defaultMonthlyBudget: number, updatedAt: string }.

Updates UserSettings (feat-014 currency selector + budget input, feat-018 triggers currency toast). Request body: { currency?: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY', defaultMonthlyBudget?: number (>=0) }. Response 200: { id: string, currency: string, defaultMonthlyBudget: number, updatedAt: string, currencyChanged: boolean }. currencyChanged flag drives the toast notification (feat-018). 400 on invalid currency code or negative budget.

Returns the Budget record for a specific month (feat-001, feat-014). Path param: month (YYYY-MM). Response: { id: string, month: string, monthlyLimit: number, currency: string, createdAt: string, updatedAt: string }. 404 if no budget set for that month (caller should fall back to UserSettings.defaultMonthlyBudget).

Creates or updates the Budget for a specific month (upsert) (feat-014 monthly budget input). Path param: month (YYYY-MM). Request body: { monthlyLimit: number (>=0), currency: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY' }. Response 200: { id: string, month: string, monthlyLimit: number, currency: string, createdAt: string, updatedAt: string }. 400 on invalid month or negative limit.

Exports expense data as CSV for the selected month or all months (feat-016). Query params: month (YYYY-MM, optional — omit for all-time export), format ('csv', required). Response: CSV file download with Content-Type: text/csv, Content-Disposition: attachment; filename='cashpulse-export-{month}.csv'. Columns: date, description, category, amount, paymentMethod, note. 400 on invalid params.

Returns all recurring expense templates (feat-023). Response: { items: Array<{ id: string, description: string, amount: number, categoryId: string, categoryName: string, categoryColor: string, paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string, recurrencePeriod: 'MONTHLY'|'WEEKLY'|'YEARLY', isActive: boolean, createdAt: string, updatedAt: string }> }.

Creates a new recurring expense template (feat-023). Request body: { id: string (client-generated uuid), description: string (max 255), amount: number (>0), categoryId: string (uuid), paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string (max 100), recurrencePeriod: 'MONTHLY'|'WEEKLY'|'YEARLY', isActive: boolean }. Response 201: full RecurringTemplate object. 400 on validation failure.

Updates a recurring expense template (feat-023). Request body: { description?: string, amount?: number (>0), categoryId?: string, paymentMethod?: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string (max 100), recurrencePeriod?: 'MONTHLY'|'WEEKLY'|'YEARLY', isActive?: boolean }. Response 200: updated RecurringTemplate object. 404 if not found. 400 on validation failure.
Deletes a recurring expense template (feat-023). Response 200: { success: true, deletedId: string }. 404 if not found.

Instantiates a recurring template as a new Expense for a given date (feat-023 quick-add). Request body: { date: string (ISO date, no future dates), amount?: number (override default amount) }. Response 201: created Expense object (same shape as POST /api/expenses response). 404 if template not found. 400 if date is in the future or template isActive=false.

structured.themeStrategy: Single light-mode theme only. All tokens are defined as static extensions in tailwind.config.js under the `theme.extend` key. No dark-mode variant is required for this greenfield client-side app. Token values are consumed exclusively via Tailwind utility classes; arbitrary value syntax is prohibited. CVA variant maps reference these token-backed class names directly. The `motion-reduce:` variant must wrap every animation/transition utility. Token categories: color (neutral, primary, semantic, budget, scrim, chart), space (numeric scale), text (font-size scale), font (weight scale), border-radius (named card/button/badge/input), shadow (card/elevated), layout (per-screen structural constants), animation (duration/delay constants as CSS custom properties surfaced through tailwind.config.js), and component-specific constants (chart colors, calendar highlights, input thresholds, ring widths, nav dimensions).

tasks.title: Scaffold project structure, Dexie DB, Zustand store, and design tokens
tasks.description: Initialize the Vite + React + TypeScript project with Tailwind CSS v3, CVA, tailwind-merge, shadcn/ui, Recharts, Dexie.js, and Zustand. Configure tailwind.config.js with all design tokens (colors, spacing, radii, shadows, animations). Create the Dexie database schema (db.ts) with versioned migrations for all entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, QuickAddSuggestion, RecurringTemplate). Set up Zod validation schemas for each entity. Create the Zustand store with slices pattern (expenses, categories, budget, settings, monthSummary, categorySpending, dailySpending, suggestions). Implement the cn() utility helper and base CVA component variants (Button, Card, Badge, Input, Chip). Set up React Router with routes for /, /add-expense, /insights. Seed default categories.

tasks.title: Implement Dexie repository modules for all entities
tasks.description: Create typed repository modules for Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, and QuickAddSuggestion entities. Each repository exports async CRUD methods and query helpers (e.g., getExpensesByMonth, getCategorySpending, getDailySpending, getTopExpenses, getMonthComparison). Implement derived data computation functions for MonthSummary, CategorySpending, and DailySpending. Implement QuickAddSuggestion frequency tracking logic. Wire repositories to Zustand store hydration and optimistic update patterns.

tasks.title: Build TopNavigationBar with NavTabs, MonthNavigator, and SettingsTrigger
tasks.description: Implement the 64px TopNavigationBar component with CashPulse logo (pulse icon + text, links to /), three NavTabs (Dashboard, Add Expense, Insights) with sliding underline indicator using React Router NavLink, MonthNavigator pill with left/right chevrons for month navigation (stores selected month in Zustand), and SettingsTrigger gear icon button. Ensure full keyboard navigation, focus rings, and responsive behavior. Month changes update the global Zustand month state.

tasks.title: Build Dashboard page with BudgetSummaryCard and CategoryDonutChart
tasks.description: Implement the DashboardPage with two-column DashboardLayout. Build the BudgetSummaryCard showing total spent vs monthly budget with AnimatedAmountDisplay (counter animation on month change), BudgetProgressBar (blue <80%, amber 80-100%, red >100%), StatusBadge (On Track / Heads Up / Over Budget), and remaining amount. Build the CategoryDonutChartCard with a 160px Recharts PieChart showing category spending distribution, center total transaction count, CategoryLegend with colored dots, and hover tooltips. Handle empty states (gray ring with 0 transactions, $0.00 budget). Implement responsive breakpoints.

tasks.title: Build RecentExpensesList with CategoryFilter and ExpenseDetailPopover
tasks.description: Implement the RecentExpensesListCard in the dashboard right column with scrollable expense rows. Each ExpenseRow shows category-colored left border, CategoryIconCircle, merchant/description, category label, date, amount (prefixed with –), and payment method. Build CategoryFilterDropdown using Radix Select to filter by category. Build ExpenseDetailPopover (320px, Radix Popover) anchored to clicked rows showing amount, category with colored dot, date, payment method, note, Edit button (navigates to /add-expense?edit=id), and Delete button with inline confirmation ('Confirm delete?' with 3-second auto-revert). Handle empty state with receipt icon and Add Expense CTA.

tasks.title: Build Add/Edit Expense form with all inputs and validation
tasks.description: Implement the AddExpensePage with a single-column form (max-width 520px). Build HeroAmountInput (32px, auto-focused, numeric-only with decimal auto-formatting), CategorySelectorGrid (3x2 chips with colored selection states), DatePickerInput with mini CalendarDropdown (no future dates, today highlighted), PaymentMethodSelector (Cash, Debit Card, Credit Card, UPI/Other chips), NoteInput (100 char max with counter at 80+), and full-width SaveButton. Implement disabled state when amount is $0 or no category selected. On save, validate with Zod, persist via Zustand optimistic update + Dexie, show '✓ Saved!' with emerald background, then navigate to dashboard. Support edit mode via query param (pre-fill form from existing expense).

tasks.title: Build QuickEntrySuggestions and InlineCategoryCreation
tasks.description: Implement the QuickEntrySuggestions horizontal scrollable row below the save button on AddExpensePage, showing 3-4 suggestion chips based on frequent past expenses (e.g., '☕ Starbucks $4.50'). Clicking a chip auto-fills the entire form with cascade animation (50ms per field). Hidden entirely when no frequent expenses exist. Build InlineCategoryCreation: '+ New Category' link below category grid that opens an inline row with text input, 6-color picker circles, and Save mini-button. Saving adds the new category chip and auto-selects it.

tasks.title: Build Insights page with PeriodSummary and DailySpendingChart
tasks.description: Implement the SpendingInsightsPage with full-width layout. Build PeriodSummaryCard with three StatCells (Total Spent, Daily Average, Transactions) separated by vertical dividers, each showing value, label, and trend indicator (up/down arrow with percentage vs last month, green for decrease, red for increase). Build DailySpendingChartCard with Bar/Line toggle using ChartModeToggle. Bar chart shows vertical bars per day (blue-600, red-500 for 2x+ average). Line chart shows smooth curve with filled area. Both support hover tooltips (date, amount, transaction count). Dashed amber average line. Charts animate on first render with staggered entry, respecting prefers-reduced-motion. Handle empty state.

tasks.title: Build TopCategoriesBreakdown, BiggestExpenses, and MonthComparison cards
tasks.description: Build TopCategoriesBreakdownCard with horizontal StackedBar showing proportional category segments (lift on hover with tooltip), followed by sorted CategoryProgressList with colored dot, name, individual progress bar, amount, and percentage. Build BiggestExpensesCard listing top 5 expenses sorted by amount with rank circle (#1 highlighted blue-600), merchant name, category, date, and amount. Build MonthComparisonCard with side-by-side vertical ComparisonBars (grow from bottom on load), and ComparisonBadge showing difference ('You spent $142 less' in emerald or 'more' in red). Build ExportCsvButton with '✓ Exported' feedback.

tasks.title: Build SettingsDialog with currency, budget, category manager, and toast
tasks.description: Implement the SettingsDialog as a centered Radix Dialog modal (max-width 420px) with dark scrim overlay. Build CurrencySelector dropdown (USD, EUR, GBP, INR, JPY), BudgetLimitInput with currency prefix (default $2,000), CategoryManager with colored dot list, trash icons (disabled for default categories), and '+ Add category' link. Dismissible via close button, scrim click, or Escape. Build CurrencyChangeToast using Radix Toast (bottom-center, auto-dismiss 2 seconds) triggered when currency changes. Persist settings via Zustand optimistic update + Dexie. Currency change updates all displayed amounts with appropriate symbol.

tasks.title: Build expense search and recurring expense templates
tasks.description: Implement search functionality: add a search input to the expenses list that filters by merchant name, note, or amount across all months using client-side Zustand store filtering. Implement RecurringTemplate management: list, create, update, delete, and apply templates as new expenses. Add recurring templates UI section accessible from the Add Expense page or Settings. These are could-have features with simpler UI treatment.

tasks.title: Implement responsive breakpoints, accessibility polish, and reduced motion
tasks.description: Apply responsive layout breakpoints across all pages: 768-1024px stacks dashboard to single column with reduced chart heights; 1024-1440px uses standard two-column; >1440px maximizes whitespace. Audit and add ARIA roles/labels on all charts (role='img' with aria-label), interactive elements, and dynamic content. Ensure all focus rings are 2px blue-600 with 2px offset. Verify minimum 36x36px click targets. Apply motion-reduce: variants to all animations (chart entry, counter, cascade, slide transitions). Implement programmatic reduced motion check for Recharts animation prop. Ensure color is never the sole indicator for any status.

tasks.title: Integration testing for core user flows
tasks.description: Write integration tests covering the core user flows: (1) Add a new expense via the form, verify it appears in the dashboard expenses list and updates budget summary. (2) Edit an expense via the popover Edit button, verify changes persist. (3) Delete an expense with inline confirmation, verify removal. (4) Change month via MonthNavigator, verify dashboard data updates. (5) Change currency in Settings, verify toast and amount formatting. (6) Export CSV from Insights. (7) Quick entry suggestion auto-fill. (8) Inline category creation. Test Zustand store hydration from Dexie on app reload.

tasks.title: Scaffold Vite + React + TypeScript project with Tailwind, shadcn/ui, and core config
tasks.description: Initialize the Vite project with React 18 and TypeScript. Configure Tailwind CSS v3 with all design tokens (colors, spacing, radii, shadows, animations) from the design system diff. Set up CVA, tailwind-merge cn() helper, shadcn/ui primitives, React Router, Zustand, Dexie.js, Recharts, and Zod dependencies. Create the base folder structure (components, lib, stores, repositories, schemas, pages).

tasks.title: Dexie database, Zod schemas, repository modules, and Zustand store slices
tasks.description: Create the Dexie.js database definition (db.ts) with versioned schema for all entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, QuickAddSuggestion, RecurringTemplate). Implement Zod validation schemas for each entity. Build typed repository modules (ExpenseRepository, CategoryRepository, BudgetRepository, SettingsRepository, SuggestionRepository). Create Zustand store with slices pattern: expenses, categories, budget, settings, ui (selectedMonth). Implement hydration from Dexie on init and optimistic update pattern. Seed default categories and UserSettings on first run.

tasks.title: Zustand derived selectors for MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, and suggestions
tasks.description: Implement Zustand selectors that compute MonthSummary (totalSpent, budgetLimit, remainingAmount, budgetStatus, spendPercentage), CategorySpending (per-category totals, percentages), DailySpending (per-day totals), top expenses, month comparison, and QuickAddSuggestion ranking from raw expense/category/budget data. Use useShallow to prevent unnecessary re-renders. These selectors replace server-side aggregation endpoints since this is a client-only app.

tasks.title: Shared UI primitives: AnimatedAmountDisplay, StatusBadge, EmptyState, CategoryIconCircle, ChartTooltip
tasks.description: Build reusable shared components used across multiple screens. AnimatedAmountDisplay with counter animation and motion-reduce support. StatusBadge with CVA variants for On Track/Heads Up/Over Budget. EmptyState component with icon, message, and optional CTA. CategoryIconCircle with colored background. ChartTooltip for Recharts. Currency formatting utility respecting UserSettings currency. All components use Tailwind tokens only with CVA variants and proper focus rings/ARIA attributes.

tasks.title: TopNavigationBar with NavTabs, MonthNavigator, and SettingsTrigger
tasks.description: Implement the 64px top navigation bar with CashPulse logo (pulse icon + text, links to /), three navigation tabs (Dashboard, Add Expense, Insights) with sliding underline indicator using React Router NavLink, MonthNavigator pill with left/right chevrons that updates the Zustand ui.selectedMonth, and settings gear icon button. Fully keyboard navigable with proper ARIA roles. Responsive behavior for tablet breakpoint.

tasks.title: Dashboard page: BudgetSummaryCard and CategoryDonutChartCard (left column)
tasks.description: Build the Dashboard page layout with two-column responsive grid. Implement BudgetSummaryCard showing total spent vs monthly budget with AnimatedAmountDisplay, BudgetProgressBar (blue <80%, amber 80-100%, red >100%), StatusBadge, and remaining amount. Implement CategoryDonutChartCard with 160px Recharts PieChart, center transaction count, hover-expand segments with tooltip, and CategoryLegend with colored dots, names, and amounts. Handle empty states (no expenses: gray donut ring with 0 transactions, $0 budget card). Counter animations on month change with motion-reduce support.

tasks.title: Dashboard: RecentExpensesListCard with ExpenseRows, CategoryFilter, and ExpenseDetailPopover
tasks.description: Implement the right column RecentExpensesListCard with scrollable expense rows. Each ExpenseRow is a white card with category-colored left border, CategoryIconCircle, merchant name, category label, date, formatted amount (–prefix), and payment method. Add CategoryFilterDropdown using Radix Select to filter by category. Implement ExpenseDetailPopover (320px Radix Popover) anchored to clicked row showing full details, Edit button (navigates to /add-expense?edit=id), and Delete button with inline confirmation ('Confirm delete?' with 3-second auto-revert). Handle empty state when no expenses exist.

tasks.title: Add/Edit Expense form with all inputs, validation, and save flow
tasks.description: Build the AddExpensePage with single-column form (max-width 520px). Implement HeroAmountInput (32px, auto-focused, numeric-only with decimal auto-formatting), CategorySelectorGrid (3x2 chips with colored selection ring), DatePickerInput with mini calendar dropdown (Radix Popover, no future dates), PaymentMethodSelector (Cash/Debit Card/Credit Card/UPI-Other chips), NoteInput (100 char max with counter at 80+), and full-width SaveButton. Disabled state when amount is $0 or no category. On save: Zod validation, optimistic Zustand update, Dexie persist, button shows '✓ Saved!' with emerald bg then navigates to dashboard. Support edit mode via query param (pre-fill form from existing expense).

tasks.title: QuickEntrySuggestions chips and InlineCategoryCreation on Add Expense screen

Clicking a chip auto-fills the entire form with cascade animation (50ms per field). Hidden when no frequent expenses exist. Implement InlineCategoryCreation: '+ New Category' link below category grid that opens inline row with text input, 6-color picker circles, and Save mini-button. Saving creates category via optimistic update and auto-selects it in the grid.

tasks.title: Insights page: PeriodSummaryCard and DailySpendingChartCard with Bar/Line toggle

Implement PeriodSummaryCard with three StatCells (Total Spent, Daily Average, Transactions) separated by vertical dividers, each with value, label, and trend indicator (up/down arrow with percentage vs last month, green for decrease, red for increase). Implement DailySpendingChartCard with Recharts BarChart/LineChart toggle. Bar chart: blue-600 bars, red-500 for 2x+ average. Line chart: smooth curve with filled area. Both have hover tooltips (date, amount, count). Dashed amber average line. Staggered entry animation with motion-reduce support. Empty state for no data.

Implement MonthComparisonCard with side-by-side vertical bars (grow from bottom animation), difference badge ('You spent $X less/more'). Implement ExportCsvButton that generates CSV from expense data with '✓ Exported' feedback.

CategoryManager with colored dot list, trash icons (disabled for default categories), and '+ Add category' link that opens inline creation row. alternatives.name: localStorage + in-memory state (Zustand/Context)
alternatives.description: All 8 data entities are stored as serialized JSON in the browser's localStorage. A lightweight state manager (Zustand or React Context + useReducer) holds the working in-memory copy, syncing to localStorage on every mutation. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed on-the-fly via selector functions rather than stored separately. QuickAddSuggestion is built from a frequency map over Expense records.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies if using React Context; +1 dependency (zustand ~3KB gzipped) for Zustand variant
localStorage is capped at ~5MB per origin — sufficient for years of daily expense entries (~200 bytes/record × 3,650 records ≈ 730KB)
No query language: filtering/aggregation for Insights screen must be hand-written JS — adds ~200–400 LOC in selector/util layer
Data is device-local and non-portable (aligns with scope-exclusion of cloud sync)
Synchronous localStorage.setItem can block the main thread on large writes; mitigated by debouncing or using a Web Worker
Instant reads from memory satisfy the 150ms micro-interaction and 350ms screen-transition constraints with no async overhead
Affects: state store module (1 file), 8 entity schemas (1 file), selector layer (~3–5 files), 7 screen components (read hooks) — blast radius ~12–15 files

alternatives.name: IndexedDB via Dexie.js
alternatives.description: All 8 entities are persisted in a structured IndexedDB database using Dexie.js as the ergonomic wrapper. Each entity maps to a Dexie table with typed indexes (e.g., Expense indexed on date, categoryId; Budget indexed on categoryId+month). Derived summary entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via Dexie's Collection API and cached in React state after each mutation. QuickAddSuggestion is a live query over Expense frequency.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: dexie (~23KB gzipped) — no other runtime deps required
IndexedDB storage quota is origin-scoped and typically 60%+ of available disk — effectively unlimited for this use case

All reads are async (Promise-based); requires careful use of React Suspense or loading states to avoid violating 150ms interaction budget — async overhead is ~1–5ms for indexed lookups, well within budget
Dexie's query API (where, filter, sortBy) eliminates hand-written aggregation logic, reducing selector LOC by ~50% vs. alt-1
Schema migrations are versioned via Dexie's upgrade() API — safe for future entity additions
Requires a db.ts schema file + migration version, 8 table definitions, and async data-access hooks per entity — ~18–22 files affected
Slightly higher initial complexity than localStorage but significantly better scalability and queryability for the Insights screen charts

alternatives.name: SQLite via sql.js (WASM) + localStorage persistence
alternatives.description: A full SQLite database runs in-browser via sql.js (compiled to WebAssembly). The binary database blob is serialized and saved to localStorage (or IndexedDB) on each write. All 8 entities are proper relational tables with foreign keys and SQL aggregation queries powering the Insights screen (GROUP BY category, SUM amounts, etc.). A thin repository layer wraps sql.js calls.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 large dependency: sql.js WASM bundle is ~1.3MB uncompressed (~400KB gzipped) — significantly increases initial load time, potentially violating the 350ms screen-transition feel on first load
WASM initialization is async and takes 200–800ms on first load — requires a loading splash or skeleton screen before any data is accessible
Full SQL expressiveness: complex Insights queries (rolling 30-day averages, category rank, daily spend curves) are trivial to write and maintain
Database must be fully loaded into memory on each page load from the serialized blob — adds ~10–50ms overhead per session start
Overkill for 8 entities and a single-user desktop app with no concurrent writes
Affects: db initialization module, 8 table DDL files, repository layer (~8 files), React data hooks (~8 files), build config (WASM asset handling) — blast radius ~25–30 files
No backend required — fully client-side, consistent with scope exclusions

alternatives.name: Supabase (PostgreSQL) — client-only, anonymous/local mode
alternatives.description: Data is persisted in a hosted Supabase (PostgreSQL) project using the Supabase JS client. All 8 entities map to Postgres tables with RLS disabled (single-user, no auth per scope exclusions). Supabase's auto-generated REST and realtime APIs are used for CRUD. Derived summary entities are computed via Postgres views or RPC functions.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: @supabase/supabase-js (~40KB gzipped) plus a hosted Supabase project (free tier: 500MB DB, 2GB bandwidth)

Requires network round-trips for every read/write — baseline latency of 50–200ms per operation depending on region, which risks violating the 150ms micro-interaction constraint for chip selections and form submissions without optimistic UI
Optimistic UI updates (update local state immediately, sync to Supabase in background) can recover the latency issue but adds ~300–500 LOC of conflict-resolution logic

Scope exclusion explicitly rules out cloud storage and backend API development — this alternative directly conflicts with 'Out of scope: Data backup, sync, or cloud storage' and 'Out of scope: Backend API development'
Provides full SQL power, realtime subscriptions, and future-proofing for multi-user (also out of scope)
Data loss risk if Supabase project is paused (free tier pauses after 1 week of inactivity) — poor UX for a daily-use app
Affects: supabase client config, 8 table migrations, repository layer, all 7 screen data hooks, environment variable setup — blast radius ~25–30 files

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency in-memory/localStorage to a hosted cloud database, covering every realistic option for a greenfield, desktop-only, single-user, no-auth personal finance app. Options requiring a native runtime (SQLite binary, Node.js server) were excluded because the project is a browser-only web app with no backend scope. Firebase/Firestore was excluded for the same reason as Supabase — it conflicts with the cloud-storage scope exclusion and introduces auth dependencies. PouchDB was excluded because its CouchDB sync model is unnecessary overhead for a single-user app and it duplicates IndexedDB's capabilities with more complexity. The four selected alternatives give the Architecture Writer a clear gradient: minimal-change localStorage (alt-1), the recommended structured browser DB (alt-2), a power-user SQL option with real cost (alt-3), and the explicitly out-of-scope cloud option (alt-4) included for completeness and to document why it was rejected.
structured.recommendation: alt-2 — IndexedDB via Dexie.js. It is the sweet spot for CashPulse: structured, queryable, async-safe, and capable of powering the Insights screen's aggregation queries without hand-rolling JS reducers. The single Dexie.js dependency is small and well-maintained. Async reads are fast enough (1–5ms for indexed lookups) to comfortably satisfy all performance constraints. It scales to years of expense data without the 5MB localStorage ceiling, and its versioned migration API protects against future schema changes. Alt-1 (localStorage) is acceptable if the team wants zero dependencies, but the hand-written aggregation burden for the Insights screen makes it a false economy. Alt-3 and Alt-4 are over-engineered for this scope.

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS v3 (Utility-First)
alternatives.description: Use Tailwind CSS as the sole styling layer. Design tokens (spacing, radii, shadows, colors) are codified in tailwind.config.js under a custom theme extension. All components — cards, buttons, inputs, badges, chips — are composed from utility classes directly in JSX. Responsive breakpoints (768px, 1024px, 1440px) map to Tailwind's `md`, `lg`, and `xl` prefixes. Focus rings are enforced via `focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-blue-600 focus-visible:ring-offset-2`. `prefers-reduced-motion` is handled via the `motion-safe:` and `motion-reduce:` variants. Inter font is loaded via @font-face or Google Fonts and set as `fontFamily.sans` in config.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency (tailwindcss + postcss + autoprefixer — typically installed together as a unit)

Requires tailwind.config.js to encode all 8 design tokens (4px base unit, 16px card radius, 10px button radius, 20px badge radius, 8px input radius, card shadow, elevated shadow, blue-600 accent) — one-time setup cost of ~1–2 hours
JSX markup becomes verbose for complex components (e.g., the Add Expense form with 6+ fields); mitigated by extracting reusable component wrappers
PurgeCSS/content scanning must be configured correctly or unused styles bloat the bundle — misconfiguration risk in greenfield
No runtime style computation: dynamic styles (e.g., category color chips with per-category hues) require inline styles or CSS variables alongside Tailwind, creating a mixed pattern
Enforces strict design token discipline — deviating from the token set requires config changes, not ad-hoc values, which is a feature but adds friction for one-off overrides
Blast radius: affects all 7 screen files + ~20 shared component files (cards, buttons, inputs, badges, chips, popovers, modals) = ~27 files

alternatives.name: CSS Modules (Minimal-Change Baseline)
alternatives.description: Use CSS Modules with locally-scoped class names, co-located with each component file (e.g., `Card.module.css` next to `Card.tsx`). A single `tokens.css` file at the project root defines all design tokens as CSS custom properties (--spacing-base: 4px, --radius-card: 16px, --radius-button: 10px, --color-primary: #2563EB, etc.). All components import from `tokens.css` via `:root` variables. Responsive breakpoints are written as standard `@media` queries inside each module. `prefers-reduced-motion` is a standard `@media` block. Focus rings are a single shared `.focusRing` utility class imported where needed. This approach requires zero new runtime dependencies if the project uses Vite or Create React App (both support CSS Modules natively).
tradeoffs: Zero new runtime dependencies — CSS Modules are supported natively by Vite, webpack, and Next.js
Lowest blast radius of all options: each component owns its styles; changes are isolated to that component's .module.css file
Requires discipline to keep tokens.css as the single source of truth — without tooling enforcement, token drift is possible across 27+ module files
More verbose for responsive variants: each breakpoint must be repeated per-component rather than via a shared prefix system
Dynamic per-category color chips require inline style={{ backgroundColor: category.color }} — no abstraction layer, but straightforward
No design-system-level co-location of variants (e.g., button sizes, card elevations) — variant logic lives in JS conditionals mapping to multiple CSS class names
Slightly more boilerplate per component (~5–10 extra lines of CSS import + className logic) compared to Tailwind
Blast radius: only tokens.css (1 file) is global; all other changes are isolated per component = ~27 component-scoped files, each independently changeable

alternatives.name: styled-components v6 (CSS-in-JS)
alternatives.description: Use styled-components to define all UI primitives as typed, themeable React components. A single `<ThemeProvider>` at the app root injects the full design token set (spacing, radii, shadows, colors, typography) as a typed theme object. Component variants (e.g., Button primary/secondary, Card default/elevated) are expressed as props-driven interpolations. The `createGlobalStyle` helper sets Inter font, CSS resets, and focus ring defaults globally. `prefers-reduced-motion` is handled via a `useReducedMotion` hook that conditionally passes animation props. Dynamic category colors (per-entity hue) are first-class: `background: ${props => props.categoryColor}` with no mixed-pattern workaround needed.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 runtime dependency (styled-components ~12KB gzip) that ships in the JS bundle — increases initial JS parse time vs. zero-runtime alternatives
Requires wrapping the entire app in ThemeProvider — touches the root App.tsx/main.tsx file and all 7 screen entry points if screens are tested in isolation

CSS-in-JS generates class names at runtime; for micro-interactions (hover, chip selection within 150ms), the runtime overhead is negligible on modern hardware but adds ~1–3ms per component mount in benchmarks
Server-side rendering (if ever adopted) requires ServerStyleSheet setup — not a current concern (greenfield, desktop-only) but a future migration cost
Excellent TypeScript integration: theme type can be declared once and all styled components get autocomplete for token values — reduces token drift risk
Dynamic per-category color chips are natively supported without inline style workarounds
Babel plugin (babel-plugin-styled-components) recommended for readable class names and SSR support — adds 1 dev dependency and babel config change
Blast radius: ThemeProvider touches root (1 file) + all 7 screens + ~20 shared components = ~28 files; however, each component's styles are fully self-contained

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS + Class Variance Authority (CVA)
alternatives.description: Extend the Tailwind-only approach (alt-tailwind) with Class Variance Authority (CVA) to manage component variant logic. CVA provides a typed, declarative API for defining component variants (e.g., Button: size × intent, Badge: status × size) that maps to Tailwind class strings. This eliminates the verbose conditional className logic that Tailwind alone requires for multi-variant components. The design token config lives in tailwind.config.js as before. CVA adds a thin abstraction layer (~2KB) with zero runtime style computation. This is the most structured option for a design system with strict token adherence and multiple variant axes (the CashPulse system has at least 5 distinct interactive component types with variants).
tradeoffs: Adds 2 dependencies: tailwindcss (+ postcss/autoprefixer) and cva (~2KB) — both are zero-runtime in production output
CVA requires defining variant schemas upfront for each component — ~30 minutes of additional setup per complex component (Button, Badge, Input, Chip, Card) but pays off immediately in type safety
Tailwind's dynamic class limitation still applies: category color chips with arbitrary per-entity hex values still require inline styles or CSS variables — same workaround as alt-tailwind
Best-in-class TypeScript DX: variant props are fully typed and autocompleted, reducing design token drift across 7 screens and 20+ components
Slightly steeper learning curve than plain Tailwind for developers unfamiliar with CVA's variant/compoundVariants API
Tailwind's JIT engine ensures only used class combinations are in the final CSS bundle — no bloat from unused variant combinations
Blast radius: same as alt-tailwind (~27 files) plus cva() wrapper definitions added to each component file — net ~27 files modified, no new file types introduced

structured.rationale:
Four alternatives were selected to cover the full spectrum from zero-dependency to fully-featured CSS-in-JS, with two Tailwind variants to address the token-enforcement vs. variant-management tradeoff that is particularly relevant for CashPulse's strict design system. 

**Excluded approaches:**
- **Sass/SCSS modules**: Offers no meaningful advantage over CSS Modules + custom properties for this project; custom properties already provide the token system Sass variables would give, and Sass adds a build step without adding capability relevant to the constraints.
- **Emotion**: Functionally similar to styled-components but with a smaller ecosystem and less community momentum; styled-components covers the CSS-in-JS case more completely.
- **Vanilla Extract / Linaria (zero-runtime CSS-in-JS)**: Valid options but introduce significant build pipeline complexity (Vite plugin configuration, type generation) that is disproportionate for a greenfield single-developer project of this scope (7 screens, 25 features).
- **UnoCSS**: Atomic CSS alternative to Tailwind, but less mature tooling and fewer resources for a financial app design system.

**Why these four cover the decision space:** CSS Modules (alt-css-modules) is the minimal-change baseline with zero new dependencies. Tailwind (alt-tailwind) is the most popular utility-first choice with strong token enforcement. Tailwind+CVA (alt-tailwind-cva) addresses the variant-management gap that plain Tailwind has for a multi-variant design system. styled-components (alt-styled-components) covers the CSS-in-JS case where dynamic per-category colors and theme propagation are first-class concerns.
structured.recommendation:
**Recommended: alt-tailwind-cva (Tailwind CSS + CVA)**

CashPulse has a strict, well-defined design token set (8 tokens, 5+ component variant axes) and a greenfield codebase — the ideal conditions for Tailwind + CVA. The reasons:

1. **Token enforcement is structural, not cultural**: tailwind.config.js makes it impossible to use off-token values without an explicit config change. For a financial app where visual consistency signals trustworthiness, this matters.
2. **WCAG 2.1 AA focus rings are trivial**: `focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-blue-600 focus-visible:ring-offset-2` is a single reusable Tailwind pattern applied consistently.
3. **prefers-reduced-motion is first-class**: Tailwind's `motion-reduce:` variant handles the animation constraint declaratively without a custom hook.
4. **CVA resolves Tailwind's main weakness**: The 5+ component types with variants (Button, Badge, Chip, Input, Card) benefit enormously from CVA's typed variant schemas — this directly reduces the risk of inconsistent states across 7 screens.
5. **Zero runtime overhead**: Both Tailwind and CVA produce static CSS/class strings — no JS bundle cost for styling, keeping micro-interaction performance well within the 150ms constraint.
6. **The one workaround** (per-category dynamic colors via inline styles or CSS variables) is a known, contained pattern affecting only the Category chip component — not a systemic issue.

alternatives.name: shadcn/ui (Radix UI primitives + Tailwind CSS)
alternatives.description: shadcn/ui is a collection of copy-paste components built on top of Radix UI accessibility primitives and styled with Tailwind CSS utility classes. Components are vendored directly into the project (no runtime library dependency), giving full control over markup, tokens, and styling. Radix primitives handle all keyboard navigation, focus management, and ARIA roles out of the box. Custom design tokens (Inter, 4px grid, border-radius scale, shadow values) are configured once in tailwind.config and propagate everywhere. Charts would be handled by a separate library (e.g., Recharts or Visx) since shadcn/ui does not include chart primitives beyond a thin Recharts wrapper.
tradeoffs: PROS: Zero runtime bundle overhead — components are local source files, tree-shaken naturally
PROS: Radix primitives give WCAG 2.1 AA keyboard nav, focus management, and ARIA for free (Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip, etc.)
PROS: Tailwind utility classes make the 4px spacing grid, 16px/10px/20px/8px radius scale, and shadow tokens trivially enforceable via tailwind.config theme extension
PROS: prefers-reduced-motion handled via Tailwind's motion-reduce: variant — no custom CSS needed
PROS: No vendor lock-in; components are owned code and can be modified without forking a library
CONS: Adds 2 direct dependencies (Radix UI umbrella + Tailwind CSS) plus 1 dev dependency (tailwindcss PostCSS plugin)
CONS: Requires a separate charting library (Recharts adds ~180KB gzipped); chart ARIA wrappers must be hand-authored
CONS: No built-in data grid or complex table component — expense list table must be built from scratch
CONS: Initial setup requires configuring Tailwind theme tokens, which takes ~2–4 hours but is a one-time cost
CONS: Component updates are manual (re-run shadcn CLI or copy-paste) — no npm upgrade path

alternatives.name: Mantine v7 (full-featured component library)
alternatives.description: Mantine is a fully-featured React component library with 100+ components, built-in form management (@mantine/form), date pickers, number inputs with formatting, notifications, and a charting package (@mantine/charts, built on Recharts). It ships its own CSS-in-JS-free styling system using CSS Modules and CSS custom properties. All components are WCAG-compliant with keyboard navigation. The design token system (colors, spacing, radius, shadows, fonts) is configured via MantineProvider theme object, making it straightforward to enforce CashPulse's Inter font, 4px grid, and radius scale. The NumberInput component directly satisfies the amount field's numeric-only, decimal-formatting, and max-value constraints.
tradeoffs: PROS: NumberInput with precision, min/max, and formatter props directly implements the $99,999.99 amount field requirement — zero custom input logic
PROS: DatePickerInput has maxDate prop — future date prevention is a one-liner
PROS: @mantine/charts provides Bar, Line, Donut charts with accessible wrappers and animation control (withAnimation prop)
PROS: Notification system handles the 3-second auto-revert delete confirmation pattern natively
PROS: MantineProvider theme enforces Inter, 4px spacing, and all radius tokens globally — design consistency is structural
PROS: prefers-reduced-motion respected automatically by Mantine's animation system
CONS: Adds 3 dependencies (@mantine/core, @mantine/hooks, @mantine/form) plus optional @mantine/charts and @mantine/dates — total ~5 packages
CONS: Full Mantine core bundle is ~120KB gzipped before tree-shaking; with charts and dates, effective bundle grows to ~250KB
CONS: Mantine's default visual style (radius, shadow, color palette) requires theme override to match CashPulse tokens exactly — risk of visual drift if overrides are incomplete
CONS: CSS custom property approach means global styles are injected — potential specificity conflicts if any other CSS framework is used alongside
CONS: Upgrading major versions (e.g., v7→v8) has historically required significant migration effort across all component usages

alternatives.name: Material UI (MUI) v6 with custom theme
alternatives.description: MUI is the most widely adopted React component library, offering a comprehensive set of components (DataGrid, DatePicker, Charts via MUI X), an emotion-based styling engine, and a deeply configurable theme system. The MUI theme can be overridden to replace Material Design tokens with CashPulse's Inter font, blue-600 primary, 4px spacing unit, and custom border-radius values. MUI X provides a premium DataGrid for the expense list and a Charts package. All components meet WCAG 2.1 AA. However, MUI's visual identity is strongly Material Design, requiring significant theme customization to achieve CashPulse's 'sharp, structured, confident' financial aesthetic.
tradeoffs: PROS: MUI X DataGrid handles sorting, filtering, and virtualization for the expense list out of the box
PROS: MUI X DatePicker has disableFuture prop — future date prevention is trivial
PROS: Largest ecosystem, most Stack Overflow answers, most third-party integrations
PROS: Emotion-based sx prop and theme system can express all CashPulse design tokens
PROS: Full WCAG 2.1 AA compliance across all components with tested keyboard navigation
CONS: Adds 4 dependencies (@mui/material, @emotion/react, @emotion/styled, @mui/x-date-pickers) — heaviest dependency footprint of all alternatives
CONS: Bundle size is the largest: @mui/material alone is ~300KB gzipped before tree-shaking; with MUI X Charts and DataGrid, effective bundle can reach 500KB+

CONS: Material Design aesthetic requires extensive theme overrides (elevation system, ripple effects, typography scale) to match CashPulse's card-based financial design — estimated 8–12 hours of theme work
CONS: Emotion runtime adds ~7KB gzipped and introduces CSS-in-JS hydration complexity in SSR scenarios
CONS: MUI X DataGrid and Charts are partially behind a commercial license (MIT for Community, paid for Pro/Premium features like row grouping)

alternatives.name: Custom components on Radix UI primitives only (minimal change / no UI library)
alternatives.description: The minimal-change, zero-framework alternative: use only Radix UI headless primitives (Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip, Checkbox) for accessibility-critical interactive elements, and hand-author all visual components using plain CSS Modules (or a single global stylesheet). No Tailwind, no component library. Design tokens are defined as CSS custom properties in a single :root block. This approach gives maximum control over every pixel, guarantees the exact CashPulse design token system is implemented without fighting library defaults, and keeps the bundle lean. Recharts is added only for charts. This is the approach taken by teams that treat the design system as a first-class product artifact.
tradeoffs: PROS: Smallest possible bundle — Radix UI primitives are individually installed and tree-shaken; total overhead ~30–50KB gzipped
PROS: Zero visual override work — every component is built to spec from day one, no fighting library defaults
PROS: CSS custom properties for tokens are trivially auditable and enforced via design token linting (style-dictionary)
PROS: No major-version migration risk — Radix primitives are stable and individually versioned
PROS: prefers-reduced-motion handled with a single CSS @media block in the global stylesheet
CONS: Highest upfront development cost — all form inputs, cards, badges, chips, and layout components must be hand-built; estimated 3–5 additional dev-days vs. shadcn/ui
CONS: Adds 1 dependency (Radix UI per-primitive packages, e.g., @radix-ui/react-dialog, @radix-ui/react-popover) — but these are small and individually scoped
CONS: No pre-built NumberInput with formatting — amount field decimal auto-formatting must be implemented manually (or via a small utility like react-number-format, adding 1 dependency)
CONS: No pre-built DatePicker — requires either building one from scratch or pulling in a focused library (react-day-picker, ~15KB gzipped)
CONS: Accessibility correctness for custom components depends entirely on developer discipline — Radix covers interactive primitives but layout/display components have no guardrails
CONS: Slower iteration speed for a 7-screen, 25-feature greenfield app — component inventory must be built before feature work begins

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-library to full-featured, covering the realistic decision space for a greenfield desktop-first React app. MUI was included despite its weight because it is the most commonly evaluated alternative and its MUI X DataGrid/Charts are genuinely compelling for an expense tracker. Mantine was included because its NumberInput and DatePickerInput directly address two of the most constraint-heavy form fields in CashPulse (amount formatting, future-date prevention). shadcn/ui was included as the pragmatic middle ground — Radix accessibility + Tailwind token enforcement with no runtime overhead. The custom/headless option was included as the required 'minimal change' alternative (lowest blast radius, no library opinions to fight). Excluded: Chakra UI (v3 is in flux, smaller ecosystem than Mantine for equivalent feature set), Ant Design (enterprise aesthetic incompatible with CashPulse's sharp financial design language, heavy bundle), Headless UI (Tailwind Labs, less comprehensive than Radix for the required primitives — Popover, Select, Tooltip all require Radix anyway).
structured.recommendation: shadcn/ui (alt-shadcn-ui). For CashPulse's specific constraints, shadcn/ui hits the optimal intersection: Radix primitives deliver WCAG 2.1 AA keyboard navigation and focus management for free across all interactive elements (Popover for category picker, Dialog for delete confirmation, Select for filters); Tailwind's theme extension enforces the entire CashPulse token system (Inter, 4px grid, radius scale, shadow values) structurally rather than by convention; the vendored component model means zero runtime library overhead and no upgrade-migration risk. The two gaps — charting and amount formatting — are filled by Recharts (thin shadcn/ui chart wrapper already exists) and react-number-format respectively, both well-maintained and scoped. Mantine is the strong runner-up if the team wants to move faster on form primitives and is comfortable with a larger bundle and theme-override discipline.

alternatives.name: Dexie.js (IndexedDB wrapper) — Minimal Change
alternatives.description: Use Dexie.js as a lightweight, promise-based IndexedDB wrapper. Define schemas for all 8 entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion) as Dexie tables with typed indexes. Dexie provides a fluent query API, versioned migrations, and live queries via dexie-react-hooks. All data lives client-side in IndexedDB — perfectly aligned with the scope exclusion of backend API and cloud sync. No server required.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: dexie@^3.x (~47KB gzipped); optionally dexie-react-hooks for reactive queries (+8KB)
IndexedDB storage is origin-scoped and browser-managed — no cross-device sync (already out of scope)
Querying is limited to indexed fields; complex aggregations for MonthSummary/CategorySpending require manual JS computation or pre-computed derived tables
No built-in ORM-style relations — joins between Expense↔Category↔Budget must be done in application code
Versioned schema migrations are manual but straightforward for greenfield projects
Affects: db/ layer (~3 files), all 8 entity store definitions, ~5 service/hook files that query data — blast radius is contained

alternatives.name: Zustand + localStorage (In-Memory Store with Persistence)
alternatives.description: Use Zustand as the global state manager with the zustand/middleware persist plugin serializing all entity slices to localStorage (or sessionStorage). Each entity type gets a dedicated slice: expenseSlice, categorySlice, budgetSlice, settingsSlice, etc. Derived data (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed as Zustand selectors rather than stored entities. QuickAddSuggestion is derived from recent Expense history. This approach treats the data layer as pure in-memory state with transparent persistence.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: zustand@^4.x (~3KB gzipped) — likely already in the stack for UI state; persist middleware is built-in
localStorage has a hard 5–10MB quota per origin — with 5+ entity types and growing expense history, this cap can be hit within months of daily use
No indexed querying — filtering/sorting expenses by date, category, or amount requires full in-memory array scans (acceptable for <1000 records, degrades beyond)
Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) must be recomputed on every relevant state change — requires memoized selectors to meet 150ms micro-interaction budget
Serialization of large datasets on every write can cause jank; requires debounced persistence strategy
Affects: store/ directory (~8 slice files), ~12 component files consuming store hooks, 0 new infrastructure files — lowest blast radius of all options

alternatives.name: Dexie.js + Zustand Hybrid (Persistent DB + Reactive UI State)
alternatives.description: Combine Dexie.js for durable, indexed storage of write-heavy entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, QuickAddSuggestion) with Zustand for ephemeral UI state and pre-computed derived views (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending). Dexie handles persistence and querying; Zustand holds the current session's working data and derived aggregates computed on app load or on expense mutation. dexie-react-hooks' useLiveQuery bridges the two layers reactively. This is the recommended production pattern for client-only financial apps.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 dependencies: dexie@^3.x (~47KB) + dexie-react-hooks (~8KB); zustand likely already present
Two data layers increase conceptual surface area — developers must understand which entities live where
Requires a clear data-flow contract: Dexie is the source of truth, Zustand is a read cache — violating this causes stale-data bugs
Derived entity computation (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) must be triggered on Expense/Budget writes and memoized to stay within 150ms interaction budget
Migration strategy needed for Dexie schema versions as entities evolve (manageable in greenfield)
Affects: db/ layer (3–4 files), store/ layer (3–4 slice files), ~8 service/hook files, ~15 component files — medium blast radius but clean separation of concerns
Enables future features (export, offline-first) without architectural rework

alternatives.name: PGlite (in-browser SQLite via WASM) + Drizzle ORM
alternatives.description: Use PGlite (a WASM-compiled PostgreSQL running entirely in the browser) with Drizzle ORM for schema definition, type-safe queries, and migrations. All 8 entities are defined as Drizzle table schemas with proper foreign keys (Expense→Category, Expense→Budget, etc.). Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via SQL GROUP BY queries rather than JS aggregation. Data persists via PGlite's IndexedDB backend. This gives full relational SQL semantics client-side.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new dependencies: @electric-sql/pglite (~3MB WASM bundle) + drizzle-orm (~35KB) — the WASM bundle is a significant initial load cost requiring code-splitting and lazy initialization
PGlite WASM initialization takes 200–800ms on first load — must be handled with a loading state to avoid blocking the 350ms screen transition budget
Drizzle migrations must be bundled and run client-side on app init — adds complexity to the bootstrap sequence
Overkill for 8 entities and a single-user desktop app with no backend; SQL power is underutilized
Full relational integrity (FK constraints, cascading deletes) is a genuine advantage for Expense↔Category↔Budget relationships
Affects: db/ schema files (~8 table definitions), migration files, db client singleton, ~10 service files, ~15 component files — highest blast radius but most future-proof for complex queries
Bundle size increase of ~3MB WASM requires explicit lazy loading strategy to meet performance constraints

structured.rationale: All four alternatives are implementable without a backend (aligned with the hard scope exclusion of backend API development) and store data client-side. Two alternatives were excluded from consideration: (1) a traditional server-side ORM like Prisma/TypeORM — ruled out because the project is explicitly frontend-only with no backend; (2) plain fetch + REST against a mock API — ruled out because it introduces a fake dependency that would need to be replaced and adds latency that threatens the 150ms micro-interaction budget. The four selected alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-infrastructure (Zustand/localStorage) to full relational SQL (PGlite/Drizzle), giving the Architecture Writer a clear cost/benefit gradient. The Dexie+Zustand hybrid (alt-03) is recommended as it matches the entity complexity (8 types, some derived), respects the performance constraints via indexed queries and memoized selectors, and keeps the bundle lean (~55KB total additions) without the WASM overhead of PGlite.
structured.recommendation: alt-03 — Dexie.js + Zustand Hybrid. For CashPulse's 8 entities, the split is natural: Dexie owns durable, indexed, write-heavy entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, QuickAddSuggestion) while Zustand owns derived/computed views (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) that are recalculated on mutation and cached for the session. This satisfies the 150ms micro-interaction budget (indexed Dexie reads are sub-millisecond for typical expense volumes), keeps the bundle under 60KB of new JS, and avoids the localStorage quota risk of alt-02 and the WASM load penalty of alt-04. The two-layer contract is well-documented and widely used in local-first web apps.

alternatives.name: In-Memory Store with Zustand (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: Use Zustand (or React Context + useReducer) as a client-side in-memory store to cache all 8 data entities for the lifetime of the browser session. All reads are synchronous from the store; writes update the store optimistically before any persistence layer (localStorage). No network layer is involved since backend is out of scope. Data is seeded once on app load from localStorage and held in memory thereafter. Selectors are memoized with useMemo/useCallback to prevent unnecessary re-renders.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency (zustand ~3KB gzipped) or zero if using React Context
All data lives in JS heap — no persistence across hard refreshes unless paired with localStorage
Synchronous reads guarantee sub-150ms micro-interactions and sub-350ms screen transitions with zero async overhead
No cache invalidation complexity — single source of truth for the session
Memory footprint is negligible for personal expense data (hundreds of records max)
Requires manual rehydration logic on app mount (~1 file: store/index.ts)
Affects 8 entity slices in the store + ~7 screen components that consume data = ~15 files touched

alternatives.name: localStorage-Backed Cache with TTL (Structured Persistence)
alternatives.description: Implement a lightweight cache layer (hand-rolled or via idb-keyval) that persists all 8 entities to localStorage with per-key TTL metadata. On app load, the cache is read synchronously; stale entries (e.g., MonthSummary older than 1 hour) are flagged for background recomputation. Writes are synchronous to localStorage. Derived aggregates (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed and cached separately from raw Expense records to avoid re-aggregation on every render. A cache version key handles schema migrations.
tradeoffs: Zero new runtime dependencies if hand-rolled; adds 1 dependency (idb-keyval ~1KB) if using IndexedDB variant
localStorage is synchronous and limited to ~5MB — sufficient for personal expense history but requires pruning strategy for multi-year data
TTL logic adds ~150 lines of cache utility code (1 new file: lib/cache.ts)
Derived entity caching (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) eliminates recalculation on Dashboard and Insights screens, directly supporting 600ms chart render target
Schema migration logic needed when data entities evolve (1 versioning utility)
Affects: lib/cache.ts (new), store slices for all 8 entities, 3 screen components (Dashboard, Insights, AddExpense) = ~12 files
No cross-tab synchronization — two open tabs can diverge (acceptable for single-user desktop app)
