navigationTargets.trigger: expense-row: row click (opens expense detail popover)
date-picker-input: input field click (opens calendar dropdown)
export-csv-button: click triggers GET /api/export/csv download (stays on screen)
popover-actions: Delete confirmed (DELETE /api/expenses/{id} success, closes popover)

dataBindings.transform: response.date (used to pre-select date in edit mode)

navigationTargets.trigger: calendar-day-grid: day selection (closes sheet, returns selected date to Add Expense form)
filter-dropdown-menu: category item selected (closes sheet, filters expense list on dashboard with 200ms fade)

structured.themeStrategy: Single light-mode theme only. All tokens are defined as Tailwind theme extensions in tailwind.config.js. Per-category dynamic colors bypass the token system via CSS custom properties (--category-color) set through inline style on each category-scoped element, consumed via Tailwind's arbitrary property syntax (e.g., bg-[var(--category-color)]). No dark-mode or brand-switching variants are required for this scope; the token set is structured semantically (color.brand.*, color.surface.*, color.status.*, color.chart.*, color.text.*, color.overlay.*, space.*, typography.size.*, typography.weight.*, radius.*, shadow.*) so a dark-mode layer can be added later by overriding leaf values without renaming tokens.

tasks.title: Project scaffold: Vite + React + Tailwind + Dexie + Zustand
tasks.description: Initialize the Vite project with React 18 + TypeScript. Configure Tailwind CSS v3 with the full design token set (colors, spacing, typography, radius, shadows). Install and configure Dexie.js, Zustand, shadcn/ui, Recharts, react-number-format, and CVA. Set up folder structure (components/, store/, db/, schemas/, lib/, pages/).

tasks.title: Dexie database schema, Zod validators, and seed data
tasks.description: Define the Dexie.js database with tables for Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, and QuickAddSuggestion with proper indexes. Create Zod validation schemas for all durable entities. Implement database versioning/migration and seed default categories (Food, Transport, Shopping, Bills, Entertainment, Health) and default UserSettings (USD, $2000).

tasks.title: Dexie data service layer for CRUD and derived computations
tasks.description: Implement service functions for all Dexie CRUD operations: expenses (create, update, delete, list, search, top), categories (create, update, delete, list), budgets (create/upsert, update, get), settings (get, update), and quick-add suggestions (compute from expense frequency). Implement derived data computation functions for MonthSummary, CategorySpending, and DailySpending that aggregate from raw expense data. Include CSV export generation utility.

tasks.title: Zustand store with filter, UI, and derived data slices
tasks.description: Create the Zustand store organized into independent slices: filterSlice (selectedMonth, activeCategoryFilter), uiSlice (activeTab, settingsOpen, popoverTargetId, chartViewMode), derivedSlice (MonthSummary, CategorySpending[], DailySpending[] caches with recompute actions that call derived.service). Wire up shallow equality selectors. Implement recompute triggers that refresh derived data from Dexie on mutations.

tasks.title: Shared UI primitives: Button, Badge, Card, Chip, Input, Toast, EmptyState
tasks.description: Build the foundational CVA-based UI components used across all screens. Button (primary, secondary, ghost variants + sizes + disabled/loading states), StatusBadge (on-track/heads-up/over-budget), Card (default surface), Chip (selectable with color states for categories and payment methods), Input (with label, error, character counter), ToastProvider with toast context, and EmptyState (icon + message + optional CTA). All components use Tailwind tokens only with focus-visible rings and min 36x36 click targets.

tasks.title: Top navigation bar, routing, and month selector
tasks.description: Implement the TopNavBar component (64px) with AppLogo (pulse icon + CashPulse text, links to /), NavTabs (Dashboard, Add Expense, Insights) with sliding underline indicator, MonthSelector pill with left/right chevrons for month navigation, and settings gear IconButton. Set up React Router with routes for / (Dashboard), /add-expense (Add Expense), and /insights (Insights). Month changes update the filterSlice selectedMonth and trigger derived data recomputation.

tasks.title: Dashboard page: Budget Summary card and Category Donut chart
tasks.description: Build the DashboardPage with two-column layout. Left column: BudgetSummaryCard showing total spent vs monthly budget with animated counter, BudgetProgressBar (blue <80%, amber 80-100%, red >100%), StatusBadge, and remaining amount. CategoryDonutChartCard with 160px Recharts PieChart donut, center transaction count, hover-expand segments with tooltips, and DonutLegend with colored dots, names, and amounts. Handle empty states (gray ring, $0.00, empty progress bar). Responsive: stacks to single column at 768-1024px.

tasks.title: Dashboard: Recent Expenses list with filter, popover, and empty state
tasks.description: Build the right column of the Dashboard: RecentExpensesCard with header and CategoryFilterDropdown (Radix Select with All Categories + category options). RecentExpensesList rendering ExpenseRow cards with category-colored left border, CategoryIconCircle, merchant name, category label, date, amount (–$XX.XX), and payment method. Rows are hoverable and clickable. ExpenseDetailPopover (320px Radix Popover) showing full expense details with Edit button (navigates to /add-expense?id=X) and Delete button with inline confirmation (text changes to 'Confirm delete?' with 3s auto-revert). EmptyState for no expenses. Responsive scrollable list.

tasks.title: Add/Edit Expense form with all inputs and save flow
tasks.description: Build the AddExpensePage with single-column form (max-width 520px). HeroAmountInput (32px, auto-focused, numeric-only with react-number-format decimal formatting). CategorySelectorGrid (3x2 chip grid with colored selection states). DatePickerInput with mini calendar dropdown (Radix Popover, no future dates). PaymentMethodSelector (Cash, Debit Card, Credit Card, UPI/Other chips). NoteInput (100 char max with counter at 80+). SaveButton (full-width, disabled when amount=$0 or no category, shows '✓ Saved!' with emerald bg on success then navigates to /). Edit mode: pre-fills form from expense ID in query params. Validates via Zod before Dexie write. Triggers derived data recomputation on save.

tasks.title: Quick Entry Suggestions and Inline Category Creation
tasks.description: Build QuickEntrySuggestions: horizontal scrollable row of 3-4 suggestion chips below save button, sourced from QuickAddSuggestion data. Clicking a chip auto-fills the form with cascade animation (50ms per field). Hidden when no frequent expenses exist. Build InlineCategoryCreation: '+ New Category' link below category grid that expands an inline row with text input, 6-color picker circles, and Save mini-button. Saving creates category via Dexie service, adds chip to grid, and auto-selects it.

tasks.title: Insights page: Period Summary, Daily Spending Chart, and Month Comparison
tasks.description: Build the InsightsPage with full-width layout. PeriodSummaryCard with three StatCells (Total Spent, Daily Average, Transactions) separated by vertical dividers, each with value, label, and trend indicator (up/down arrow + percentage vs last month, green for decrease, red for increase). DailySpendingChartCard with Bar/Line toggle (ChartViewToggle). DailyBarChart (blue-600 bars, red-500 for 2x+ average, dashed amber average line). DailyLineChart (smooth curve with filled area). Both support hover tooltips and animate on first render with motion-safe. MonthComparisonCard with side-by-side vertical bars (current vs last month) growing from bottom, and difference badge below.

tasks.title: Insights: Top Categories, Biggest Expenses, and Export CSV
tasks.description: Build TopCategoriesCard with horizontal stacked bar (proportional category segments, lift on hover with tooltip) and sorted category list with colored dot, name, individual progress bar, amount, and percentage. BiggestExpensesCard listing top 5 expenses by amount with rank circle (#1 blue-600), merchant name, category, date, and amount. ExportCsvButton (secondary button with download icon) that triggers CSV generation from export service, changes text to '✓ Exported' with emerald text for 1.5s. Handle empty states for all cards.

tasks.title: Settings dialog with currency, budget, and category management
tasks.description: Build SettingsDialog as a centered Radix Dialog modal (max-width 420px) with dark scrim overlay. CurrencySelector dropdown (USD, EUR, GBP, INR, JPY). MonthlyBudgetInput with currency prefix and numeric formatting (default $2,000). CategoryManager with colored dot list of categories, trash icons (disabled for default categories), and '+ Add category' link that opens inline creation row. Dismissible via close button, scrim click, or Escape. Currency change triggers toast notification ('Currency updated to EUR') via ToastProvider. Saves to Dexie via settings and category services.

tasks.title: Responsive breakpoints, reduced motion, and keyboard accessibility polish
tasks.description: Add responsive layout breakpoints across all pages: 768-1024px stacks dashboard to single column with reduced chart heights; 1024-1440px standard two-column; >1440px maximizes whitespace. Implement prefers-reduced-motion support: wrap all chart animations with motion-safe checks, disable bounce/cascade effects, replace slide transitions with instant cuts. Polish keyboard navigation: ensure Tab/Enter/Space/Escape work on all interactive elements, verify 2px blue-600 focus rings with 2px offset, ensure chart tooltips are keyboard-accessible, verify 36x36px minimum click targets, add ARIA roles/labels to charts and interactive elements.

tasks.title: Integration: end-to-end user flows and cross-screen data consistency
tasks.description: Wire up all cross-screen interactions and verify data flow: adding an expense on /add-expense updates dashboard budget summary, donut chart, and recent expenses list on /. Deleting via popover removes from list and recomputes derived data. Month navigation updates all screens. Settings currency change propagates to all amount displays. Edit flow pre-fills form correctly. Quick entry suggestions update after repeated expense patterns. Export CSV generates correct file. Verify empty states render correctly when no data exists.

tasks.title: Scaffold Vite + React + TypeScript project with Tailwind, shadcn/ui, and Dexie
tasks.description: Initialize the Vite project with React 18 and TypeScript. Configure Tailwind CSS v3 with the full design token set (colors, spacing, typography, radius, shadows) in tailwind.config.js. Install and configure shadcn/ui, Recharts, react-number-format, Dexie.js, Zustand, CVA, and Zod. Set up the project directory structure with folders for components/, screens/, store/, db/, schemas/, lib/, and hooks/. Configure React Router with routes for /, /add-expense, and /insights.

tasks.title: Define Dexie database, Zod schemas, and seed default categories
tasks.description: Create the Dexie.js database class with tables for Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, and QuickAddSuggestion with proper indexes. Define Zod validation schemas for each durable entity in a shared schemas/ directory. Implement database versioning and seed 6 default categories (Food, Transport, Shopping, Bills, Entertainment, Health) with colors and icons. Initialize singleton UserSettings with USD currency and $2000 default budget.

tasks.title: Create Zustand store with filter, UI, and derived data slices
tasks.description: Implement the Zustand store organized into independent slices: filterSlice (active category filter, selected month YYYY-MM), uiSlice (modal open state, active tab, popover target expense ID), and derivedSlice (MonthSummary, CategorySpending[], DailySpending[] caches). Create service functions that read from Dexie, compute derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending), and update the Zustand derived slice. Implement recompute functions triggered after every Dexie write. Use shallow equality selectors.

tasks.title: Build shared UI primitives: Button, Badge, Chip, Card, IconButton, EmptyState, Toast
tasks.description: Create reusable UI components using CVA for variant management and Tailwind tokens. Build Button (primary, secondary, ghost variants + sizes + disabled/loading states), StatusBadge (ON_TRACK/HEADS_UP/OVER_BUDGET with colors), Chip (selectable with colored states), Card (default surface with shadow), IconButton (36x36 min click target), EmptyState (icon + message + optional CTA), and ToastProvider with toast notification system (success/warning/error/info with auto-dismiss timers). All components include focus-visible rings and motion-safe animations.

tasks.title: Build TopNavBar with logo, navigation tabs, month selector, and settings trigger
tasks.description: Implement the 64px TopNavBar component with CashPulse logo (pulse icon + text, links to /), three NavTabs (Dashboard, Add Expense, Insights) with sliding underline indicator based on current route, MonthSelector pill with left/right chevrons for month navigation updating the Zustand filterSlice, and a settings gear IconButton that opens the settings dialog. MonthSelector displays formatted month/year (e.g., 'Mar 2026'). Tabs use React Router NavLink for active state.

tasks.title: Build Dashboard left column: Budget Summary Card and Category Donut Chart
tasks.description: Implement the DashboardLeftColumn with BudgetSummaryCard showing total spent vs monthly budget with animated counter effect, progress bar (blue <80%, amber 80-100%, red >100%), StatusBadge (On Track/Heads Up/Over Budget), and remaining amount. Build CategoryDonutChartCard with a 160px Recharts PieChart donut showing category spending distribution with colored segments, center total transaction count, hover expand with tooltip (name, amount, percentage), and DonutLegend listing categories with colored dots, names, and amounts. Handle empty states (gray ring with 0 transactions, $0 budget). Amounts animate on month change.

tasks.title: Build Dashboard right column: Recent Expenses List with filter, rows, and detail popover
tasks.description: Implement the DashboardRightColumn with RecentExpensesCard containing a header with category filter dropdown (using Radix Select) and a scrollable list of ExpenseRow components. Each row is a white card with category-colored left border, CategoryIconCircle, merchant/description, category label, date, amount (prefixed with –), and payment method. Rows are hoverable and clickable. Build the ExpenseDetailPopover (320px, anchored to clicked row) showing amount, category with colored dot, date, payment method, optional note, Edit button (navigates to /add-expense?edit={id}), and Delete button with inline confirmation ('Confirm delete?' with 3-second auto-revert). Build the CategoryFilterDropdown component. Handle empty state with receipt icon and 'No expenses yet' message with Add Expense CTA.

tasks.title: Build Add/Edit Expense Form with all inputs, validation, and save flow
tasks.description: Implement the AddExpensePage with a single-column form (max-width 520px). Build HeroAmountInput (32px, auto-focused, numeric-only with react-number-format decimal auto-formatting), CategorySelectorGrid (3x2 chips with colored selection states), DatePickerInput with mini calendar dropdown (no future dates, using Radix Popover), PaymentMethodSelector (Cash, Debit Card, Credit Card, UPI/Other chips), NoteInput (100 char max with counter at 80+), and full-width SaveButton. Disabled state when amount is $0 or no category selected. On save, validate with Zod, write to Dexie, show '✓ Saved!' with emerald background, then navigate to dashboard. Support edit mode via query param (?edit={id}) that pre-fills the form.

tasks.title: Build Quick Entry Suggestions and Inline Category Creation on Add Expense screen

Build InlineCategoryCreation: a '+ New Category' link below the category grid that opens an inline row with text input, 6-color picker circles, and Save mini-button. Saving creates a new category via Dexie, adds the chip to the grid, and auto-selects it.

Build DailySpendingChartCard with Bar/Line toggle using Recharts: BarChart with vertical bars per day (blue-600, red-500 for 2x+ average), LineChart with smooth curve and filled area, hover tooltips (date, amount, count), dashed amber average line, staggered entry animations. Build MonthComparisonCard with side-by-side vertical bars (this vs last month), grow-from-bottom animation, and difference badge ('You spent $X less/more'). Handle empty/no-data states.

Build ExportCsvButton that generates CSV from expense data (date, description, category, amount, paymentMethod, note), triggers download, and shows '✓ Exported' with emerald text for 1.5s. Build MonthlyBudgetInput with currency prefix and numeric formatting (default $2,000). Build CategoryManager showing colored dot list of categories with trash icons (disabled for default categories) and '+ Add category' link that opens inline creation row. Dismissible via close button, scrim click, or Escape key.

Implement prefers-reduced-motion support: replace chart animations with instant renders, disable bounce/cascade effects, replace slide transitions with instant cuts using the useReducedMotion hook. Add ARIA roles and labels on charts and interactive elements. Ensure 36x36px minimum click targets throughout.

Ensure the settings dialog is accessible from any screen via the TopNavBar gear icon. Verify month navigation updates all dashboard and insights data reactively. Ensure expense creation/edit/delete flows trigger derived data recomputation and UI updates across screens. Wire the edit flow from ExpenseDetailPopover to AddExpensePage with query params. Ensure currency changes propagate to all displayed amounts. Add the global layout wrapper with TopNavBar on all pages.

tasks.description: Implement the SettingsDialog as a centered Radix Dialog modal (max-width 420px) with dark scrim overlay. Contains CurrencySelector dropdown (USD, EUR, GBP, INR, JPY), MonthlyBudgetInput with currency prefix (default $2,000, numeric with react-number-format), CategoryManager with colored dot list, trash icons (disabled for default categories), and '+ Add category' link that opens inline creation row. Dismissible via close button, scrim click, or Escape. Currency change triggers toast notification ('Currency updated to EUR', 2s auto-dismiss). Updates UserSettings and Budget via services.

tasks.title: Wire up app routing, page assembly, and responsive layout
tasks.description: Configure React Router with routes for / (Dashboard), /add-expense (Add Expense), /add-expense/:id (Edit Expense), and /insights (Insights). Assemble InsightsPage by combining insights-summary-charts and insights-categories-expenses sections. Wire SettingsDialog to open from gear icon in TopNavBar. Implement responsive layout breakpoints across all pages. Ensure month navigation triggers counter animations and chart re-renders. Connect all pages to Zustand store for consistent state.

tasks.title: Write integration tests for core user flows
tasks.description: Write integration tests covering the critical user flows: adding an expense (form validation, save, redirect), editing an expense (pre-fill, update), deleting an expense (inline confirmation, 3s revert), budget summary updates after expense changes, category filter on dashboard, month navigation, settings dialog (currency change toast, budget update), CSV export, and empty states. Test reduced motion behavior and keyboard navigation for key interactive elements.

alternatives.name: localStorage + in-memory state (Zustand/Context)
alternatives.description: All 8 data entities are stored as serialized JSON in the browser's localStorage. A lightweight state manager (Zustand or React Context + useReducer) holds the working in-memory copy, syncing to localStorage on every mutation. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed on-the-fly via selector functions rather than stored separately. QuickAddSuggestion is built from a frequency map over Expense records.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies if using React Context; +1 dependency (zustand ~3KB gzipped) for Zustand variant
localStorage cap of ~5MB limits history to roughly 50,000–100,000 expense records — more than sufficient for a single-user personal tracker
No query language: filtering/aggregation for Insights charts must be hand-written JS — adds ~200–400 LOC of utility functions
Data is not portable across browsers/devices (scope-exclusion: no sync/cloud — acceptable per constraints)
Synchronous localStorage.setItem can block the main thread on large payloads; mitigated by debouncing writes or using a Web Worker
Blast radius: affects ~3 files (store initializer, persistence middleware, entity selectors) — lowest blast radius of all options
Chart renders stay well within 600ms since all data is already in memory — no async fetch latency

alternatives.name: IndexedDB via Dexie.js
alternatives.description: All 8 entities are stored in a structured IndexedDB database managed by Dexie.js. Each entity maps to a Dexie table with typed indexes (e.g., Expense indexed on date, categoryId; Budget indexed on categoryId). Derived summary entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via Dexie's Collection API and cached in React state. Dexie's liveQuery() hook enables reactive UI updates without manual cache invalidation.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: dexie (~23KB gzipped) — no backend or build-time tooling changes required
Supports storage of hundreds of thousands of records with indexed queries — future-proof for power users
Async API (Promise-based) adds complexity to data-fetching layer; all reads must be awaited, requiring loading states in 7 screens
liveQuery() reactivity simplifies Dashboard and Insights refresh but requires wrapping components in useLiveQuery — affects ~10–15 component files
IndexedDB is supported in all target evergreen browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) — no compatibility risk
Migration path: Dexie's versioned schema migrations handle entity changes cleanly (e.g., adding fields to Expense)
Blast radius: medium — affects store layer (~5 files), all data-fetching hooks (~8 hooks), and loading-state UI in screens (~7 screens)

alternatives.name: SQLite via sql.js (WASM) + localStorage persistence
alternatives.description: sql.js compiles SQLite to WebAssembly, running a full relational database in-browser. All 8 entities are modeled as normalized SQL tables with foreign keys and indexes. The database binary is serialized to localStorage (or a File System Access API file) on each write. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via SQL GROUP BY queries, making Insights aggregations trivial to implement correctly.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: sql.js (~1.5MB WASM binary) — significantly increases initial bundle size and load time; requires async WASM initialization before any data access
WASM load adds ~300–800ms cold-start latency on first page load, potentially conflicting with the 350ms screen-transition constraint on initial render
Full SQL expressiveness makes complex Insights queries (e.g., rolling 30-day averages, category % breakdowns) trivial — 10–20 LOC vs 200+ LOC in alt-1
Serializing the entire DB binary to localStorage on every write is expensive for large datasets; requires debouncing or a Web Worker to avoid main-thread jank
Overkill for the 8 entities and single-user scope defined in this PRD — engineering complexity outweighs query convenience
Blast radius: high — affects build config (WASM asset handling), store layer, all query hooks, and requires async initialization guard across all 7 screens

alternatives.name: Supabase (PostgreSQL) — client-only, anonymous/local mode
alternatives.description: Supabase provides a hosted PostgreSQL backend with a typed JS client. All 8 entities are modeled as Postgres tables with Row Level Security. The Supabase JS client handles all CRUD operations. Since auth and multi-user are out of scope, a single anonymous session or a fixed local user ID is used. Supabase Realtime can power live Dashboard updates. This option is included as the 'maximum capability' alternative.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: @supabase/supabase-js (~75KB gzipped) plus a required Supabase project (external service dependency)
Violates the soft scope-exclusion constraint: 'Out of scope: Data backup, sync, or cloud storage' — data would live in a remote Postgres instance
Violates the soft scope-exclusion constraint: 'Out of scope: Backend API development' — requires Supabase project setup, table migrations, and RLS policy configuration
Network latency for every read/write risks breaching the 150ms micro-interaction and 350ms screen-transition performance constraints without aggressive client-side caching
Requires internet connectivity — app becomes non-functional offline, which is a poor UX for a daily-use expense tracker
Blast radius: critical — affects build config, environment variables, all data hooks, error handling for network failures, and introduces an external service dependency with its own SLA

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency in-browser storage to a fully hosted cloud database, covering every realistic option for a greenfield, desktop-web-only, single-user personal finance app. PostgreSQL standalone (without Supabase) was excluded because it requires a backend server, which directly violates the hard scope-exclusion on backend API development. Firebase/Firestore was excluded for the same reason (cloud sync out of scope) and because it adds more complexity than Supabase without meaningful benefit here. A pure in-memory (no persistence) option was excluded because it would lose all data on page refresh, making the app unusable as a daily tracker. The four selected alternatives represent: (1) the minimal-change, zero-infrastructure path; (2) the structured browser-native path with real query capabilities; (3) the full-SQL-in-browser path for completeness; and (4) the cloud path to explicitly surface its constraint violations.
structured.recommendation: alt-2 (IndexedDB via Dexie.js) is the recommended choice. It provides structured, indexed storage that scales gracefully with real user data, supports all 8 entities with proper relationships and efficient queries, and keeps everything client-side (respecting all scope-exclusions around backend, sync, and auth). Dexie's liveQuery() eliminates manual cache invalidation across the Dashboard and Insights screens, and its async API is a natural fit for React's data-fetching patterns. The single 23KB dependency is a proportionate cost. alt-1 (localStorage) is a valid fallback if the team wants absolute zero dependencies and is comfortable writing manual aggregation logic for the Insights charts — it should be chosen if the Insights feature set is simple (e.g., only current-month totals, no rolling windows).

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS v3 (Utility-First)
alternatives.description: Use Tailwind CSS as the sole styling layer. Design tokens (spacing, radii, shadows, colors) are codified in tailwind.config.js under a custom theme extension. All components — cards, chips, popovers, form inputs, badges — are styled via utility classes directly in JSX. The 4px base spacing unit maps to Tailwind's default scale (space-1 = 4px). Custom values for card-radius (16px → rounded-2xl), button-radius (10px → rounded-[10px]), badge-radius (20px → rounded-full), and input-radius (8px → rounded-lg) are added to the theme. Focus rings are enforced via ring-2 ring-blue-600 ring-offset-2 utilities. The prefers-reduced-motion constraint is handled via Tailwind's motion-reduce: variant prefix on all transition/animation utilities.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Zero runtime CSS-in-JS overhead — all styles are statically extracted at build time, directly supporting the 150ms micro-interaction and 350ms screen transition performance budgets
POSITIVE: Tailwind's JIT engine produces a minimal CSS bundle (~5–15KB gzipped for a project this size), keeping parse/render time negligible
POSITIVE: motion-reduce: variant natively handles the prefers-reduced-motion soft constraint with no extra code
POSITIVE: Responsive breakpoint utilities (md:, lg:, xl:) map cleanly to the three required breakpoints (768px, 1024px, 1440px)
NEGATIVE: Adds 1 new dependency (tailwindcss + postcss + autoprefixer — 3 dev deps total); requires PostCSS pipeline configuration
NEGATIVE: Custom token values (10px button radius, 20px badge radius, specific shadow values) require tailwind.config.js entries — any token drift between config and design must be manually audited
NEGATIVE: Long className strings on complex components (e.g., the Add Expense form with validation states) reduce JSX readability; mitigated by extracting component abstractions
NEGATIVE: WCAG 2.1 AA focus ring enforcement (2px blue-600, 2px offset) must be applied consistently by convention — no compile-time enforcement; requires team discipline or a lint rule
NEGATIVE: Inline conditional styling for validation states (e.g., error borders on amount input) requires string interpolation or a utility like clsx/cva — adds 1–2 additional micro-dependencies

alternatives.name: CSS Modules (Minimal Change / Zero Runtime)
alternatives.description: Use CSS Modules with standard CSS (or optionally PostCSS for nesting). Each component gets a co-located .module.css file. Design tokens are defined once in a global :root block (tokens.css) as CSS custom properties (--radius-card: 16px; --color-primary: #2563eb; --shadow-card: 0 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,0.06); etc.) and consumed via var() in module files. Focus rings, WCAG targets, and reduced-motion rules live in a global base.css. This is the most framework-agnostic, lowest-dependency approach — supported natively by Vite, Next.js, and Create React App without additional configuration.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Zero additional runtime dependencies — CSS Modules are natively supported by all major React bundlers (Vite, Next.js, CRA) out of the box
POSITIVE: CSS custom properties for tokens provide a single source of truth; token changes in tokens.css propagate everywhere instantly with no rebuild required for dev
POSITIVE: Scoped class names eliminate all style collision risk across the 7 screens and shared component library
POSITIVE: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) blocks in base.css or per-module files handle the motion constraint cleanly with standard CSS
POSITIVE: Lowest blast radius of all options — adopting this approach touches only the build config (0 changes) and introduces a file-per-component pattern

NEGATIVE: Verbose for highly dynamic styles — the Add Expense form's real-time validation states (error/success borders, focus rings) require multiple class compositions via composes: or clsx, adding boilerplate
NEGATIVE: No built-in utility for responsive variants — breakpoint media queries must be manually written in each module file, increasing per-component CSS line count by ~30% vs Tailwind

NEGATIVE: Design token enforcement is purely by convention; a developer can hardcode #2563eb instead of var(--color-primary) with no tooling warning unless a stylelint rule is configured (adds 1 dev dep)
NEGATIVE: Theming or future dark mode (currently out of scope but a common follow-on) requires re-architecting token structure
NEGATIVE: Slightly higher cognitive overhead when tracing styles — must navigate between .jsx and .module.css files; tooling like CSS Modules TypeScript types (adds 1 dev dep) helps

alternatives.name: styled-components v6 (CSS-in-JS)
alternatives.description: Use styled-components to co-locate styles with component logic. A ThemeProvider at the app root exposes all design tokens (colors, spacing scale, radii, shadows, typography) as a typed theme object. Each component (Card, Button, Input, Badge, Chip) is a styled primitive. Dynamic styles for validation states, focus rings, and interaction states are expressed as interpolated props (e.g., ${props => props.$hasError && 'border-color: var(--color-error)'}). The createGlobalStyle API handles base resets, Inter font loading, and the prefers-reduced-motion media query globally.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Strongest co-location of logic and styles — component props drive visual states (error, disabled, selected) with full TypeScript type safety on the theme object
POSITIVE: ThemeProvider enforces token usage at the component API level — impossible to use an off-token color without explicitly bypassing the theme
POSITIVE: Excellent for the design system's dynamic states: chip selection, popover open/close, budget status colors all map naturally to prop-driven style interpolations

NEGATIVE: Adds 1 production dependency (styled-components ~12KB gzipped) with a runtime CSS injection cost — styles are generated and injected at component render time, creating a measurable overhead risk against the 150ms micro-interaction budget on lower-end hardware
NEGATIVE: styled-components v6 requires React 18+ and does not support React Server Components (RSC) — if the project ever moves to Next.js App Router, a full migration would be required

NEGATIVE: CSS-in-JS runtime means no static CSS extraction by default — critical path CSS is not available until JS executes, slightly increasing Time to First Meaningful Paint vs CSS Modules or Tailwind
NEGATIVE: Larger learning curve for developers unfamiliar with the tagged template literal API; interpolation bugs (missing semicolons, prop naming collisions) can be subtle
NEGATIVE: Bundle size impact: styled-components adds ~12KB gzipped to the production bundle — acceptable for this app size but non-trivial
NEGATIVE: Debugging requires styled-components Babel plugin (1 additional dev dep) to get readable class names in DevTools; without it, generated class names are hashes

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS + CVA (Class Variance Authority) — Structured Utility-First
alternatives.description: Extends alt-tailwind with Class Variance Authority (CVA) as a variant management layer. CVA defines typed component variant APIs (e.g., button({ intent: 'primary', size: 'md' })) that resolve to Tailwind class strings. This directly addresses Tailwind's readability weakness for complex multi-state components like the Add Expense form, category chips, and budget status badges. Tailwind handles all base styling and responsive utilities; CVA handles the combinatorial variant logic (validation states × focus states × disabled states). The tailwind-merge utility (1 dep) resolves class conflicts when variants are composed. This is the approach used by shadcn/ui and is well-established in the React/Tailwind ecosystem.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Retains all performance benefits of Tailwind (static extraction, zero runtime, JIT) while adding structured variant management
POSITIVE: CVA produces fully typed variant APIs — TypeScript will error if an invalid variant combination is passed to a component, enforcing design system consistency at compile time
POSITIVE: Directly solves the WCAG focus ring consistency problem: focus ring utilities are baked into the CVA base definition for each interactive component, not applied ad-hoc
POSITIVE: Scales well across all 7 screens and 25 features — the variant system grows linearly with component complexity rather than exponentially
POSITIVE: shadcn/ui components (if adopted) use this exact pattern, providing a ready-made accessible component library aligned with the design tokens
NEGATIVE: Adds 2 additional dev/prod dependencies beyond Tailwind: cva (~2KB) and tailwind-merge (~3KB) — total of 5 deps (tailwindcss, postcss, autoprefixer, cva, tailwind-merge)
NEGATIVE: CVA introduces a new mental model on top of Tailwind — developers must learn both the utility-first approach and the variant definition API
NEGATIVE: tailwind.config.js token configuration is still required and still subject to drift from design specs — same audit discipline needed as alt-tailwind

NEGATIVE: Slightly more upfront scaffolding: each shared component (Button, Input, Card, Badge, Chip) needs a CVA definition file before it can be used, adding ~1–2 hours of initial setup per component

structured.rationale: Four alternatives were selected to cover the full spectrum from zero-dependency to full CSS-in-JS, with two Tailwind variants included because the utility-first approach is the dominant pattern for greenfield React projects in 2024 and the CVA extension meaningfully changes the tradeoff profile. styled-components was included as the canonical CSS-in-JS representative despite its runtime cost, because its ThemeProvider pattern is the strongest token-enforcement mechanism. CSS Modules was included as the mandatory "minimal change" alternative — it requires no new dependencies and is natively supported by all bundlers. Excluded alternatives: (1) Sass/SCSS — adds a preprocessor dependency with no meaningful advantage over CSS custom properties for this project's token needs; (2) Emotion — functionally similar to styled-components with the same RSC incompatibility and runtime cost, redundant to include both; (3) Vanilla Extract — zero-runtime CSS-in-JS is compelling but adds significant build complexity (Vite plugin required) and has a smaller ecosystem, making it a poor fit for a greenfield project prioritizing delivery speed; (4) UnoCSS — too experimental for a financial application requiring long-term maintainability. The recommendation favors alt-tailwind-cva because it uniquely satisfies the WCAG enforcement, performance, and design token consistency constraints simultaneously, at the cost of only 2 additional small dependencies.
structured.recommendation: alt-tailwind-cva — Tailwind CSS + CVA is the recommended approach for CashPulse. It is the only alternative that simultaneously satisfies all three critical constraints: (1) **Performance** — zero runtime CSS generation means all micro-interactions, screen transitions, and chart renders operate purely on pre-extracted CSS with no JS overhead; (2) **WCAG 2.1 AA** — CVA bakes focus rings, minimum touch targets, and ARIA-compatible state classes into each component's variant definition, making compliance structural rather than conventional; (3) **Design Consistency** — the tailwind.config.js theme extension is the single source of truth for all 9 design tokens (Inter, 4px unit, 5 radius values, 2 shadow values, blue-600), and CVA's typed variant API prevents off-token usage at compile time. The 5-dependency footprint is the lowest of any approach that provides this level of structural enforcement. If the team has strong CSS Modules familiarity and wants to minimize onboarding time, alt-css-modules is a sound fallback — it achieves the performance goals equally well and adds zero dependencies, at the cost of manual breakpoint repetition and convention-only token enforcement.

alternatives.name: localStorage + in-memory state (Zustand/Context)
alternatives.description: All 8 data entities are stored as serialized JSON in the browser's localStorage. A lightweight state manager (Zustand or React Context + useReducer) holds the working in-memory copy, syncing to localStorage on every mutation. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed on-the-fly via selector functions rather than stored separately. QuickAddSuggestion is built by scanning recent Expense records at form-open time.
tradeoffs: Zero new runtime dependencies if using React Context; +1 dependency (zustand ~3KB gzipped) for Zustand variant
Storage cap of ~5MB per origin — sufficient for years of single-user expense records (avg expense record ~200 bytes → ~25,000 records before limit)
No query language: filtering/aggregation for Insights screen must be hand-written JS — adds ~200–400 LOC of utility functions
Data is not portable across browsers or devices (scope-exclusion: no sync/cloud — acceptable per constraints)
Derived entities recomputed on every read; with <1,000 records this is sub-millisecond, well within 600ms chart render budget
Blast radius: affects ~5 files (store setup, 3 screen containers, 1 shared hook) — lowest of all options
No migration tooling needed — greenfield start means no existing schema to evolve
Data survives page refresh but is lost on browser data clear — acceptable for a no-auth, no-sync scope

alternatives.name: IndexedDB via Dexie.js
alternatives.description: All 8 entities are stored in a structured IndexedDB database using Dexie.js as the wrapper. Each entity maps to a Dexie table with typed indexes (e.g., Expense indexed on date, categoryId; Budget indexed on categoryId+month). Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are stored as materialized views updated via Dexie transactions on every Expense write. QuickAddSuggestion is a live query over the Expense table.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: dexie (~23KB gzipped) — no backend required
Supports structured queries with indexes: date-range filtering for Insights charts is O(log n) vs O(n) full scan in localStorage approach
Async API (Promise-based) requires careful integration with React rendering — all data fetches must be wrapped in useEffect or React Query; adds ~3–5 files of query hooks
Schema versioning built-in via Dexie migrations — future entity changes are manageable without data loss
Storage quota is much larger than localStorage (~60% of available disk space in Chrome) — effectively unlimited for this use case
Blast radius: affects ~10–12 files (db schema file, 8 entity hooks, 3 screen containers)
Dexie liveQuery enables reactive UI updates without manual re-fetch — reduces boilerplate in screen components
Slightly more complex debugging than localStorage (requires DevTools Application > IndexedDB panel)

alternatives.name: SQLite via sql.js (WASM) + localStorage persistence
alternatives.description: A full SQLite database runs in-browser via sql.js (compiled to WebAssembly). The DB is serialized to a Uint8Array and persisted in localStorage or IndexedDB on every write. All 8 entities are proper relational tables with foreign keys. Derived entities are computed via SQL views (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending as VIEW definitions). QuickAddSuggestion is a SQL query over recent Expense rows.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 large dependency: sql.js WASM bundle (~1.5MB uncompressed, ~500KB gzipped) — significantly increases initial load time, likely violating the 350ms screen transition budget on first load
Full SQL expressiveness: complex Insights aggregations (GROUP BY category, date truncation, running totals) are trivial to write and maintain
WASM initialization is async and must complete before any DB operation — requires a loading gate on app startup, adding UX complexity
Serialization of the entire DB to localStorage on every write is O(DB size) — with 1,000+ records this could exceed 150ms micro-interaction budget for write operations
Blast radius: affects ~15 files (db init module, migration scripts, 8 entity repositories, 3 screen containers, 1 loading gate component)
Overkill for single-user, no-sync, no-backend scope — relational integrity benefits are not leveraged without multi-user or server-side validation
Origin Private File System (OPFS) variant via wa-sqlite would solve serialization perf but adds further complexity and Safari support caveats

alternatives.name: Supabase (PostgreSQL) — client-only, anonymous sessions
alternatives.description: A Supabase project provides a hosted PostgreSQL database accessed via the Supabase JS client. All 8 entities are proper Postgres tables. Anonymous auth sessions (Supabase anon key + RLS policies scoped to session ID stored in localStorage) provide data isolation without a login flow. Derived entities are Postgres materialized views or computed via Supabase RPC functions.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: @supabase/supabase-js (~75KB gzipped) plus a Supabase project (free tier: 500MB DB, 2GB bandwidth)
Violates the soft scope-exclusion constraint: 'Out of scope: Data backup, sync, or cloud storage' — data now lives in the cloud even without explicit user intent
Network latency for every read/write: even at p50 ~50ms round-trip, write operations risk exceeding the 150ms micro-interaction budget without optimistic UI updates
Requires optimistic UI layer (+5–8 files of mutation hooks with rollback logic) to meet performance constraints
Anonymous sessions tied to localStorage anon token — clearing browser data orphans all cloud records with no recovery path (worse UX than pure local storage)
Blast radius: affects ~20+ files (supabase client, 8 entity service modules, optimistic mutation hooks, 3 screen containers, error boundary components)
Enables future features (sync, multi-device) at zero migration cost — but those are explicitly out of scope
Free tier project pauses after 1 week of inactivity — unacceptable for a production personal finance tool without a paid plan

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency local state to fully hosted cloud DB, covering every realistic option for a greenfield, desktop-web-only, no-auth, no-sync personal expense tracker. Options excluded: (1) Plain in-memory state without persistence — rejected because data loss on page refresh is unacceptable for a financial tracker. (2) PouchDB/CouchDB — rejected because its sync-first design adds complexity with no benefit given the explicit no-sync scope exclusion. (3) Firebase Firestore — rejected for the same reasons as Supabase but with a heavier SDK. (4) Redux Persist — rejected as it is a persistence layer, not a data store, and would be layered on top of alt-1 anyway. The four selected alternatives represent meaningfully different architectural commitments: local flat JSON (alt-1), local structured/indexed (alt-2), local relational WASM (alt-3), and remote hosted relational (alt-4).
structured.recommendation: alt-2 (IndexedDB via Dexie.js). It is the sweet spot for CashPulse's requirements: it handles all 8 entities with proper indexing for fast date/category range queries needed by the Insights screen, stays entirely local (respecting all scope exclusions around sync and cloud), has a manageable blast radius of ~10–12 files, and its async reactive model (liveQuery) pairs cleanly with React without fighting the rendering model. Alt-1 (localStorage) is a valid minimal-change fallback if the team wants to defer complexity, but the hand-written aggregation code for Insights charts will become a maintenance burden as the feature set grows to 25 features across 8 entities. Alt-3 and Alt-4 are over-engineered for this scope.

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS v3 (Utility-First)
alternatives.description: Use Tailwind CSS as the sole styling layer. Design tokens (spacing, radii, shadows, colors) are codified in tailwind.config.js under the `extend` key. All components — cards, chips, popovers, form inputs — are styled via utility classes directly in JSX. The `prefers-reduced-motion` constraint is handled via Tailwind's `motion-safe:` and `motion-reduce:` variants. Focus rings are enforced with `focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-blue-600 focus-visible:ring-offset-2`. Responsive breakpoints (768px, 1024px, 1440px) map to custom `sm`, `md`, `lg` values in config. Inter font loaded via `@font-face` or Google Fonts and set as `fontFamily.sans` in config.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Zero runtime CSS-in-JS overhead — all styles resolved at build time via PostCSS, directly supporting the 150ms micro-interaction and 350ms transition performance constraints.
POSITIVE: Single source of truth for design tokens in tailwind.config.js — Inter font, 4px spacing unit, 16px/10px/20px/8px radii, and shadow values are defined once and reused everywhere.
POSITIVE: `motion-reduce:` variant natively handles prefers-reduced-motion without extra JS logic.
POSITIVE: No new runtime dependencies — only devDependencies (tailwindcss, postcss, autoprefixer).
NEGATIVE: Verbose JSX class strings for complex components (e.g., expense cards with hover, focus, and responsive states can exceed 15 utility classes per element).
NEGATIVE: Enforcing 36×36px minimum click targets requires discipline — no compile-time enforcement; must be caught in review.
NEGATIVE: Custom shadow values (`0 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,0.06)` and `0 8px 24px rgba(0,0,0,0.10)`) require manual `extend.boxShadow` entries; not auto-generated.
NEGATIVE: Purge/content config must cover all 7 screens and component files or styles will be stripped in production builds.
NEGATIVE: Adds 3 devDependencies: tailwindcss, postcss, autoprefixer.

alternatives.name: CSS Modules (Minimal Change, Zero Runtime)
alternatives.description: Use CSS Modules — locally scoped `.module.css` files co-located with each component. Design tokens are defined once in a global `tokens.css` file using CSS custom properties (e.g., `--radius-card: 16px`, `--shadow-elevated: 0 8px 24px rgba(0,0,0,0.10)`, `--color-primary: #2563eb`) and imported where needed. Animations and transitions are written in plain CSS with `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` blocks. Focus rings use `:focus-visible` pseudo-class. Responsive layouts use CSS Grid/Flexbox with `@media` queries at 768px, 1024px, and 1440px breakpoints. Supported natively by all modern bundlers (Vite, webpack, Next.js) with zero additional dependencies.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Zero additional dependencies — CSS Modules are supported natively by Vite, Next.js, and Create React App out of the box.
POSITIVE: Lowest blast radius of all alternatives — each component's styles are isolated; a change to ExpenseCard.module.css affects only that component.
POSITIVE: CSS custom properties in tokens.css provide a single source of truth for all design tokens; changes propagate instantly via the cascade.
POSITIVE: Zero runtime overhead — styles are statically extracted at build time, fully supporting all performance constraints.
POSITIVE: `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` is idiomatic CSS — no framework knowledge required.
NEGATIVE: Requires discipline to keep tokens.css as the canonical source; developers may hardcode values in module files, causing drift across 7 screens.
NEGATIVE: No utility classes — responsive variants (e.g., single-column at 768px) must be written per-component, leading to repetitive media query blocks across ~25 feature components.
NEGATIVE: Dynamic styles (e.g., category color chips driven by entity-002 Category data) require inline styles or CSS custom property injection, mixing styling concerns.
NEGATIVE: Refactoring shared patterns (e.g., card shadow applied to Dashboard, Add Expense, and Insights cards) requires updating multiple .module.css files rather than one config.
NEGATIVE: No built-in linting for token usage — a separate stylelint config would be needed to enforce token consumption.

alternatives.name: styled-components v6 (CSS-in-JS with Theme Provider)
alternatives.description: Use styled-components with a central `<ThemeProvider>` wrapping the app. The theme object encodes all design tokens: `theme.radii.card = '16px'`, `theme.shadows.elevated`, `theme.spacing.base = 4`, `theme.colors.primary = '#2563eb'`, etc. Each component (ExpenseCard, CategoryChip, BudgetBar, AmountInput) is a styled component consuming the theme. The `createGlobalStyle` helper sets Inter font and CSS resets. `prefers-reduced-motion` is handled via a `useReducedMotion` hook that conditionally passes animation props to styled components. Dynamic category colors (entity-002) are passed as props to styled components, eliminating inline style hacks.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: ThemeProvider is the strongest token enforcement mechanism — all 7 screens and 25 features consume tokens from one typed object, preventing drift.
POSITIVE: Dynamic styling for category color chips (entity-002) and budget status indicators is idiomatic — props-based interpolation avoids inline styles.
POSITIVE: TypeScript theme typing (DefaultTheme) provides compile-time enforcement of token usage across all components.
POSITIVE: Component-level encapsulation aligns with the card-based design language — each card variant is a discrete styled component.

NEGATIVE: Adds 1 production runtime dependency (styled-components ~12KB gzipped) — CSS is injected at runtime, adding ~2-5ms overhead per render cycle that could pressure the 150ms micro-interaction budget on low-end hardware.
NEGATIVE: Server-side rendering requires `ServerStyleSheet` or `StyleSheetManager` setup — relevant if Next.js is chosen as the framework.
NEGATIVE: styled-components v6 dropped IE11 support (acceptable per constraints) but introduced breaking changes from v5 — migration risk if the team has prior v5 experience.
NEGATIVE: Bundle size impact: styled-components adds ~12KB gzipped to the production bundle vs. 0KB for CSS Modules.
NEGATIVE: Debugging in DevTools shows generated class names (e.g., `sc-abc123`) unless Babel plugin is configured, adding 1 more devDependency.
NEGATIVE: Affects all component files across all 7 screens — highest blast radius for initial setup.

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS + CVA (Class Variance Authority) — Hybrid Token-Safe Approach
alternatives.description: Extend the Tailwind-only approach (alt-tailwind) with Class Variance Authority (CVA) to manage component variants systematically. CVA defines typed variant maps for recurring component patterns: e.g., `cva('rounded-[16px] shadow-card', { variants: { elevated: { true: 'shadow-elevated' } } })` for cards; `cva('h-9 min-w-[36px] rounded-[10px]', { variants: { intent: { primary: 'bg-blue-600', ghost: 'bg-transparent' } } })` for buttons. This enforces the 36×36px click target constraint at the component API level. All design tokens live in tailwind.config.js; CVA consumes them via class names. `tailwind-merge` (twMerge) resolves class conflicts when consumers override variants.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: CVA variant maps enforce the 36×36px minimum click target and focus ring standards at the component API level — misuse causes a TypeScript error, not a visual regression.
POSITIVE: Combines Tailwind's zero-runtime performance with structured, typed component APIs — best of both worlds for a design-system-conscious greenfield project.
POSITIVE: Adds only 2 small production dependencies: `cva` (~1.5KB) and `tailwind-merge` (~2KB) — negligible bundle impact vs. styled-components.
POSITIVE: Category color chips (entity-002) can use CVA's `compoundVariants` to map category slugs to Tailwind color classes, keeping dynamic styling declarative.
POSITIVE: Scales cleanly across all 25 features and 7 screens — new variants are additive, not refactors.
NEGATIVE: Adds 2 production dependencies (cva, tailwind-merge) and 3 devDependencies (tailwindcss, postcss, autoprefixer) — 5 total new packages.
NEGATIVE: CVA requires upfront investment in defining variant schemas for each component family; initial setup is slower than plain Tailwind.
NEGATIVE: Developers unfamiliar with CVA face a learning curve — the variant/compoundVariant mental model is non-obvious.

NEGATIVE: Tailwind class strings in CVA definitions are still not statically type-checked against the Tailwind config — invalid class names fail silently at build time (though Tailwind IntelliSense mitigates this in VS Code).
NEGATIVE: Blast radius is high for initial setup (all component files), though subsequent changes are localized to individual CVA definitions.

structured.rationale: Four alternatives were selected to cover the full spectrum from minimal-change to fully-structured: (1) plain Tailwind for teams prioritizing velocity and build-time performance, (2) CSS Modules as the zero-dependency baseline that minimizes blast radius, (3) styled-components for teams that want runtime dynamism and typed theme enforcement, and (4) Tailwind+CVA as a structured middle ground that addresses Tailwind's main weakness (unguarded variant sprawl) without the runtime cost of CSS-in-JS. Excluded alternatives: Sass/SCSS — adds a preprocessor dependency with no meaningful advantage over CSS custom properties for this token set, and lacks the component-scoping story needed for 25 features. Emotion — functionally equivalent to styled-components but with a smaller ecosystem and less community momentum for greenfield projects in 2024. Vanilla Extract — compelling for type-safe tokens but requires a Vite/webpack plugin and has a steeper learning curve disproportionate to a single-app project scope. Open Props — too opinionated on token naming to accommodate the specific CashPulse design token set (4px base, custom radii, specific shadow values).
structured.recommendation: alt-tailwind-cva — Tailwind CSS + CVA is the recommended approach for CashPulse. The greenfield context means there is no migration cost. The performance constraints (150ms micro-interactions, 350ms screen transitions) are best served by zero-runtime CSS resolved at build time. CVA directly addresses the WCAG 2.1 AA constraint by encoding the 36×36px click target and 2px focus ring requirements into typed component APIs that fail at compile time rather than in QA. The design token set (Inter, 4px spacing, specific radii and shadows) maps cleanly to tailwind.config.js `extend` entries. The 2 small production dependencies (cva + tailwind-merge, ~3.5KB combined) are a negligible tradeoff for the structural discipline they provide across 7 screens and 25 features.

alternatives.name: shadcn/ui (Radix UI primitives + Tailwind CSS)
alternatives.description: shadcn/ui is a collection of copy-paste, unstyled-by-default components built on Radix UI accessibility primitives and styled with Tailwind CSS utility classes. Components are owned by the project (not a node_modules dependency), making them fully customizable. Radix primitives provide battle-tested WCAG 2.1 AA keyboard navigation, focus management, and ARIA semantics out of the box. Tailwind's JIT compiler ensures minimal CSS bundle. Design tokens (Inter font, 4px spacing unit, border radii, shadows) map directly to Tailwind config values. No chart components included — a separate charting library (e.g., Recharts) is required.
tradeoffs: PROS: Zero runtime overhead — components compile to plain HTML/CSS with no JS abstraction layer, keeping micro-interactions well under 150ms
PROS: Radix Dialog, Popover, Select, and Tooltip primitives ship with full keyboard nav (Tab/Enter/Space/Escape) and focus trapping — satisfies all hard accessibility constraints with no extra work

PROS: Design tokens (Inter, 4px base, 10px button radius, 16px card radius, 20px badge radius, 8px input radius, exact box-shadow values) map 1:1 to tailwind.config.js — zero drift between spec and implementation
PROS: prefers-reduced-motion is handled at the Tailwind layer via motion-safe:/motion-reduce: variants — single config change disables all animations globally
PROS: Components are inlined into the codebase — no upstream breaking changes, full control over focus ring color (blue-600, 2px, 2px offset) and 36×36px minimum touch targets
CONS: Adds 2 dependencies: tailwindcss + @radix-ui/* (one package per primitive used, ~8–12 packages for this project's 7 screens)
CONS: No built-in chart components — requires pairing with Recharts or Visx (1 additional dependency, ~45KB gzipped)
CONS: Copy-paste model means component updates from upstream must be manually merged — maintenance overhead for a long-lived project
CONS: Tailwind class verbosity can make JSX noisy; requires team discipline or a class-merging utility (clsx + tailwind-merge, 2 small deps)

alternatives.name: Mantine v7
alternatives.description: Mantine is a fully-featured React component library with 100+ components, built-in form management (useForm), date pickers, number inputs with formatting, and a first-class theming system. It uses CSS Modules (no Tailwind dependency) and ships its own CSS custom properties for tokens. Mantine's NumberInput supports decimal formatting and min/max constraints natively — directly satisfying the amount input validation constraint ($99,999.99, 2dp, numeric-only). DatePicker supports maxDate constraints (no future dates). Mantine also includes a Notification system and Modal with full keyboard management.
tradeoffs: PROS: NumberInput with decimalScale=2, max=99999.99, and allowNegative=false satisfies the amount validation constraint with zero custom code
PROS: DatePickerInput with maxDate={new Date()} blocks future date selection natively
PROS: Built-in useForm hook with field-level validation reduces form boilerplate for the Add Expense screen
PROS: Theme object maps cleanly to CashPulse tokens: fontFamily='Inter', defaultRadius values, spacing scale, and shadow definitions all configurable in one MantineProvider
PROS: WCAG 2.1 AA compliant components with built-in focus management; focus ring color customizable via theme.focusRing and theme.focusRingStyles
CONS: Adds 1 large dependency: @mantine/core (~120KB gzipped) plus optional @mantine/dates, @mantine/form — total ~160KB gzipped, heavier than shadcn/ui approach

CONS: Overriding Mantine's default border radii, shadows, and focus ring styles to match CashPulse's exact spec requires careful theme configuration and CSS variable overrides — risk of visual drift if not audited
CONS: No built-in chart components — still requires Recharts or similar (1 additional dependency)
CONS: Mantine's animation defaults (transitions on Modal, Popover, Select) must be individually tuned or globally disabled for prefers-reduced-motion compliance — not automatic
CONS: Affects all 7 screens and all 25 features — any Mantine major version upgrade is a project-wide migration event (high coupling)

alternatives.name: Material UI (MUI) v5
alternatives.description: MUI is the most widely adopted React component library, offering a comprehensive suite of components with a mature accessibility story, robust theming via the sx prop and createTheme, and an extensive ecosystem. MUI's theme system supports custom typography (Inter), spacing multipliers (4px base), shape.borderRadius overrides, and shadow arrays. However, MUI's Material Design visual language (elevation shadows, ripple effects, default typography scale) conflicts significantly with CashPulse's sharp, structured financial design language — requiring substantial style overrides to achieve the specified card shadows, radii, and focus ring behavior.
tradeoffs: PROS: Largest ecosystem and community — highest likelihood of finding solutions to edge cases in form validation, date pickers, and data tables
PROS: MUI's TextField supports inputProps for numeric constraints; DatePicker (via @mui/x-date-pickers) supports maxDate
PROS: Emotion-based styling allows precise CSS-in-JS overrides for every token in the CashPulse design spec
PROS: Strong WCAG 2.1 AA baseline — focus management, ARIA roles, and keyboard navigation are well-tested across all components

CONS: Adds 3 dependencies: @mui/material, @emotion/react, @emotion/styled — total ~90KB gzipped core, but Emotion runtime adds JS overhead that can push micro-interaction timing toward the 150ms boundary

CONS: Material Design defaults (ripple animations, elevation shadows, Roboto font assumptions, rounded pill buttons) require 15–25 theme overrides to match CashPulse's exact visual spec — high risk of inconsistency
CONS: MUI's default focus ring (thin outline) must be globally overridden to achieve 2px blue-600 ring with 2px offset — requires GlobalStyles injection affecting all components
CONS: @mui/x-date-pickers is a separate package with its own release cadence — version mismatches are a recurring maintenance issue
CONS: Bundle size is the largest of all alternatives; tree-shaking helps but named imports must be enforced via ESLint rules to avoid regressions
CONS: No chart components — still requires Recharts or similar

alternatives.name: Custom Components on Radix UI Primitives (Minimal Change / No Framework)
alternatives.description: Build all UI components from scratch using only Radix UI headless primitives for complex interactive elements (Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip, Checkbox) and plain React + CSS Modules for everything else. No component library is adopted. This is the minimal-dependency alternative — it adds only the specific Radix primitives needed (approximately 6–8 packages) and uses CSS Modules for scoped styling. Design tokens are defined once in a tokens.css file and consumed everywhere. This approach gives maximum control over every pixel and interaction timing, at the cost of building and maintaining all component logic internally.
tradeoffs: PROS: Minimal blast radius — only the specific files that use a given primitive are affected by any change; no global library upgrade risk
PROS: Zero style override battles — every border radius, shadow, focus ring, and spacing value is authored directly to spec from day one
PROS: Smallest possible bundle — only ship code that is actually used; no tree-shaking configuration required
PROS: prefers-reduced-motion handled with a single CSS @media block in tokens.css — applies globally with no library-specific workarounds
PROS: Focus ring (2px blue-600, 2px offset) implemented once in a global :focus-visible rule — consistent across all components without overriding library defaults

CONS: Requires building ~15–20 components from scratch (Button, Input, Card, Badge, Chip, Select, Modal, Popover, DatePicker, Tooltip, Table, Tabs, ProgressBar, Skeleton, ConfirmDialog) — estimated 3–5 additional dev-days upfront
CONS: Amount input auto-formatting (decimal, numeric-only, $99,999.99 max) must be hand-implemented with an onKeyDown/onChange handler — no library shortcut
CONS: DatePicker with maxDate constraint requires either a custom calendar component or a lightweight standalone library (react-day-picker, 1 dep, ~8KB gzipped)
CONS: No Quick Add suggestion autocomplete out of the box — must implement combobox pattern manually (or use Radix Combobox/Downshift, 1 additional dep)

CONS: Accessibility correctness for complex patterns (combobox, date picker, modal focus trap) depends entirely on developer implementation quality — higher risk of WCAG gaps vs. battle-tested library components

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full decision space for a greenfield React application with strict design token fidelity and WCAG 2.1 AA hard constraints. MUI was included despite its design-language mismatch because it is the most commonly evaluated option and its tradeoffs need to be explicitly documented for the Architecture Writer to rule it out confidently. shadcn/ui and the custom Radix approach were both included because they share the same underlying primitive layer (Radix UI) but differ significantly in build-vs-buy tradeoff — shadcn/ui provides pre-styled starting points while the custom approach starts from zero. Mantine was included as the strongest 'batteries-included' alternative given its native NumberInput and DatePicker capabilities that directly address two of CashPulse's most specific constraints (amount validation, future date blocking). Excluded: Chakra UI (similar profile to Mantine but weaker TypeScript story and slower v3 adoption), Ant Design (enterprise aesthetic incompatible with CashPulse's sharp financial design language, large bundle), and Headless UI by Tailwind Labs (fewer primitives than Radix, no Combobox in stable release at time of writing).
structured.recommendation: alt-shadcn-ui — shadcn/ui on Radix UI primitives with Tailwind CSS is the strongest fit for CashPulse. The hard accessibility constraint (WCAG 2.1 AA, keyboard nav, focus rings) is fully satisfied by Radix primitives with zero custom ARIA work. The design token constraint (exact radii, shadows, Inter font, 4px spacing) maps 1:1 to tailwind.config.js with no override battles. The copy-paste ownership model means the 2px blue-600 focus ring and 36×36px touch targets are set once and never overridden by a library update. The only gap — charts — requires one additional dependency (Recharts) regardless of which alternative is chosen. The performance constraint (150ms micro-interactions, 350ms screen transitions) is easiest to guarantee with shadcn/ui's zero-runtime-overhead approach compared to Mantine's or MUI's JS animation layers.

alternatives.name: Dexie.js (IndexedDB wrapper) — Minimal Abstraction
alternatives.description: Use Dexie.js as a lightweight, promise-based IndexedDB wrapper. Define schemas for all 8 entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion) as Dexie tables with typed indexes. Dexie provides a clean query API (table.where().equals(), .orderBy(), .filter()) without a heavy ORM layer. All data lives in the browser's IndexedDB — no backend required, matching the scope-exclusion of backend API development. Computed entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are derived via Dexie live queries or on-demand aggregation functions.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: dexie (~25KB gzipped) — no other runtime deps required
No automatic relationship resolution — joins between Expense↔Category↔Budget must be written manually as async lookups
Computed entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) require hand-written aggregation logic (~3-5 utility functions)
IndexedDB storage limit is browser-managed (~50-80% of available disk); sufficient for personal expense data but not explicitly bounded
Schema migrations handled via Dexie version() API — straightforward but requires discipline as entities evolve
Excellent read performance for filtered/sorted expense lists; well within 150ms micro-interaction budget for typical datasets (<10,000 rows)
Affects: db/ schema file (1), entity repository files (8), aggregation utils (3-5) — blast radius ~12-14 files

alternatives.name: Dexie.js + Dexie Cloud / liveQuery — Reactive ORM Pattern
alternatives.description: Extend Dexie.js (alt-01) with Dexie's built-in liveQuery() observable API to create a reactive data layer. UI components subscribe to live queries that automatically re-render when underlying IndexedDB data changes — eliminating manual cache invalidation. This is the recommended pattern for React/Vue/Svelte frontends. All 8 entities are defined as Dexie tables; liveQuery wraps reads for Dashboard (recent expenses, budget status) and Insights (CategorySpending, DailySpending aggregations). Dexie Cloud is explicitly excluded since cloud sync is out of scope.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: dexie (~25KB gzipped) — liveQuery is included in core Dexie, no extra package
Requires framework integration adapter: useLiveQuery() hook for React, or dexie-react-hooks (~2KB) — adds 1 optional dependency
liveQuery re-runs entire query on any table mutation — aggregation queries over large datasets may re-compute more than necessary; mitigated by memoization
Reactive pattern adds ~2-3 custom hooks or composables per screen (Dashboard, Insights, AddExpense) — ~6-9 new files
Eliminates need for global state manager (Redux/Zustand) for server-state — reduces overall complexity
Aggregation for MonthSummary/CategorySpending still requires manual reduce logic but is co-located with liveQuery subscriptions
Affects: db/ schema (1), repository/query files (8), custom hooks (6-9), aggregation utils (3) — blast radius ~18-21 files

alternatives.name: RxDB — Full Reactive Document Database
alternatives.description: Use RxDB, a reactive NoSQL database for browsers built on top of IndexedDB (via PouchDB adapter). RxDB provides a full ORM-like experience: typed collections, schema validation (JSON Schema), reactive queries via RxJS observables, and built-in migration support. All 8 entities become RxDB collections with explicit JSON Schema definitions. Relationships (Expense→Category, Expense→Budget) are managed via population (similar to Mongoose populate). Computed entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) can be derived collections or query-time aggregations.
tradeoffs: Adds 2-3 dependencies: rxdb (~120KB gzipped), rxjs (~45KB gzipped), pouchdb-adapter-idb — significantly larger bundle than Dexie
JSON Schema validation on every write adds ~1-3ms overhead per insert — negligible for expense entry but worth monitoring
RxJS observable pattern requires team familiarity; steeper learning curve than Dexie's promise API
Built-in schema migration system is more robust than Dexie's version() — better for long-lived apps with evolving entities
Population (join-like) queries for Expense↔Category are declarative and reduce manual async lookup code
RxDB's reactive query subscriptions integrate cleanly with React via useRxQuery hook — similar DX to alt-02 but more structured
Overkill for a greenfield personal tracker with 8 entities and no sync requirement — most advanced features (replication, conflict resolution) are unused
Affects: db/ schema files (8 JSON schemas), collection setup (1), repository/query files (8), React hooks (6-9) — blast radius ~23-26 files

alternatives.name: Plain localStorage + Zustand — Zero-Dependency Minimal Store
alternatives.description: Forgo a dedicated ORM entirely. Use Zustand (or React Context + useReducer) as the in-memory state store with localStorage as the persistence layer via a custom serializer. All 8 entities are stored as JSON arrays/maps in localStorage keys (e.g., 'cashpulse_expenses', 'cashpulse_categories'). Computed entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are derived via selector functions (useMemo / Zustand selectors) at read time. A thin persistence middleware (zustand/middleware persist) handles serialization automatically.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: zustand (~3KB gzipped) — smallest possible footprint; persist middleware is bundled
localStorage is synchronous and limited to ~5MB per origin — sufficient for personal expense history (~2-3 years of daily entries) but not scalable beyond that
No query language — filtering/sorting expenses requires Array.filter/sort in JS; fine for <5,000 records but degrades beyond that
No schema validation or migration tooling — entity shape changes require manual data migration scripts or version-keyed localStorage keys
Computed entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending) recalculate on every relevant state change — must be memoized carefully to stay within 150ms interaction budget
Simplest possible implementation: no async, no IndexedDB, no workers — entire data layer is ~200-300 lines of TypeScript
Risk: localStorage is synchronous on the main thread; large reads/writes (full expense history) could cause jank during screen transitions
Affects: store/ files (3-4 Zustand slices), selector utils (5-6), localStorage key constants (1) — blast radius ~10 files

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-abstraction (localStorage + Zustand) to full reactive ORM (RxDB), all implementable in a greenfield browser-only app with no backend. Backend-based ORMs (Prisma, TypeORM, Drizzle) were excluded because the scope explicitly rules out backend API development — all data must live client-side. SQLite-in-browser (via sql.js or wa-sqlite) was excluded because WASM bundle overhead (~1MB+) conflicts with the 350ms screen transition budget and adds disproportionate complexity for 8 entities. Firebase/Supabase were excluded as they require backend infrastructure and cloud sync, which is out of scope. The four selected alternatives represent: (1) a pragmatic minimal-dep IndexedDB solution, (2) the same solution with a reactive layer for better UI integration, (3) a full-featured reactive document DB for teams wanting schema rigor, and (4) the absolute minimum viable data layer for a fast greenfield start.
structured.recommendation: alt-02 — Dexie.js with liveQuery() is the recommended choice. It satisfies all hard constraints (no backend, browser-only, WCAG-irrelevant at the data layer) and the critical soft constraint of 150ms micro-interactions: Dexie's IndexedDB queries on personal-scale datasets are consistently sub-10ms. The liveQuery reactive pattern eliminates manual cache invalidation, reducing bugs in the Dashboard and Insights screens where stale data would be most visible. It adds only 1-2 small dependencies (~27KB total), keeps the blast radius manageable (~18-21 files), and leaves room to grow without the complexity tax of RxDB. alt-04 (localStorage) is tempting for its simplicity but the synchronous I/O risk and 5MB cap make it a liability as the app matures. alt-03 (RxDB) is over-engineered for 8 entities with no sync requirement.

alternatives.name: In-Memory Store with Zustand (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: Use Zustand (or React Context + useReducer) as a client-side in-memory store to cache all 8 data entities for the lifetime of the browser session. All reads are synchronous from the store; writes update the store optimistically and persist to localStorage for cross-session durability. No external infrastructure required. On app boot, hydrate from localStorage; on mutation, write-through to localStorage. This is the minimal-change, zero-dependency-infrastructure option.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency (zustand ~3KB gzipped) if not already present; zero if using React Context
Data is lost on tab close unless localStorage write-through is implemented (adds ~30 lines of middleware)
localStorage is synchronous and blocks the main thread for large payloads — mitigated by keeping entity payloads small (8 entities, personal-scale data)
No cache invalidation complexity: single user, single tab, no concurrent writers
Blast radius: 3–5 files (store definition, 3 screen-level data hooks, 1 persistence middleware)
Covers all performance targets: synchronous reads guarantee sub-150ms micro-interactions and sub-350ms screen transitions
No TTL or stale-data logic needed — user controls all mutations explicitly

alternatives.name: React Query (TanStack Query) with In-Memory Cache + localStorage Persistence
alternatives.description: Adopt TanStack Query as the data-fetching and caching layer. Each entity type (Expense, Category, Budget, MonthSummary, etc.) maps to a query key. React Query's in-memory cache holds all fetched data with configurable staleTime (e.g., 5 minutes for MonthSummary/CategorySpending, Infinity for Category/UserSettings). The persistQueryClient plugin serializes the cache to localStorage on blur/unload, rehydrating on next boot. Optimistic updates via useMutation cover Add/Edit/Delete Expense flows. This is the most future-proof option if a real backend is ever introduced.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new dependencies: @tanstack/react-query (~13KB gzipped) and @tanstack/query-persist-client-core for localStorage persistence
Requires wrapping the app in QueryClientProvider — 1 file change at root
Overkill for a purely local, single-user greenfield app with no real API today; all 'fetches' are reads from localStorage, adding async indirection to what could be synchronous reads
staleTime and gcTime must be tuned per entity: Category/Budget can be Infinity; Expense list needs shorter staleTime if future sync is added
Blast radius: 6–9 files (QueryClientProvider root, 8 custom hooks — one per entity, persistence config)
Devtools (@tanstack/react-query-devtools) add excellent observability at zero production cost
