Positions the app well for a future backend API with zero refactor of component data-access patterns

alternatives.name: HTTP Cache Headers + Service Worker (Cache-First Strategy)
alternatives.description: Implement a Service Worker using Workbox that intercepts all data requests (even if they are local JSON files or a mock API) and applies a Cache-First strategy for static reference data (Category, UserSettings, Budget) and a Network-First / Stale-While-Revalidate strategy for dynamic data (Expense list, MonthSummary, DailySpending). The Service Worker cache acts as the persistence layer. Pairs with standard HTTP Cache-Control headers if a real API is introduced later.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new dependencies: workbox-webpack-plugin (or vite-plugin-pwa) and workbox-strategies
Service Workers only activate after first load — first-visit performance is unaffected; subsequent visits benefit from cache-first reads
Requires HTTPS in production (localhost is exempt); not a constraint for a desktop web app but worth noting
Cache invalidation for mutations (Add/Edit/Delete Expense) requires explicit cache.delete() calls in the SW or a versioned cache key strategy — adds ~50 lines of SW logic
Debugging Service Workers is significantly harder than in-memory stores; requires Chrome DevTools Application panel familiarity
Blast radius: 4–6 files (SW registration, Workbox config, 2–3 fetch handler definitions, manifest.json)
Overkill for a greenfield desktop-only app with no offline-first requirement in scope; best justified if PWA/offline is a future roadmap item
Does not directly accelerate micro-interaction performance (150ms target) since SW cache is still async I/O

alternatives.name: IndexedDB with Dexie.js (Structured Local Database)
alternatives.description: Use Dexie.js as an IndexedDB wrapper to store all 8 entities in a structured, queryable local database. Each entity maps to a Dexie table with typed indexes (e.g., Expense indexed by date, categoryId; CategorySpending indexed by month). Reads are async but fast (<5ms for indexed queries on personal-scale data). Mutations are transactional. This replaces both the API layer and the cache layer — the browser IS the database for this single-user app.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: dexie (~23KB gzipped)
All data access is async (Promise-based) — components must handle loading states even for local reads, adding UI complexity (skeleton states for all 7 screens)

Async reads introduce latency indirection: micro-interactions that depend on data (chip selections showing budget remaining) may not meet 150ms target without pre-loading data into React state on screen mount
Requires a schema migration strategy (Dexie versioning) from day one — adds ~20 lines of version management code
Blast radius: 8–12 files (Dexie db definition, 8 entity-specific repository modules, updates to all screen-level data hooks)
Significant storage capacity advantage over localStorage (GBs vs ~5MB) — irrelevant for personal expense tracking at typical usage volumes
Best justified if the app needs complex queries (e.g., 'expenses by category in date range') that would be expensive to compute in-memory; Dexie's liveQuery enables reactive UI updates

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-infrastructure to structured local database, all implementable without a backend (which is explicitly out of scope). Redis was excluded because it requires server-side infrastructure, violating the scope constraint. HTTP-only caching without a Service Worker was excluded because there is no real HTTP API in scope. The four selected alternatives cover: (1) the simplest possible synchronous in-memory approach, (2) the industry-standard async data-fetching cache that future-proofs for a backend, (3) a PWA/Service Worker approach for offline-first scenarios, and (4) a full structured local database. Each has a meaningfully different blast radius and complexity profile, giving the Architecture Writer a clear spectrum to choose from.
structured.recommendation: alt-1 — In-Memory Store with Zustand. CashPulse is a greenfield, single-user, desktop-only app with no backend in scope. The performance targets (150ms micro-interactions, 350ms screen transitions) are best served by synchronous in-memory reads, which Zustand provides natively. localStorage write-through covers cross-session persistence. The blast radius is the lowest of all alternatives (3–5 files), and the mental model is simple enough that any developer can reason about it without Service Worker or IndexedDB debugging overhead. If a real backend API is introduced in a future phase, migrating from Zustand to TanStack Query (alt-2) is a well-documented, incremental refactor — not a rewrite.

alternatives.name: Browser LocalStorage with In-Memory State (No External API)
alternatives.description: All data entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion) are persisted entirely in browser LocalStorage, serialized as JSON. A lightweight in-memory store (e.g., Zustand or React Context + useReducer) acts as the runtime layer, hydrated from LocalStorage on app load and flushed on every mutation. No backend, no payment provider, no network calls. Aggregations (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed client-side from the raw Expense array on demand or cached in LocalStorage after each write.
tradeoffs: Zero external dependencies added — no SDK, no API key, no network latency
All 8 data entities fit comfortably within the 5–10 MB LocalStorage quota for typical personal expense volumes (thousands of records)
Computed aggregates (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) must be recalculated or invalidated on every Expense write — adds ~1–3ms CPU cost client-side, negligible for <10k records
Data is device-local only — no cross-device sync (explicitly out of scope per constraints)
No authentication surface (explicitly out of scope)
Blast radius: affects only the data-access layer (~3–5 files: store, persistence adapter, entity hooks) — zero UI component changes required
Risk: LocalStorage is synchronous and blocks the main thread on large reads; mitigated by keeping serialized payload small and reading once on boot

alternatives.name: IndexedDB via Dexie.js (Structured Client-Side Database)
alternatives.description: Replace LocalStorage with IndexedDB accessed through the Dexie.js wrapper library. Each data entity maps to a Dexie table with typed schema and indexes (e.g., Expense indexed by date, categoryId; Budget indexed by categoryId). Queries for dashboard aggregations (MonthSummary, DailySpending) use Dexie's async query API with compound indexes, avoiding full-table scans. Dexie's liveQuery() enables reactive UI updates without manual cache invalidation. No backend or payment provider involved.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: dexie (~25 KB gzipped) — no other new dependencies
Async API (Promise/await) throughout the data layer — requires refactoring any synchronous LocalStorage reads (~5–8 files affected)
Supports storage of 100 MB+ of expense data with indexed queries — future-proof for power users
liveQuery() enables automatic re-render on data change, reducing boilerplate in dashboard and insights screens
IndexedDB is supported in all target evergreen browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) — no compatibility risk
Schema migrations handled by Dexie versioning — adds ~20 lines of migration boilerplate per schema change
Blast radius: data-access layer + async adapter refactor (~6–10 files: store, 8 entity hooks/queries, migration file)
Slightly higher initial complexity than LocalStorage but eliminates manual aggregation caching

alternatives.name: Mock REST API via MSW (Mock Service Worker) with LocalStorage Backing
alternatives.description: Define a RESTful API contract (e.g., GET /expenses, POST /expenses, GET /budgets, GET /insights/monthly) and implement it entirely in-browser using Mock Service Worker (MSW). MSW intercepts fetch/XHR calls at the Service Worker level and routes them to handler functions that read/write LocalStorage. The UI layer communicates only through fetch() calls — identical to how it would talk to a real backend. This decouples the UI from the storage implementation and makes the app backend-ready with zero UI changes when a real API is introduced.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new dependencies: msw (~50 KB gzipped in dev, tree-shaken in prod) and optionally a fetch client like ky or native fetch
Requires a service worker registration file (mockServiceWorker.js) in the public directory — 1 additional file
Network round-trip simulation adds ~0–5ms latency (loopback) — well within the 350ms screen transition and 150ms micro-interaction budgets
All 8 entity endpoints must be hand-authored as MSW handlers (~8–16 handler functions, ~200–400 lines)
Enables seamless swap to a real backend later: replace MSW handlers with real base URL — zero UI component changes
Loading states and error boundaries must be implemented in UI (adds ~3–5 UI files for suspense/error handling)
Blast radius: data-access layer + service worker setup + handler files (~10–15 files); UI components gain loading/error states
MSW is a dev/test tool by design — production bundle excludes worker overhead entirely

alternatives.name: JSON File Import/Export with SessionStorage Runtime Cache (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: The absolute minimal-change approach: all runtime state lives in React component state (or a single top-level Context), initialized from a bundled seed JSON file (defaultData.json) on first load. Users can export their data as a JSON file download and re-import it on next visit via a file picker. SessionStorage is used as a write-through cache to survive page refreshes within a session. No external libraries, no IndexedDB, no service worker. This is the smallest possible footprint for a greenfield desktop-only personal tracker.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies — uses only native browser APIs (File API, sessionStorage, JSON.stringify/parse)
Data does not persist across browser sessions without a manual export/import step — acceptable for a demo or MVP but poor UX for daily use
SessionStorage limit (~5 MB) is sufficient for typical expense volumes but lower than IndexedDB
No reactive query layer — aggregations (MonthSummary, CategorySpending) must be recomputed on every render or memoized with useMemo (~5–8 useMemo hooks across Insights and Dashboard screens)
Import/export UI requires 1 new settings screen component and 2 utility functions (~3 files)
Blast radius: 3–5 files total (context/store, seed data file, import-export utilities) — lowest of all alternatives
Not suitable as a long-term architecture if persistence or sync is ever added — would require full data-layer rewrite

structured.rationale: The gap is labeled 'payment-provider' but the project constraints make clear this is a frontend-only, desktop-web personal expense *tracker* — not a payment processing application. Backend API development, bank/card integration, and cloud sync are all explicitly out of scope. Therefore, the real architectural decision is: how should the 8 data entities be stored and accessed client-side? Four alternatives were selected spanning the full spectrum from zero-dependency in-memory state to a mock API contract. Excluded alternatives: (1) Real payment SDKs (Stripe, Braintree) — irrelevant, no payment processing occurs; (2) Real backend/BaaS (Supabase, Firebase) — explicitly out of scope (no backend API, no cloud storage, no auth); (3) SQLite via WASM — adds ~1.5 MB binary, disproportionate for this use case. The four chosen alternatives cover the realistic decision space for a greenfield, frontend-only, desktop-first tracker.
structured.recommendation: alt-indexeddb — Dexie.js over IndexedDB is the best fit for CashPulse's daily-use pattern. The QuickAddSuggestion entity requires frequency-ranked queries, CategorySpending and DailySpending need indexed date-range aggregations, and the Insights screen demands sub-600ms chart renders with potentially thousands of expense records. Dexie's liveQuery() eliminates manual cache invalidation across the Dashboard, Add Expense, and Insights screens, directly supporting the 350ms screen transition budget. The single added dependency (dexie, ~25 KB) is proportionate to the benefit. If the team wants the absolute minimum viable path for an MVP demo, alt-local-storage is the fallback.

alternatives.name: In-App Toast + Inline Validation Only (No External Channels)
alternatives.description: Deliver all user feedback exclusively through in-app UI primitives: transient toast notifications for action confirmations (expense saved, deleted, budget exceeded), inline field-level validation messages on the Add Expense form, and persistent inline status banners on the Dashboard for budget threshold warnings. No external delivery infrastructure (email, push, browser notifications) is introduced. Toasts are rendered in a fixed portal at the top-right of the viewport, auto-dismiss after 4 seconds, and include an undo affordance for destructive actions (aligned with the 3-second auto-revert safety constraint). All toasts are ARIA live regions (role='status' or role='alert') for screen reader compatibility.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies — implemented entirely with React state + a lightweight portal (already implied by the stack)
Fully satisfies the hard scope-exclusion constraint: push notifications and email alerts are explicitly out of scope
Blast radius is minimal: affects only a shared ToastProvider context, a ToastContainer component, and inline validation logic within AddExpenseForm (~3–5 files)
No backend infrastructure required — consistent with the 'visual and interaction design only' scope constraint
Toasts must respect prefers-reduced-motion: entry/exit animations must degrade to instant appear/disappear (requires media query check in animation logic)
Cannot reach the user when the app is not open — acceptable given scope exclusions
Requires careful z-index management to avoid conflicts with popovers, modals, and the category chip overlay
references: WCAG 2.1 SC 4.1.3 — Status Messages must be programmatically determinable via ARIA live regions
WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices: Alert and Status roles
React Portal API for overlay rendering outside the DOM tree

alternatives.name: Web Notifications API (Browser Push) for Budget Threshold Alerts
alternatives.description: Use the browser's native Notification API to deliver budget threshold alerts even when the tab is backgrounded. On first budget-approaching event (e.g., >80% of monthly budget consumed), the app requests notification permission. Approved users receive OS-level notifications with the budget category name and remaining amount. All in-app toasts from alt-toast-inline are retained as the primary channel; browser notifications serve as a supplementary out-of-focus channel. No server-side push infrastructure (no service worker push, no VAPID keys, no backend) — notifications are fired directly from the client JS when the app is open and a threshold is crossed.
tradeoffs: Explicitly excluded by the hard scope-exclusion constraint: 'Push notifications or email alerts for budget thresholds' are out of scope — this alternative VIOLATES a scope constraint and should only be considered if scope is revised
Adds 0 npm dependencies (native browser API), but requires a permission-request UX flow (~2 new components: PermissionPrompt, NotificationService module)
Browser support is broad (Chrome, Firefox, Edge) but Safari requires macOS 13+ and has historically been inconsistent — conflicts with the browser compatibility soft constraint
Notification permission prompts are intrusive if shown too early; UX best practice requires contextual triggering, adding design complexity
No service worker required for this client-only variant, but without one, notifications only fire while the tab is open — limiting utility
Blast radius: affects BudgetService logic, a new NotificationService module, UserSettings entity (store permission state), and a PermissionPrompt UI component (~5–7 files)
references: Scope exclusion constraint: 'Push notifications or email alerts for budget thresholds'
Safari Web Push support matrix (Apple Developer Documentation)
UX pattern: Permission request timing — Google Web Fundamentals

alternatives.name: Internal Event Bus with Pluggable Notification Adapters
alternatives.description: Implement a lightweight client-side event bus (publish/subscribe pattern) that decouples notification triggers from notification delivery. Domain events (ExpenseAdded, BudgetThresholdCrossed, ExpenseDeleted) are published by data mutation hooks. A NotificationOrchestrator subscribes to these events and routes them to registered adapter implementations. In the initial greenfield build, only two adapters are registered: ToastAdapter (renders in-app toasts, identical to alt-toast-inline) and ConsoleAdapter (dev-only logging). The architecture is designed so future adapters (email, push) can be plugged in without modifying trigger sites. The event bus is a plain JS EventEmitter pattern — no external library required.
tradeoffs: Adds architectural indirection: 1 EventBus module, 1 NotificationOrchestrator, 2 adapter files, and event type definitions (~6–9 files) vs. ~3–5 files for the minimal toast-only approach
No new npm dependencies if implemented as a plain EventEmitter; alternatively, mitt (0.2kb) could be added for a typed, tree-shakeable bus — adds 1 dependency
Future-proofs the notification layer for scope expansion without modifying existing trigger code — high long-term value for a greenfield project
Slight over-engineering risk for a single-user desktop app with explicit scope exclusions on external notification channels
Blast radius is medium: the event bus touches every data mutation hook (useAddExpense, useDeleteExpense, useBudgetCheck) plus the new orchestration layer (~8–12 files)
Fully compliant with all in-scope constraints — no external channels are activated in the initial build
Testing is simplified: adapters can be swapped for mock adapters in unit tests without mocking UI components
references: Node.js EventEmitter pattern adapted for browser environments

alternatives.name: React Context Notification System with Typed Severity Levels
alternatives.description: Implement a self-contained notification system using React Context + useReducer. A NotificationContext provides dispatch and notifications state globally. Components dispatch typed notification actions: { type: 'EXPENSE_SAVED' | 'BUDGET_WARNING' | 'DELETE_CONFIRMED' | 'VALIDATION_ERROR', severity: 'info' | 'warning' | 'error' | 'success' }. A NotificationRenderer component consumes the context and maps severity to visual treatment (toast style, icon, ARIA role). Budget warnings render as persistent banners on the Dashboard; transient confirmations render as auto-dismissing toasts. Inline validation errors render directly within the AddExpenseForm via the same context. This is a step above the minimal toast approach but avoids the full event-bus abstraction.
tradeoffs: Adds 0 new npm dependencies — pure React patterns
Blast radius is low-to-medium: NotificationContext, NotificationReducer, NotificationRenderer, and updates to AddExpenseForm, Dashboard, and mutation hooks (~6–8 files)
Typed severity levels enforce consistent visual treatment across all 7 screens without ad-hoc styling decisions
useReducer pattern makes notification state predictable and easy to test with React Testing Library

Context re-renders: if NotificationContext is placed at the app root, any notification dispatch triggers a re-render of all consumers — mitigated by splitting context into state and dispatch contexts (a standard optimization)
Slightly more boilerplate than a simple toast utility function, but significantly more maintainable as the feature set grows to 25 features
Fully satisfies WCAG 4.1.3 via ARIA live regions on the NotificationRenderer; severity maps cleanly to role='alert' (error) vs role='status' (info/success)
prefers-reduced-motion compliance is centralized in NotificationRenderer — one place to handle animation degradation
references: React Context + useReducer pattern (React official docs)
Kent C. Dodds — Application State Management with React (blog)

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full design space available within the project's hard constraints. The scope-exclusion constraints explicitly rule out push notifications and email alerts, which eliminates all server-side notification infrastructure (WebSockets, FCM, SMTP, service worker push) from consideration — those were excluded before this memo was written. The remaining solution space is entirely client-side. The four alternatives represent a clear progression: (1) minimal toast-only with no abstraction, (2) browser Notification API (included as a cautionary alternative that violates scope — useful for the Architecture Writer to explicitly document the rejection), (3) an event-bus architecture that maximizes future extensibility at the cost of upfront complexity, and (4) a React Context system that balances structure with pragmatism for a greenfield single-user app. Alternatives based on third-party notification services (Novu, Knock, Courier) were excluded because they require backend integration, violating the 'visual and interaction design only' scope constraint. WebSocket-based real-time notifications were excluded for the same reason.
structured.recommendation: alt-react-context-notification — The React Context + useReducer notification system is the best fit for CashPulse's greenfield context. It provides typed severity levels that map cleanly to the 7-screen information architecture, centralizes WCAG 4.1.3 compliance and prefers-reduced-motion handling in a single renderer, adds zero dependencies, and keeps blast radius low. It is meaningfully more structured than the bare toast approach (alt-toast-inline) without the over-engineering risk of a full event bus (alt-event-bus-notification) for a single-user desktop app with 25 features. The split context optimization (separate state and dispatch contexts) prevents unnecessary re-renders and keeps micro-interaction performance within the 150ms constraint.

alternatives.name: Client-Side In-Memory Filtering (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: All expense records are loaded into memory on app initialization (or per-screen mount) and filtered entirely on the client using JavaScript array methods. A single `useExpenseFilter` hook encapsulates filter logic for amount range, category, date range, and keyword. No additional API endpoints are required — the existing data-fetch for the Dashboard and Insights screens already retrieves Expense records. Filtering state lives in a Zustand/Context store slice and triggers synchronous re-renders. Debounced text input (150ms) keeps micro-interactions within the 150ms soft constraint.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies — uses native Array.prototype.filter/sort already available
Synchronous filtering guarantees sub-150ms micro-interaction response for datasets up to ~2,000 expense records (typical personal tracker volume)
Entire dataset must be resident in memory; degrades gracefully only up to ~5,000 records before noticeable jank on low-end hardware
No pagination complexity — simplifies Dashboard and Insights list components
Adds 1 new hook file (useExpenseFilter.ts) and 1 store slice (~80 LOC); blast radius is contained to filter UI components and list renderers
Re-fetching on every session start increases initial load time slightly (~50–200ms depending on record count)
Cannot support server-side aggregations for Insights charts — MonthSummary and CategorySpending entities must be derived client-side, adding ~2 utility functions

alternatives.name: Structured Query Parameters with Local Storage Persistence
alternatives.description: Filter state (category[], dateRange, amountMin, amountMax, keyword) is serialized into URL query parameters using a router library (e.g., React Router v6 `useSearchParams`). On each filter change, the app re-queries a local data layer (IndexedDB via Dexie.js or a mock REST layer) using the structured params. This enables shareable/bookmarkable filter URLs, browser back/forward navigation through filter states, and persistent filter sessions across page refreshes. The local data layer acts as the 'API', keeping the project within the scope-exclusion of no backend API development.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: Dexie.js (~25KB gzipped) for structured IndexedDB queries with index support on category, date, and amount fields
Enables indexed queries — O(log n) lookups vs O(n) scan, meaningful for 1,000+ records
URL-based filter state requires migrating Dashboard and Insights screens to use `useSearchParams` — affects ~3 screen components and ~2 layout components
Dexie schema migration must be versioned; any Expense entity schema change (entity-001) requires a migration script, adding ongoing maintenance overhead
Browser back button navigates through filter states, which may surprise users expecting it to navigate between screens — requires careful history management
Increases initial setup complexity: IndexedDB population on first load, schema definition, and seed data logic adds ~150 LOC across 2–3 new files
Full WCAG 2.1 AA keyboard navigation for filter chips and dropdowns must be explicitly wired to URL state updates — no additional accessibility cost but requires careful event handling

alternatives.name: Fuse.js Fuzzy Search + Structured Filter Composition
alternatives.description: Combines fuzzy full-text search (via Fuse.js) for the keyword/description field with structured predicate filters for category, date range, and amount. Fuse.js indexes the Expense `description` and `merchant` fields on data load. Structured filters (category chips, date picker, amount slider) are applied as post-processing predicates on the Fuse.js result set. This gives users a Google-like search experience for expense descriptions while maintaining precise structured filtering for financial data. The filter pipeline is: raw data → Fuse.js fuzzy match → predicate chain → sorted result. All processing is client-side.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: Fuse.js (~8KB gzipped) — lightweight but an additional bundle cost

Fuzzy search dramatically improves UX for misspelled merchant names or partial descriptions (e.g., 'Starbck' matches 'Starbucks') — high usability value for the Add Expense Quick Add suggestions (entity-008)
Fuse.js index must be rebuilt when new expenses are added — requires cache invalidation logic in the expense mutation handlers (~2 files affected)
Fuzzy matching can surface unexpected results; requires tuning of threshold (0.3–0.4 recommended) and key weights to avoid confusing financial users who expect exact matches on amounts
Fuse.js does not support numeric range queries natively — amount and date range filters must still be implemented as separate predicate functions (~30 LOC)
Result ranking by Fuse.js score may conflict with user expectation of chronological ordering — requires explicit sort override after fuzzy filter
Blast radius: 1 new search service file, updates to 2 list components (Dashboard expense list, Insights breakdown), and 1 filter state hook — moderate but well-contained

alternatives.name: React Query + Mock Service Worker (MSW) Simulated API
alternatives.description: Implements a fully structured search API contract using Mock Service Worker (MSW) to intercept fetch requests and return filtered data from an in-memory store. React Query manages caching, background refetching, and loading states. Filter parameters are passed as query strings to a mock `/api/expenses?category=&dateFrom=&dateTo=&q=` endpoint. This approach mirrors a real backend API contract exactly, making future backend integration a drop-in replacement (swap MSW handlers for real endpoints). Ideal for a greenfield project where backend may be added later.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new dependencies: @tanstack/react-query (~13KB gzipped) and msw (~30KB, dev-only) — largest bundle impact of all alternatives
MSW is dev/test only and tree-shaken from production builds — production bundle only includes React Query overhead
React Query's staleTime and cacheTime configuration enables instant UI responses from cache while background refetches run — directly supports the 150ms micro-interaction constraint
Establishes a real API contract (request/response shape) that a future backend must honor — reduces future migration risk significantly for a greenfield project
MSW setup requires a service worker registration file (mockServiceWorker.js) in the public directory and handler definitions (~100 LOC) — affects build pipeline and public asset management
React Query's devtools add debugging overhead during development but are excluded from production
Highest blast radius: affects all 7 screens (each needs QueryClientProvider context), all 8 data entity fetch sites, and introduces a new /mocks directory — significant upfront investment
Pagination and cursor-based loading become trivially implementable via useInfiniteQuery — future-proofs the Insights screen for large datasets

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency minimal change to full API-contract simulation, covering the realistic decision space for a greenfield, desktop-first, client-only personal expense tracker. Backend API development is explicitly out of scope (hard scope-exclusion constraint), which eliminates server-side search solutions (Elasticsearch, PostgreSQL full-text search, Algolia) entirely — those were excluded. A pure regex-based search was excluded as it offers no meaningful advantage over Array.filter for this dataset size and adds developer ergonomics cost. The four selected alternatives represent: (1) the simplest possible implementation, (2) a URL-state + structured storage upgrade, (3) a UX-enhancing fuzzy search layer, and (4) a future-proof API-contract approach. Each is implementable with a standard React + TypeScript stack with no backend requirement.
structured.recommendation: alt-search-01 (Client-Side In-Memory Filtering) is the recommended starting point for CashPulse. The scope is explicitly a personal expense tracker with no backend, no multi-user support, and no cloud sync — meaning realistic dataset sizes will comfortably stay under 2,000 records per user session. In-memory filtering is synchronous, guarantees the 150ms micro-interaction constraint without any async complexity, requires zero new dependencies, and keeps blast radius at its lowest. The single `useExpenseFilter` hook can be progressively enhanced with Fuse.js (alt-search-03) later if user research reveals merchant name search is a pain point. The MSW approach (alt-search-04) is compelling for future-proofing but its upfront cost is disproportionate for a scoped greenfield UI project with no imminent backend plans.

adrs.title: Client-Side Data Persistence via IndexedDB (Dexie.js)
adrs.decision: Use Dexie.js as the IndexedDB wrapper for all 8 data entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion). Dexie tables with typed indexes on date, categoryId, and month fields. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via Dexie liveQuery subscriptions that reactively update the UI on any Expense mutation. No backend database — all data lives in the browser.
adrs.rationale: CashPulse is a single-user, desktop-only, no-auth, no-sync personal expense tracker. Backend API development, cloud storage, and multi-device sync are all explicitly out of scope. Dexie.js (alt-2 for gap-data-store, alt-02 for gap-orm-strategy, alt-indexeddb for gap-payment-provider) is the convergent choice across three independent gap analyses. It provides: (1) indexed queries for date-range and category filtering needed by Dashboard and Insights screens — O(log n) vs O(n) full scan; (2) liveQuery() reactive subscriptions that eliminate manual cache invalidation across 3 screens; (3) async API that keeps the main thread free for animations within the 150ms/350ms performance budgets; (4) storage capacity far exceeding localStorage's 5MB limit; (5) built-in schema versioning for future entity evolution. The single dependency (~25KB gzipped) is proportionate to the benefit. localStorage+Zustand (alt-1/alt-04) was rejected because synchronous main-thread I/O risks jank during screen transitions and the 5MB cap is a liability. sql.js WASM (alt-3) was rejected for its ~1.5MB bundle violating the 350ms first-load budget. Supabase (alt-4) was rejected for violating scope exclusions on cloud storage and requiring a paid plan for production reliability.
alternatives: localStorage + Zustand in-memory state — simpler but synchronous I/O risk and 5MB cap
sql.js (SQLite WASM) — full SQL power but ~1.5MB bundle, WASM init latency
Supabase PostgreSQL — violates no-cloud-storage scope exclusion, free tier pauses after inactivity
RxDB — full reactive document DB but ~165KB bundle, overkill for 8 entities with no sync

adrs.title: Styling via Tailwind CSS + Class Variance Authority (CVA)
adrs.decision: Use Tailwind CSS v3 as the utility-first styling layer with Class Variance Authority (CVA) for typed component variant management. All design tokens (Inter font, 4px spacing base, 16px/10px/20px/8px radii, card/elevated shadows, color palette) are codified in tailwind.config.js extend entries. CVA defines variant schemas for recurring component patterns (cards, buttons, chips, badges, inputs) that encode accessibility constraints (36×36px min click targets, 2px blue-600 focus rings) at the component API level. tailwind-merge resolves class conflicts when consumers override variants. prefers-reduced-motion is handled via Tailwind's motion-safe:/motion-reduce: variants.
adrs.rationale: Greenfield context eliminates migration cost. Zero-runtime CSS (build-time PostCSS) directly supports the 150ms micro-interaction and 350ms screen transition performance constraints — no JS overhead per render cycle. CVA addresses Tailwind's main weakness (unguarded variant sprawl) by encoding the WCAG 2.1 AA hard constraint (36×36px targets, focus rings) into typed component APIs that fail at compile time. The CashPulse design token set (Inter, 4px base, specific radii/shadows) maps 1:1 to tailwind.config.js extend entries. Combined production bundle of cva (~1.5KB) + tailwind-merge (~2KB) is negligible vs styled-components (~12KB runtime). CSS Modules (alt-css-modules) was rejected because repetitive media query blocks across 25 features and no compile-time token enforcement increase drift risk. styled-components (alt-styled-components) was rejected for its ~12KB runtime overhead and generated class name debugging friction.
alternatives: Plain Tailwind CSS — simpler but no typed variant enforcement for accessibility constraints
CSS Modules — zero dependencies but repetitive media queries and no token enforcement
styled-components v6 — strong theme typing but 12KB runtime overhead pressures 150ms budget

adrs.title: Component Library: shadcn/ui on Radix UI Primitives
adrs.decision: Use shadcn/ui (copy-paste components built on Radix UI accessibility primitives, styled with Tailwind CSS) as the component foundation. Radix primitives provide Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip, and Tabs with built-in WCAG 2.1 AA keyboard navigation, focus trapping, and ARIA semantics. Components are inlined into the codebase (not a node_modules dependency), giving full control over focus ring styling, click target sizing, and animation behavior. Recharts is added as a separate dependency for the Insights screen charts (donut, bar, line).
adrs.rationale: The hard accessibility constraint (WCAG 2.1 AA, full keyboard nav, focus rings, ARIA roles) is the primary driver. Radix primitives satisfy this with zero custom ARIA work for the 5 complex interactive patterns in CashPulse: Settings Dialog (modal focus trap), Expense Detail Popover, Category Filter Select, Date Picker, and Navigation Tabs. The copy-paste ownership model means the exact 2px blue-600 focus ring with 2px offset and 36×36px touch targets are set once and never overridden by upstream library updates. Design tokens map 1:1 to tailwind.config.js — no override battles. Mantine (alt-mantine) was rejected despite its excellent NumberInput and DatePicker because its ~160KB bundle and animation defaults require per-component tuning for prefers-reduced-motion compliance. MUI (alt-mui) was rejected because Material Design defaults require 15–25 theme overrides to match CashPulse's visual spec, and Emotion runtime adds JS overhead. Custom Radix-only (alt-custom-headless) was rejected because building ~15 components from scratch adds 3–5 dev-days with higher WCAG gap risk for complex patterns like date picker.
alternatives: Mantine v7 — batteries-included but ~160KB bundle and animation override burden
Material UI v5 — largest ecosystem but Material Design conflicts with CashPulse visual language
Custom components on Radix primitives — maximum control but 3-5 extra dev-days and higher a11y risk

adrs.title: Caching Strategy: Zustand In-Memory Store with Dexie Persistence
adrs.decision: Use Zustand as a lightweight in-memory cache layer that sits above Dexie.js (the persistence layer). Zustand store slices hold the working data for each screen (dashboard summary, expense list, insights aggregations). On app boot, Zustand hydrates from Dexie. On mutations, Zustand updates optimistically for instant UI response, then writes through to Dexie. Dexie liveQuery subscriptions keep Zustand slices in sync when background computations complete. No external caching infrastructure (Redis, Service Worker cache, React Query).
adrs.rationale: CashPulse is a single-user, single-tab desktop app with no backend. The 150ms micro-interaction constraint demands synchronous reads for UI-critical paths (chip selections showing budget remaining, filter changes). Zustand provides synchronous reads from its in-memory store (~3KB gzipped, zero config). Dexie handles durable persistence and indexed queries. This two-layer approach gives the best of both: instant UI reads from Zustand + structured async queries from Dexie for complex aggregations. React Query (alt-2) was rejected as overkill — all 'fetches' are local reads, and the async indirection adds unnecessary loading state complexity. Service Worker cache (alt-3) was rejected as there is no offline-first requirement in scope. Pure Dexie without Zustand (alt-4) was rejected because async reads for every micro-interaction would require skeleton states on all 7 screens.
alternatives: React Query with localStorage persistence — future-proof but overkill for local-only data
Service Worker cache-first strategy — no offline requirement in scope
Dexie-only without in-memory cache — async reads pressure 150ms micro-interaction budget

adrs.title: Notification Channel: React Context Notification System
adrs.decision: Implement all user feedback via a React Context + useReducer notification system with typed severity levels (info, warning, error, success). Transient toasts for action confirmations (expense saved, currency changed, CSV exported). Inline validation messages on the Add Expense form. Persistent status banners on Dashboard for budget threshold warnings. No external notification channels (push, email, browser Notification API). All toasts are ARIA live regions (role='status' for info/success, role='alert' for error/warning). prefers-reduced-motion compliance centralized in the NotificationRenderer component.
adrs.rationale: Push notifications and email alerts are explicitly out of scope. The PRD specifies exactly three notification patterns: (1) toast for currency change (2s auto-dismiss), (2) inline delete confirmation with 3s auto-revert, (3) budget status badges. A React Context system with typed severities maps cleanly to these patterns without over-engineering. Zero new dependencies. The split context optimization (separate state and dispatch contexts) prevents unnecessary re-renders. Browser Notification API (alt-browser-notification-api) was rejected as it explicitly violates the scope exclusion on push notifications. Event bus (alt-event-bus-notification) was rejected as architectural indirection disproportionate to a single-user app with 3 notification patterns. Bare toast utility (alt-toast-inline) was rejected as it lacks the typed severity structure needed for consistent visual treatment across 7 screens.
alternatives: In-app toast only — too unstructured for consistent treatment across 7 screens
Browser Notification API — violates scope exclusion on push notifications
Event bus with pluggable adapters — over-engineered for 3 notification patterns

adrs.title: Search and Filtering: Client-Side In-Memory Filtering
adrs.decision: Implement all search and filtering via client-side JavaScript array methods. A single useExpenseFilter hook encapsulates filter logic for category, date range, amount range, and keyword matching. Filter state lives in a Zustand store slice. Text input is debounced at 150ms. No external search library, no API endpoints, no URL-based filter state. The Dashboard category filter dropdown and Insights breakdowns consume the same filter pipeline.
adrs.rationale: CashPulse is a personal tracker where realistic dataset sizes stay under 2,000 records. Synchronous Array.filter/sort on this volume completes in <1ms, well within the 150ms micro-interaction constraint. Zero new dependencies. Blast radius is minimal (1 hook + 1 store slice). Fuse.js fuzzy search (alt-search-03) was rejected because financial users expect exact matches on amounts and categories — fuzzy results on monetary data would be confusing. URL-based filter state (alt-search-02) was rejected because browser back button navigating through filter states conflicts with screen navigation expectations. MSW mock API (alt-search-04) was rejected as disproportionate upfront cost for a scoped UI project with no imminent backend.
alternatives: Fuse.js fuzzy search — fuzzy matching inappropriate for financial data precision
URL query params with Dexie — back button conflicts with screen navigation
React Query + MSW mock API — disproportionate cost for no-backend scope

decisions.rationale: Dexie.js over IndexedDB provides indexed queries for the Insights screen's date-range and category aggregations (O(log n) vs O(n)), liveQuery() reactive subscriptions that eliminate manual cache invalidation across Dashboard/Insights/AddExpense screens, and async API that keeps the main thread free for animations. The ~25KB dependency is proportionate. localStorage (alt-1) risks main-thread jank and 5MB cap. sql.js (alt-3) adds ~1.5MB WASM bundle. Supabase (alt-4) violates scope exclusions.

Zero-runtime CSS resolved at build time meets 150ms/350ms performance constraints. CVA encodes WCAG 2.1 AA requirements (36×36px targets, focus rings) into typed component APIs. CashPulse design tokens map 1:1 to tailwind.config.js. Combined cva+tailwind-merge bundle (~3.5KB) is negligible vs styled-components runtime (~12KB). CSS Modules lack token enforcement; styled-components adds runtime overhead.

Radix UI primitives satisfy the hard WCAG 2.1 AA constraint (keyboard nav, focus trapping, ARIA) for all 5 complex interactive patterns (Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip, Tabs) with zero custom ARIA work. Copy-paste ownership model gives full control over focus ring and click target styling. Design tokens map 1:1 to Tailwind config. Mantine's ~160KB bundle and MUI's Material Design override burden are disproportionate. Recharts added separately for charts.

Dexie.js with liveQuery() is the reactive data access layer. It aligns with the gap-data-store decision (same library), provides useLiveQuery() React hooks for automatic re-renders on data changes, and eliminates the need for a separate global state manager for persistent data. Aggregation queries for MonthSummary/CategorySpending are co-located with liveQuery subscriptions. Plain Dexie without liveQuery (alt-01) requires manual re-fetch logic. RxDB (alt-03) adds ~165KB for unused sync features. localStorage+Zustand (alt-04) has synchronous I/O risk.

Zustand in-memory store provides synchronous reads for UI-critical micro-interactions (150ms budget). Combined with Dexie.js persistence (from gap-data-store decision), this creates a two-layer architecture: instant reads from Zustand + durable indexed storage in Dexie. React Query (alt-2) adds async indirection to what are local reads. Service Worker cache (alt-3) has no offline requirement. Pure IndexedDB (alt-4) forces async reads for every interaction.

This gap is mislabeled — CashPulse has no payment processing. The real decision is client-side data storage, which converges with gap-data-store: Dexie.js over IndexedDB. liveQuery() supports the QuickAddSuggestion frequency-ranked queries and CategorySpending aggregations needed by Dashboard and Insights screens. localStorage (alt-local-storage) has 5MB cap and synchronous I/O risk. MSW mock API (alt-mock-rest-api) is over-engineered for no-backend scope. JSON import/export (alt-json-file-import-export) provides poor daily-use UX.

React Context + useReducer provides typed severity levels (info/warning/error/success) that map to CashPulse's 3 notification patterns: toasts (currency change, CSV export), inline confirmations (delete with 3s revert), and status banners (budget warnings). Zero dependencies. Centralized WCAG 4.1.3 compliance via ARIA live regions and prefers-reduced-motion handling. Browser Notification API (alt-browser-notification-api) violates scope exclusion. Event bus (alt-event-bus-notification) is over-engineered for 3 patterns.

Client-side Array.filter/sort on <2,000 records completes in <1ms — well within 150ms constraint. Zero dependencies. Single useExpenseFilter hook with debounced text input. Financial data demands exact matching, not fuzzy search (alt-search-03). URL filter state (alt-search-02) conflicts with screen navigation. MSW mock API (alt-search-04) is disproportionate for no-backend scope.

implementationPatterns.title: Dexie.js liveQuery for all persistent data access
implementationPatterns.rule: All reads from IndexedDB MUST use Dexie's useLiveQuery() hook in React components. Define one db.ts file exporting a typed Dexie subclass with tables for all 8 entities. Each entity gets a dedicated repository module (e.g., expenseRepository.ts) exporting query functions that return Dexie Collection objects. Screen components consume data exclusively via useLiveQuery(() => repository.queryFn(params), [params]). Write operations use Dexie transactions (db.transaction('rw', ...)) and return Promises — the liveQuery subscription automatically triggers re-renders.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-store and gap-orm-strategy decisions (ADR-001). Centralizes all IndexedDB access through Dexie's reactive layer, eliminating manual cache invalidation and ensuring Dashboard, Add Expense, and Insights screens stay in sync after mutations.
forbids: Direct IndexedDB API calls (window.indexedDB)
localStorage for entity persistence (use only for non-entity settings like UI preferences)
Synchronous data reads from IndexedDB in render paths

implementationPatterns.title: Tailwind utility classes via design tokens only — no arbitrary values
implementationPatterns.rule: All styling MUST use Tailwind utility classes that reference tokens defined in tailwind.config.js. The config MUST extend the default theme with CashPulse design tokens: fontFamily.sans = ['Inter', ...defaultTheme.fontFamily.sans], spacing in 4px multiples, borderRadius (card: 16px, button: 10px, badge: 20px, input: 8px), boxShadow (card: '0 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,0.06)', elevated: '0 8px 24px rgba(0,0,0,0.10)'), and the full color palette from the PRD. Use Tailwind's motion-safe: and motion-reduce: variants for all animations. Never use arbitrary value syntax (e.g., `w-[347px]`) except for one-off layout constraints explicitly specified in the PRD (e.g., max-w-[520px] for Add Expense form).
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-styling-approach decision (ADR-002). Ensures all 7 screens and 25 features consume the same token set, preventing visual drift. Build-time CSS resolution meets 150ms/350ms performance constraints.
forbids: Inline style attributes for token-expressible values (colors, spacing, radii, shadows)
CSS-in-JS runtime libraries (styled-components, Emotion)
Hardcoded hex colors or pixel values in className strings — use token aliases

implementationPatterns.title: CVA variant schemas for all reusable component families
implementationPatterns.rule: Every reusable component family (Button, Card, Chip, Badge, Input, IconCircle) MUST define its variants using Class Variance Authority (cva). Variant schemas MUST encode accessibility constraints: all interactive variants include min-h-9 min-w-9 (36px) for click targets and focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-primary-600 focus-visible:ring-offset-2 for focus rings. Use tailwind-merge (twMerge) to resolve class conflicts when consumers pass additional className props. Category-color variants use CVA compoundVariants to map category slugs to Tailwind color classes.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-styling-approach decision (ADR-002) and gap-component-library decision (ADR-003). CVA provides compile-time enforcement of WCAG 2.1 AA constraints at the component API level, preventing accessibility regressions across 25 features.
forbids: Ad-hoc className construction without CVA for components used in more than one screen
Omitting focus-visible ring classes from interactive component variants

implementationPatterns.title: Typed notification context for all user-facing feedback
implementationPatterns.rule: All user-facing feedback (success confirmations, validation errors, budget warnings, action confirmations) MUST be dispatched through the NotificationContext. Dispatch typed actions with severity: 'info' | 'warning' | 'error' | 'success'. The NotificationRenderer component maps severity to visual treatment: success → emerald-500 toast (auto-dismiss 2s), error → red-500 toast (persistent until dismissed), warning → amber-500 inline banner, info → gray-900 toast (auto-dismiss 4s). All toasts render as ARIA live regions: role='alert' for error/warning, role='status' for info/success. Split the context into NotificationStateContext and NotificationDispatchContext to prevent unnecessary re-renders.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-notification-channel decision (ADR-005). Centralizes WCAG 4.1.3 compliance (status messages as ARIA live regions) and prefers-reduced-motion handling in a single renderer. Typed severities ensure consistent visual treatment across all 7 screens.
forbids: window.alert() or console-based user feedback
Ad-hoc toast implementations outside the NotificationContext
Browser Notification API (explicitly out of scope)

implementationPatterns.title: Zod schemas at data mutation boundaries
implementationPatterns.rule: Define Zod schemas for all entity write operations (createExpense, updateExpense, updateBudget, createCategory, updateSettings). Validate input at the mutation boundary — the repository write function — before persisting to Dexie. Schema constraints: Expense.amount is z.number().min(0.01).max(99999.99).multipleOf(0.01); Expense.date is z.string().refine(d => new Date(d) <= new Date(), 'Future dates not allowed'); Expense.note is z.string().max(100).optional(); Expense.paymentMethod is z.enum(['Cash', 'Debit Card', 'Credit Card', 'UPI/Other']). Parse errors are caught and dispatched to NotificationContext as severity: 'error'. Form-level validation uses the same Zod schemas via a useFormValidation hook for instant inline feedback.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-search-strategy decision (ADR-006) and constraint-nfr-009 (data integrity). Even without a backend, validating at the persistence boundary prevents corrupt data in IndexedDB. Zod schemas serve double duty: form validation (instant feedback) and write-time safety net. Aligns with baseline pattern validation-zod-at-boundary, adapted from HTTP boundaries to Dexie write boundaries for this client-only architecture.
forbids: Writing to Dexie without schema validation
Silently swallowing validation errors — always dispatch to NotificationContext

implementationPatterns.title: Zustand for UI state and in-memory data cache
implementationPatterns.rule: Use Zustand for two concerns: (1) UI-only state (active screen, selected month, filter selections, modal open/close, notification queue) and (2) in-memory cache of frequently accessed Dexie data (current month summary, category list, recent expenses). Organize as separate Zustand slices: uiSlice, expenseCacheSlice, budgetCacheSlice. Cache slices are hydrated from Dexie on app boot and kept in sync via useLiveQuery subscriptions that call Zustand setters. Mutations follow optimistic update pattern: update Zustand immediately for instant UI response, then write to Dexie, then let liveQuery confirm or rollback.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-caching-strategy decision (ADR-004). Zustand's synchronous reads guarantee sub-150ms micro-interactions for UI-critical paths (chip selections, filter changes, popover opens). Dexie liveQuery handles eventual consistency. This two-layer pattern avoids the async-everywhere problem of pure Dexie reads while maintaining data durability.
forbids: Redux or MobX — Zustand is the chosen state manager
Storing persistent entity data only in Zustand without Dexie backing
Reading directly from Dexie in event handlers that must complete within 150ms

implementationPatterns.title: No authentication — single-user local-only architecture
implementationPatterns.rule: CashPulse has no authentication layer. All data belongs to a single implicit user. No session tokens, no login flows, no auth middleware, no RLS policies. The Dexie database is scoped to the browser origin. UserSettings (entity-004) stores preferences (currency, default budget) without any user identity. Do not add auth guards, protected routes, or session checks.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from scope exclusion constraint-scope-1 (auth out of scope) and gap-auth-strategy (resolved as 'none'). Adding auth infrastructure would violate scope and increase blast radius across all screens. Overrides baseline pattern auth-middleware-required which assumes server-side sessions.
forbids: Auth middleware or route guards
Session token storage or management
User identity fields on data entities

implementationPatterns.title: Console logging in development only — no production logging infrastructure
implementationPatterns.rule: Use console.warn and console.error for development-time debugging of Dexie operations, Zod validation failures, and unexpected state transitions. Wrap all console calls in a logger utility that checks import.meta.env.DEV before emitting. No structured logging library (Pino, Winston) — there is no server to collect logs. In production builds, the logger utility is a no-op. Dexie errors that reach the UI are dispatched to NotificationContext, not logged to console.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Overrides baseline pattern logging-structured-pino which assumes a server-side Node.js runtime. CashPulse is a client-only desktop web app with no backend. Structured JSON logging has no consumer. Console logging in dev mode is sufficient for debugging IndexedDB operations and validation errors.
forbids: Production console.log statements
Importing Pino, Winston, or other server-side logging libraries
Logging sensitive financial data (amounts, descriptions) even in dev mode

implementationPatterns.title: WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility baseline for all components
implementationPatterns.rule: All interactive elements MUST have minimum 36×36px click targets (enforced via CVA variant schemas). All interactive elements MUST have visible focus indicators: 2px ring in primary-600 with 2px offset (focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-primary-600 focus-visible:ring-offset-2). Charts MUST include role='img' with descriptive aria-label summarizing the data. Color MUST NOT be the sole indicator — all color-coded statuses (budget badges, category indicators) include text labels. All modals and popovers use Radix UI primitives which provide focus trapping and Escape-to-dismiss. Respect prefers-reduced-motion via Tailwind motion-reduce: variant — replace animations with instant state changes.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from constraint-nfr-003 (hard constraint: WCAG 2.1 AA) and gap-component-library decision (ADR-003). Radix UI primitives handle complex ARIA patterns; this pattern covers the remaining application-level requirements that must be enforced by developers.
forbids: Interactive elements smaller than 36×36px
Color as sole status indicator
Animations without motion-reduce: alternative
Custom modal/popover implementations that bypass Radix focus management

implementationPatterns.title: Derived entities computed from Expense records, not stored separately
implementationPatterns.rule: MonthSummary (entity-005), CategorySpending (entity-006), DailySpending (entity-007), and QuickAddSuggestion (entity-008) are NOT stored as separate Dexie tables. They are computed on-demand from the Expense and Budget tables via aggregation functions in dedicated utility modules (e.g., computeMonthSummary.ts, computeCategorySpending.ts). These functions are called inside useLiveQuery subscriptions so they recompute reactively when Expense data changes. Results are cached in Zustand store slices for synchronous access by chart and summary components.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-store and gap-orm-strategy decisions (ADR-001). Storing derived entities separately creates a cache invalidation problem — every Expense write would need to update 4 additional tables transactionally. Computing on-demand from the source Expense table is simpler and fast enough for personal-scale data (<2,000 records, sub-5ms computation). Zustand caching provides synchronous reads for the 150ms micro-interaction budget.
forbids: Dexie tables for MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, or QuickAddSuggestion
Storing pre-computed aggregations in IndexedDB

stackConfig.frontend: React 18 with TypeScript, Vite bundler, React Router v6 for screen navigation
stackConfig.backend: None — client-only architecture (backend explicitly out of scope)
stackConfig.database: IndexedDB via Dexie.js (~25KB) — all 8 entities stored client-side with typed indexes
stackConfig.styling: Tailwind CSS v3 + Class Variance Authority (CVA) + tailwind-merge — zero-runtime, build-time CSS with typed component variants
stackConfig.componentLibrary: shadcn/ui (copy-paste components on Radix UI primitives) + Recharts for data visualization

fields.name: id
fields.description: Primary key, generated client-side (crypto.randomUUID)

fields.name: amount
fields.description: Expense amount in the user's selected currency; must be > 0

fields.name: description
fields.description: Merchant name or short description of the expense

fields.name: categoryId
fields.description: Foreign key referencing the Category entity

fields.name: date
fields.description: Date the expense occurred; no future dates allowed (enforced in UI)

fields.name: paymentMethod
fields.description: Enum-like: one of 'cash' | 'debit_card' | 'credit_card' | 'upi_other'

fields.name: note
fields.description: Optional free-text note; max 100 characters

fields.name: createdAt
fields.description: ISO 8601 timestamp of record creation, set client-side

fields.name: updatedAt
fields.description: ISO 8601 timestamp of last modification, updated on every edit

fields.name: id
fields.description: Primary key, generated client-side

fields.name: name
fields.description: Display name of the category; unique(name)

fields.name: color
fields.description: Hex color string (e.g. '#3B82F6') used for UI segments, dots, and borders

fields.name: icon
fields.description: Icon identifier string (e.g. Lucide icon name or emoji) for category chip and expense row

fields.name: isDefault
fields.description: True for seed/system categories; false for user-created categories. Default categories cannot be deleted.

fields.name: sortOrder
fields.description: Display order in category selector grid and legend lists; default categories seeded with fixed order

fields.name: createdAt
fields.description: ISO 8601 timestamp of record creation

fields.name: id
fields.description: Primary key, generated client-side

fields.name: month
fields.description: ISO month string 'YYYY-MM'; unique(month) — one budget record per calendar month

fields.name: monthlyLimit
fields.description: Total spending cap for the month in the user's currency

fields.name: currency
fields.description: ISO 4217 currency code: one of 'USD' | 'EUR' | 'GBP' | 'INR' | 'JPY'

fields.name: createdAt
fields.description: ISO 8601 timestamp of record creation

fields.name: updatedAt
fields.description: ISO 8601 timestamp of last modification

fields.name: id
fields.description: Singleton record; always a fixed well-known UUID ('settings-singleton')

fields.name: currency
fields.description: Active ISO 4217 currency code: one of 'USD' | 'EUR' | 'GBP' | 'INR' | 'JPY'. Default 'USD'.

fields.name: defaultMonthlyBudget
fields.description: Default monthly budget limit applied when creating a new month's Budget record. Default 2000.00.

fields.name: updatedAt
fields.description: ISO 8601 timestamp of last settings change; used to trigger currency-change toast

fields.name: id
fields.description: Primary key, generated client-side

fields.name: month
fields.description: ISO month string 'YYYY-MM'; unique(month) — one summary per calendar month

fields.name: totalSpent
fields.description: Sum of all expense amounts for the month

fields.name: transactionCount
fields.description: Total number of expense records for the month

fields.name: dailyAverage
fields.description: totalSpent divided by number of days elapsed (or total days if month is complete)

fields.name: budgetLimit
fields.description: Snapshot of the Budget.monthlyLimit at time of last recalculation

fields.name: budgetStatus
fields.description: Derived status: one of 'on_track' | 'heads_up' | 'over_budget'. on_track < 80%, heads_up 80–100%, over_budget > 100%.

fields.name: remainingAmount
fields.description: budgetLimit minus totalSpent; can be negative when over budget

fields.name: computedAt
fields.description: Timestamp of last aggregation computation; used to detect stale cache

fields.name: id
fields.description: Primary key, generated client-side

fields.name: categoryId
fields.description: Foreign key referencing the Category entity

fields.name: month
fields.description: ISO month string 'YYYY-MM'; unique(categoryId, month)

fields.name: totalAmount
fields.description: Sum of expense amounts for this category in this month

fields.name: percentage
fields.description: totalAmount as a percentage of MonthSummary.totalSpent for the same month

fields.name: transactionCount
fields.description: Number of expense records for this category in this month

fields.name: computedAt
fields.description: Timestamp of last aggregation computation

fields.name: id
fields.description: Primary key, generated client-side

fields.name: date
fields.description: Calendar date 'YYYY-MM-DD'; unique(date)

fields.name: totalAmount
fields.description: Sum of all expense amounts on this date

fields.name: transactionCount
fields.description: Number of expense records on this date

fields.name: computedAt
fields.description: Timestamp of last aggregation computation

fields.name: id
fields.description: Primary key, generated client-side

fields.name: description
fields.description: Merchant/description text used to match and display the suggestion chip

fields.name: amount
fields.description: Most-recent or modal amount for this recurring expense pattern

fields.name: categoryId
fields.description: Foreign key referencing the Category entity for auto-fill

fields.name: paymentMethod
fields.description: Most-frequent payment method for this pattern: one of 'cash' | 'debit_card' | 'credit_card' | 'upi_other'

fields.name: frequency
fields.description: Count of how many times this description+category pair has been logged; used for ranking chips (descending)

fields.name: lastUsedAt
fields.description: Timestamp of the most recent expense that updated this suggestion; used as tiebreaker in ranking

fields.name: updatedAt
fields.description: Timestamp of last record update

fields.name: id
fields.description: Primary key, generated client-side at dispatch time

fields.name: severity
fields.description: One of 'info' | 'success' | 'warning' | 'error' — maps to toast/banner styling

fields.name: message
fields.description: Human-readable notification text (e.g. 'Currency updated to EUR')

fields.name: autoDismissMs
fields.description: Auto-dismiss delay in milliseconds (e.g. 2000 for currency toast, 1500 for export confirmation). Null means manual dismiss only.

fields.name: createdAt
fields.description: Timestamp of notification dispatch; used for ordering and TTL checks

additions.description: List expenses with optional filters. Query params: month (YYYY-MM), categoryId (uuid), search (string, matches description/note/amount), limit (integer, default 50), offset (integer, default 0). Returns paginated array of Expense records joined with Category color/icon/name. Supports feat-003 Recent Expenses List, feat-024 Search Expenses. Response 200: { data: Array<{ id: uuid, amount: decimal, description: string, categoryId: uuid, category: { id: uuid, name: string, color: string, icon: string }, date: date, paymentMethod: 'cash'|'debit_card'|'credit_card'|'upi_other', note?: string, createdAt: timestamptz, updatedAt: timestamptz }>, total: integer, limit: integer, offset: integer }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.

Create a new expense. Request body: { amount: decimal (>0, required), description: string (max 255, required), categoryId: uuid (required), date: date (no future dates, required), paymentMethod: 'cash'|'debit_card'|'credit_card'|'upi_other' (required), note?: string (max 100) }. On success, triggers recomputation of MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, and QuickAddSuggestion for the expense's month/date. Supports feat-005 Add/Edit Expense Form. Response 201: { data: Expense }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.

Retrieve a single expense by UUID, joined with Category fields. Supports feat-004 Expense Detail Popover. Response 200: { data: { id: uuid, amount: decimal, description: string, categoryId: uuid, category: { id: uuid, name: string, color: string, icon: string }, date: date, paymentMethod: 'cash'|'debit_card'|'credit_card'|'upi_other', note?: string, createdAt: timestamptz, updatedAt: timestamptz } }. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.

Full update of an existing expense. Request body: { amount: decimal (>0, required), description: string (max 255, required), categoryId: uuid (required), date: date (no future dates, required), paymentMethod: 'cash'|'debit_card'|'credit_card'|'upi_other' (required), note?: string (max 100) }. Sets updatedAt to now. Triggers recomputation of MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion for affected months. Supports feat-005 Edit flow. Response 200: { data: Expense }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.

Delete an expense by UUID. Triggers recomputation of MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending for the expense's month. Supports feat-004 Delete with inline confirmation. Response 200: { data: { id: uuid } }. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.

Export all expenses (or filtered by month query param) as CSV. Query params: month? (YYYY-MM). Returns CSV file with columns: date, description, category, amount, paymentMethod, note. Content-Type: text/csv. Supports feat-016 Export CSV. Response 200: CSV binary stream. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.

List all categories ordered by sortOrder ascending. Response 200: { data: Array<{ id: uuid, name: string, color: string, icon: string, isDefault: boolean, sortOrder: integer, createdAt: timestamptz }> }. Supports feat-002 Category Breakdown, feat-003 filter dropdown, feat-005 category selector grid, feat-009 Top Categories, feat-014 Settings category manager.

Create a new user-defined category. Request body: { name: string (max 64, unique, required), color: string (hex #RRGGBB, required), icon: string (max 64, required) }. Sets isDefault=false, assigns next sortOrder. Supports feat-015 Inline Category Creation. Response 201: { data: Category }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope (includes uniqueness violation on name).

Update a category's name, color, or icon. Request body: { name?: string (max 64, unique), color?: string (hex #RRGGBB), icon?: string (max 64) }. Default (isDefault=true) categories may update color/icon but not name. Supports feat-014 Settings category manager. Response 200: { data: Category }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.

Delete a user-created category (isDefault=false only). Returns 400 if category has associated expenses or isDefault=true. Supports feat-014 Settings category manager trash icon. Response 200: { data: { id: uuid } }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.

Get the Budget record for the currently active month (derived from UserSettings or query param). Query params: month? (YYYY-MM, defaults to current calendar month). If no Budget record exists for the month, auto-creates one using UserSettings.defaultMonthlyBudget. Response 200: { data: { id: uuid, month: string, monthlyLimit: decimal, currency: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY', createdAt: timestamptz, updatedAt: timestamptz } }. Supports feat-001 Budget Summary Dashboard, feat-012 Month Navigation.

Upsert the Budget record for a given month (YYYY-MM path param). Request body: { monthlyLimit: decimal (>0, required), currency: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY' (required) }. Sets updatedAt to now. Triggers MonthSummary recomputation for the month. Supports feat-014 Settings monthly budget input. Response 200: { data: Budget }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.

Retrieve the aggregated MonthSummary for a given month (YYYY-MM). Returns totalSpent, dailyAverage, transactionCount, budgetLimit, budgetStatus, remainingAmount. If stale (computedAt older than latest expense updatedAt), recomputes before returning. Supports feat-001 Budget Summary Dashboard (spent, limit, remaining, status), feat-007 Spending Insights Period Summary, feat-011 Month-over-Month Comparison, feat-012 Month Navigation. Response 200: { data: { id: uuid, month: string, totalSpent: decimal, transactionCount: integer, dailyAverage: decimal, budgetLimit: decimal, budgetStatus: 'on_track'|'heads_up'|'over_budget', remainingAmount: decimal, computedAt: timestamptz } }. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope (month has no expenses and no budget).

Returns MonthSummary for the requested month AND the immediately preceding month side-by-side, plus a computed delta. Supports feat-007 trend indicators (up/down arrow with %) and feat-011 Month-over-Month Comparison card. Response 200: { data: { current: MonthSummary, previous: MonthSummary | null, delta: { totalSpent: decimal, totalSpentPct: decimal, dailyAverage: decimal, dailyAveragePct: decimal, transactionCount: integer, transactionCountPct: decimal } } }. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.

List CategorySpending records for a given month, joined with Category fields, sorted by totalAmount descending. Query params: month (YYYY-MM, required). Supports feat-002 Donut Chart segments and legend, feat-009 Top Categories stacked bar and sorted list. Response 200: { data: Array<{ id: uuid, categoryId: uuid, category: { id: uuid, name: string, color: string, icon: string }, month: string, totalAmount: decimal, percentage: decimal, transactionCount: integer, computedAt: timestamptz }> }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.

List DailySpending records for a given month (all days YYYY-MM-01 through YYYY-MM-last). Query params: month (YYYY-MM, required). Days with no expenses are included with totalAmount=0 and transactionCount=0 for complete chart rendering. Supports feat-008 Daily Spending Bar/Line Chart. Response 200: { data: Array<{ id: uuid | null, date: date, totalAmount: decimal, transactionCount: integer, computedAt: timestamptz | null }>, monthlyAverage: decimal }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.

Return the top N individual expenses for a given month sorted by amount descending. Query params: month (YYYY-MM, required), limit (integer, default 5, max 10). Supports feat-010 Biggest Expenses List. Response 200: { data: Array<{ rank: integer, id: uuid, amount: decimal, description: string, categoryId: uuid, category: { id: uuid, name: string, color: string, icon: string }, date: date, paymentMethod: 'cash'|'debit_card'|'credit_card'|'upi_other' }> }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.

Return the top N QuickAddSuggestion records ranked by frequency descending, lastUsedAt as tiebreaker. Query params: limit (integer, default 4, max 10). Joined with Category fields for chip display. Supports feat-006 Quick Entry Suggestions. Returns empty array (not 404) when no suggestions exist, enabling feat-017 hidden state. Response 200: { data: Array<{ id: uuid, description: string, amount: decimal, categoryId: uuid, category: { id: uuid, name: string, color: string, icon: string }, paymentMethod: 'cash'|'debit_card'|'credit_card'|'upi_other', frequency: integer, lastUsedAt: timestamptz }> }.

Retrieve the singleton UserSettings record. Always returns a record (auto-creates with defaults: currency='USD', defaultMonthlyBudget=2000.00 if not yet initialized). Supports feat-014 Settings Dialog initial values, feat-022 Multi-Currency Support. Response 200: { data: { id: uuid, currency: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY', defaultMonthlyBudget: decimal, updatedAt: timestamptz } }.

Update the singleton UserSettings record. Request body: { currency?: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY', defaultMonthlyBudget?: decimal (>0) }. Sets updatedAt to now. A currency change triggers the currency-change toast notification (feat-018) via the notification context. Response 200: { data: { id: uuid, currency: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY', defaultMonthlyBudget: decimal, updatedAt: timestamptz } }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.

Dispatch a new in-app notification into the React Context notification queue. Request body: { severity: 'info'|'success'|'warning'|'error' (required), message: string (max 255, required), autoDismissMs?: integer }. Returns the created Notification record. Supports feat-018 Currency Change Toast (autoDismissMs=2000), feat-016 Export CSV confirmation (autoDismissMs=1500). Response 201: { data: { id: uuid, severity: string, message: string, autoDismissMs?: integer, createdAt: timestamptz } }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.
Manually dismiss a notification from the queue before its autoDismissMs elapses. Supports manual close button on toast/banner. Response 200: { data: { id: uuid } }. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.
