structured.themeStrategy: Single-theme (light-only) with no dark mode required. All tokens are defined as static values in tailwind.config.js under `theme.extend`. The full CashPulse palette is expressed as Tailwind color keys so every utility class (bg-primary-600, text-semantic-error, shadow-card, rounded-card, etc.) resolves to a design-system token. Category colors are dynamic runtime values supplied per-entity and are NOT tokenised as static design tokens; instead, CVA compoundVariants map the fixed set of default category slugs to their Tailwind color classes, while custom user-created categories receive inline style color values only. No brand-theming or CSS custom-property runtime switching is needed for this client-only, single-brand app.

tasks.title: Project scaffold: Vite + React + Tailwind + Dexie + Zustand
tasks.description: Initialize the Vite project with React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS v3 with CashPulse design tokens (colors, spacing, radii, shadows, fonts, motion variants), React Router v6 route definitions for /, /add-expense, /insights. Install and configure Dexie.js, Zustand, CVA, tailwind-merge, shadcn/ui primitives, and Recharts. Set up the db.ts Dexie subclass with typed tables for Expense, Category, Budget, and UserSettings entities.

tasks.title: Dexie repositories, Zod schemas, derived computations, and Zustand stores
tasks.description: Create repository modules for Expense, Category, Budget, and UserSettings with typed Dexie queries and transaction-based writes. Define Zod validation schemas for all write operations (createExpense, updateExpense, createCategory, updateBudget, updateSettings). Implement derived entity computation utilities (computeMonthSummary, computeCategorySpending, computeDailySpending, computeQuickAddSuggestions). Set up Zustand store with uiSlice (selectedMonth, activeFilters, modals), expenseCacheSlice, and budgetCacheSlice. Create the logger utility and seed default categories on first boot.

tasks.title: Notification context provider and toast renderer
tasks.description: Implement NotificationStateContext and NotificationDispatchContext with useReducer for typed notification actions (info/success/warning/error). Build the NotificationRenderer component that renders toasts with severity-based styling (emerald success, red error, amber warning, gray info), auto-dismiss timers, ARIA live regions (role='alert' for error/warning, role='status' for info/success), and prefers-reduced-motion support. Support manual dismiss via close button and auto-revert timers.

tasks.title: Shared CVA component library (Button, Card, Badge, Chip, Input, IconCircle)
tasks.description: Build the reusable component library using CVA with tailwind-merge. Components include: Button (primary/secondary/ghost/danger variants, loading/disabled states, 36px min targets, focus rings), Card (standard/elevated shadow variants), StatusBadge (on-track/heads-up/over-budget), CategoryChip (with colored selection states), TextInput (with character counter), IconCircle (category-colored backgrounds), and AnimatedCounter (number animation with prefers-reduced-motion fallback). All components enforce WCAG 2.1 AA via CVA variant schemas.

tasks.title: Top navigation bar with tabs, month navigator, and settings trigger
tasks.description: Build the TopNavigationBar component (64px height) with CashPulse logo (pulse icon + text, links to /), three NavTabs (Dashboard, Add Expense, Insights) with sliding underline indicator using React Router NavLink, MonthNavigator pill with left/right chevrons that updates Zustand selectedMonth, and SettingsTrigger gear icon button. Implement keyboard navigation and focus management. Month changes dispatch to Zustand uiSlice.

tasks.title: Dashboard page: Budget summary card and category donut chart
tasks.description: Build the DashboardPage layout component with two-column responsive grid (stacks at 768-1024px). Implement BudgetSummaryCard with AnimatedCounter for total spent/budget/remaining amounts, ProgressBar with color transitions (blue <80%, amber 80-100%, red >100%), and StatusBadge. Implement CategoryDonutChartCard with 160px Recharts PieChart, center transaction count, hover-expand segments with tooltips (category name, amount, percentage), and DonutChartLegend with colored dots. Both cards consume data via useLiveQuery from computeMonthSummary and computeCategorySpending. Handle empty states (gray ring with 0 transactions, $0.00 amounts).

tasks.title: Dashboard: Recent expenses list with category filter and expense detail popover
tasks.description: Build the RecentExpensesListCard in the dashboard right column with CategoryFilterDropdown (using Radix Select), scrollable ExpenseList, and ExpenseRowItem components. Each row shows category-colored left border, CategoryIconCircle, merchant name, category label, date, formatted amount with – prefix, and payment method. Rows are hoverable and clickable. Implement ExpenseDetailPopover (320px, Radix Popover) anchored to clicked row showing full expense details, Edit button (navigates to /add-expense?id=), and Delete button with inline confirmation ('Confirm delete?' with 3s auto-revert). Handle empty state with receipt icon and Add Expense CTA. Data via useLiveQuery on expenseRepository with month and category filters.

tasks.title: Add/Edit Expense form with category selector, date picker, and payment method
tasks.description: Build the AddExpensePage with single-column form (max-width 520px). Implement HeroAmountInput (32px, auto-focused, numeric-only with decimal auto-formatting), CategorySelectorGrid (3x2 chips with colored selection states), DatePickerField with mini calendar dropdown (Radix Popover, no future dates), PaymentMethodSelector (Cash/Debit Card/Credit Card/UPI-Other chips), NoteInput (100 char max with counter at 80+), and SaveExpenseButton (disabled when amount $0 or no category, shows '✓ Saved!' with emerald bg on success then navigates to /). Form uses Zod schema validation via useFormValidation hook. Support edit mode via URL query param ?id= that pre-fills form from existing expense.

tasks.title: Quick entry suggestions and inline category creation on Add Expense
tasks.description: Build QuickAddSuggestionsRow with horizontal scrollable chips showing frequent past expenses (e.g., '☕ Starbucks $4.50'). Clicking a chip auto-fills the form with cascade animation (50ms per field, respects prefers-reduced-motion). Hidden when no frequent expenses exist. Implement InlineCategoryCreation: '+ New Category' link below category grid that expands an inline row with text input, 6-color picker circles, and Save mini-button. Saving creates category via categoryRepository and auto-selects it in the grid. Data from computeQuickAddSuggestions via useLiveQuery.

tasks.title: Insights page: Period summary card and daily spending chart with bar/line toggle
tasks.description: Build the SpendingInsightsPage layout. Implement PeriodSummaryCard with three StatCell components (Total Spent, Daily Average, Transactions) separated by vertical dividers, each showing value, label, and trend indicator (up/down arrow with percentage vs last month, green for decrease, red for increase). Implement DailySpendingChartCard with ChartModeToggle (Bar/Line), DailyBarChart (Recharts BarChart with blue-600 bars, red-500 for 2x+ average, dashed amber average line), DailyLineChart (smooth curve with filled area), hover tooltips (date, amount, count), and staggered entry animations with motion-reduce fallback. Handle empty state with 'Not enough data yet' message. Data from computeMonthSummary comparison and computeDailySpending.

tasks.title: Insights: Top categories breakdown, biggest expenses, month comparison, and CSV export
tasks.description: Build TopCategoriesBreakdownCard with horizontal StackedCategoryBar (proportional colored segments, lift on hover with tooltip) and CategoryBreakdownList (sorted by amount, colored dot, name, individual progress bar, amount, percentage). Build BiggestExpensesCard listing top 5 expenses with rank circle (#1 blue-600), merchant, category, date, amount. Build MonthComparisonCard with side-by-side vertical bars (current vs last month, grow from bottom animation) and difference badge ('You spent $X less/more'). Build ExportCsvButton that generates CSV from expenses, shows '✓ Exported' for 1.5s. Data from computeCategorySpending, expenseRepository biggest query, and computeMonthSummary comparison.

tasks.title: Settings dialog: currency selector, budget input, and category manager
tasks.description: Build SettingsDialog as a centered modal (max-width 420px) using Radix Dialog with dark scrim overlay, dismissible via close button, scrim click, or Escape. Implement CurrencySelector dropdown (USD, EUR, GBP, INR, JPY) that dispatches currency-change toast notification on change. Implement BudgetLimitInput with currency prefix and numeric validation. Implement CategoryManager with colored dot list, trash icons (delete only non-default categories without expenses), and '+ Add category' link that opens inline creation row. All mutations go through repositories with Zod validation. Focus trapping and keyboard navigation via Radix primitives.

tasks.title: Responsive breakpoints, reduced motion support, and keyboard navigation polish
tasks.description: Add responsive layout breakpoints across all pages: 768-1024px stacks dashboard to single column with reduced chart heights, 1024-1440px standard two-column, >1440px maximizes whitespace. Implement prefers-reduced-motion support using Tailwind motion-reduce: variant across all animated components (charts instant render, disable bounce/cascade, instant transitions). Polish keyboard navigation with Tab/Enter/Space/Escape flows, ensure all focus rings are 2px blue-600 with 2px offset, verify 36x36px click targets, add ARIA roles/labels to charts (role='img' with descriptive aria-label), and ensure color is never the sole indicator.

tasks.title: Currency formatting hook, expense search/filter hook, and multi-currency support
tasks.description: Implement useCurrencyFormat hook that reads UserSettings.currency from Zustand and formats amounts with appropriate currency symbol (USD $, EUR €, GBP £, INR ₹, JPY ¥). Integrate across all amount displays. Implement useExpenseFilter hook with debounced text search across description/note/amount using Array.filter/sort on Dexie results. Wire search input on Insights or Dashboard as needed. Ensure currency changes in Settings propagate to all displayed amounts via Zustand reactivity.

tasks.title: App integration: route wiring, context providers, store hydration, and end-to-end flow
tasks.description: Wire all pages into React Router routes in App.tsx. Wrap app with NotificationContext provider and NotificationRenderer. Implement Zustand store hydration from Dexie on app boot (load settings, categories, current month data). Wire SettingsDialog open/close to SettingsTrigger in TopNavigationBar. Ensure month navigation triggers re-computation of all derived entities and chart re-renders with animations. Verify all cross-screen flows: add expense → dashboard update, delete expense → list update, edit expense → form pre-fill, currency change → toast + all amounts update, CSV export → download + confirmation toast.

tasks.title: Category breakdown donut chart with legend and hover tooltips
tasks.description: Build CategoryDonutChartCard with a 160px Recharts PieChart showing spending distribution by category with colored segments. Center displays total transaction count. DonutChartLegend lists each category with colored dot, name, and amount. Segments expand on hover with tooltip showing category name, amount, and percentage. Empty state shows gray ring with 0 transactions. Uses useLiveQuery with computeCategorySpending.

tasks.title: Recent expenses list with category filter, expense rows, and empty state
tasks.description: Build RecentExpensesListCard with CategoryFilterDropdown (using Radix Select), scrollable ExpenseList, and ExpenseRowItem components. Each row shows category-colored left border, CategoryIconCircle, merchant/description, category label, date, amount (prefixed with –), and payment method. Rows are hoverable and clickable. Includes category filter dropdown to filter by category. EmptyStateExpenses shows receipt icon with 'No expenses yet' message and Add Expense CTA link.

tasks.title: Expense detail popover with edit/delete actions and inline confirmation
tasks.description: Build ExpenseDetailPopover (320px, Radix Popover) anchored to clicked expense rows. Shows amount, category with colored dot, date, payment method, and optional note. Edit button navigates to /add-expense?edit={id} pre-filled. Delete button with inline confirmation: text changes to 'Confirm delete?' with 3-second auto-revert timer. On confirm, deletes expense via repository and dispatches success notification.

tasks.title: Add/Edit expense form with hero input, category grid, date picker, and save flow
tasks.description: Build AddExpensePage with AddExpenseFormCard containing: HeroAmountInput (32px, auto-focused, numeric-only with decimal auto-formatting), CategorySelectorGrid (3x2 chips with colored selection states), DatePickerField with mini calendar dropdown (no future dates, using Radix Popover), PaymentMethodSelector (Cash/Debit Card/Credit Card/UPI-Other chips), NoteInput (100 char max with counter at 80+), and SaveExpenseButton (full-width, disabled when amount=$0 or no category). On save: validate with Zod, write to Dexie, show '✓ Saved!' emerald button, navigate to dashboard. Edit mode pre-fills from expense ID query param.

tasks.title: Quick entry suggestions and inline category creation on Add Expense
tasks.description: Build QuickAddSuggestionsRow with horizontal scrollable QuickAddChip components showing frequent past expenses (e.g., '☕ Starbucks $4.50'). Clicking a chip auto-fills the form with cascade animation (50ms per field). Hidden when no frequent expenses exist. Build InlineCategoryCreation with '+ New Category' link that expands to show text input, 6-color picker circles, and Save mini-button. Saving creates category via repository and auto-selects it in the grid.

tasks.title: Insights page with period summary, daily spending chart (bar/line toggle)
tasks.description: Build SpendingInsightsPage with PeriodSummaryCard containing three StatCell components (Total Spent, Daily Average, Transactions) with trend indicators (up/down arrows with percentage vs last month, green for decrease, red for increase). Build DailySpendingChartCard with ChartModeToggle (Bar/Line), DailyBarChart (blue bars, red for 2x+ average, dashed amber average line), and DailyLineChart (smooth curve with filled area). Both support hover tooltips (date, amount, count). Charts animate on first render with staggered entry. Includes InsightsEmptyState.

tasks.title: Top categories breakdown, biggest expenses, month comparison, and CSV export
tasks.description: Build TopCategoriesBreakdownCard with StackedCategoryBar (horizontal stacked bar with hover lift + tooltip) and CategoryBreakdownList (sorted list with colored dot, name, progress bar, amount, percentage). Build BiggestExpensesCard with ranked rows (#1 highlighted blue). Build MonthComparisonCard with side-by-side vertical bars (this vs last month) growing from bottom, with difference badge. Build ExportCsvButton that generates CSV from expenses, shows '✓ Exported' emerald text for 1.5s.

tasks.title: Settings dialog with currency selector, budget input, and category manager
tasks.description: Build SettingsDialog (Radix Dialog, max-width 420px, dark scrim) with DialogHeader and close button. CurrencySelector dropdown (USD/EUR/GBP/INR/JPY) that dispatches currency-change toast on change. BudgetLimitInput with currency prefix and numeric validation. CategoryManager with colored dot list, trash icons (only for non-default categories without expenses), and '+ Add category' link that opens inline creation row. Dismissible via close button, scrim click, or Escape.

tasks.title: Responsive breakpoints, reduced motion support, and keyboard navigation polish
tasks.description: Add responsive layout breakpoints: 768-1024px stacks dashboard to single column with reduced chart heights; 1024-1440px standard two-column; >1440px maximizes whitespace. Implement prefers-reduced-motion support via Tailwind motion-reduce: variants across all animated components (charts, counters, cascades, transitions). Polish keyboard navigation: Tab/Enter/Space/Escape flows, 2px blue-600 focus rings with 2px offset, chart tooltip keyboard access, ARIA roles/labels on charts and interactive elements, minimum 36x36px click targets verification.

tasks.title: App integration: route wiring, Zustand hydration, liveQuery sync, and search
tasks.description: Wire all pages into App.tsx with React Router routes (/, /add-expense, /insights). Implement Zustand store hydration from Dexie on app boot with useLiveQuery subscriptions that sync cache slices. Add expense search/filter hook (useExpenseFilter) with debounced text input matching description/note/amount. Connect SettingsDialog open/close to SettingsTrigger. Ensure month navigation triggers counter animations and chart re-renders. Verify all notification flows (currency change toast, CSV export, delete confirmation).

tasks.title: Scaffold Vite + React + TypeScript project with Tailwind, Dexie, Zustand, and routing
tasks.description: Initialize the Vite project with React 18 and TypeScript. Configure tailwind.config.js with all CashPulse design tokens (colors, spacing, radii, shadows, fonts, motion variants). Set up React Router v6 with routes for /, /add-expense, and /insights. Install and configure Dexie.js, Zustand, shadcn/ui, Recharts, CVA, and tailwind-merge. Create the db.ts Dexie subclass with typed tables for Expense, Category, Budget, and UserSettings.

tasks.title: Build shared CVA component library (Button, Card, Badge, Chip, Input, IconCircle)
tasks.description: Create the reusable component library using CVA with typed variants. Includes Button (primary, secondary, destructive, ghost variants with 36px min targets and focus rings), Card (with shadow tokens), StatusBadge (on-track/heads-up/over-budget), Chip (selectable with category colors), TextInput, IconCircle, and EmptyState. All components enforce accessibility constraints via CVA variant schemas.

tasks.title: Implement Dexie repositories, Zod validation schemas, and derived data computations
tasks.description: Create repository modules for Expense, Category, Budget, and UserSettings with Dexie transaction-based write operations. Define Zod validation schemas for all entity mutations (createExpense, updateExpense, createCategory, updateBudget, updateSettings). Implement derived data computation utilities: computeMonthSummary, computeCategorySpending, computeDailySpending, computeQuickAddSuggestions. Seed default categories on first app load.

tasks.title: Set up Zustand store slices and NotificationContext with toast renderer
tasks.description: Create Zustand store with uiSlice (selectedMonth, activeScreen, filters, modal states), expenseCacheSlice, and budgetCacheSlice. Implement NotificationContext with useReducer for typed severity dispatching, split into State and Dispatch contexts. Build NotificationRenderer component that renders toasts with ARIA live regions, auto-dismiss timers, and severity-based styling (emerald/red/amber/gray).

tasks.title: Build TopNavigationBar with NavTabs, MonthNavigator, and SettingsTrigger
tasks.description: Implement the 64px TopNavigationBar with CashPulse logo (pulse icon + text, links to /), three NavTabs (Dashboard, Add Expense, Insights) with sliding underline indicator using React Router NavLink, MonthNavigator pill with left/right chevrons that updates Zustand selectedMonth, and SettingsTrigger gear icon button. MonthNavigator dispatches month changes that trigger counter animations on amounts.

tasks.title: Build Dashboard left column: BudgetSummaryCard and CategoryDonutChart
tasks.description: Implement the DashboardPage layout with two-column grid. Build BudgetSummaryCard with progress bar (color changes: blue <80%, amber 80-100%, red >100%), StatusBadge (On Track/Heads Up/Over Budget), animated amount counters, and remaining amount display. Build CategoryDonutChartCard with 160px Recharts PieChart, center transaction count, legend with colored dots/names/amounts, hover expand with tooltips. Both consume derived data via useLiveQuery and Zustand cache. Handle empty states (gray ring, $0.00).

tasks.title: Build Dashboard right column: RecentExpensesList with filter and ExpenseDetailPopover
tasks.description: Implement the dashboard right column with RecentExpensesListCard containing CategoryFilterDropdown (using Radix Select), scrollable ExpenseList with ExpenseRowItem cards (category-colored left border, icon circle, merchant name, category label, date, amount with – prefix, payment method). Rows are hoverable and clickable. Build ExpenseDetailPopover (320px Radix Popover) showing expense details with Edit button (navigates to /add-expense?id=X) and Delete button with inline confirmation (text changes to 'Confirm delete?' with 3s auto-revert). Handle empty state with receipt icon and Add Expense CTA.

tasks.title: Build Add/Edit Expense form with all input fields and save flow
tasks.description: Implement AddExpensePage with single-column form (max-width 520px). Build HeroAmountInput (32px, auto-focused, numeric-only with decimal auto-formatting), CategorySelectorGrid (3x2 chips with colored selection states), DatePickerField with mini calendar dropdown (Radix Popover, no future dates), PaymentMethodSelector (Cash/Debit Card/Credit Card/UPI-Other chips), NoteInput (100 char max with counter at 80+), and SaveExpenseButton (disabled when amount=$0 or no category). On save: validate with Zod, write to Dexie, show '✓ Saved!' with emerald background, navigate to dashboard. Support edit mode via URL query param loading existing expense.

tasks.title: Build QuickAddSuggestions row and InlineCategoryCreation on Add Expense screen
tasks.description: Implement QuickAddSuggestionsRow with horizontal scrollable chips showing frequent past expenses (emoji + merchant + amount). Clicking a chip auto-fills the form with cascade animation (50ms per field). Hidden when no suggestions exist. Build InlineCategoryCreation: '+ New Category' link below category grid that expands an inline row with text input, 6-color picker circles, and Save mini-button. Saving creates category via Dexie and auto-selects it in the grid.

tasks.title: Build Insights page: PeriodSummaryCard and DailySpendingChart with bar/line toggle
tasks.description: Implement SpendingInsightsPage layout. Build PeriodSummaryCard with three StatCells (Total Spent, Daily Average, Transactions) separated by vertical dividers, each with value, label, and trend indicator (up/down arrow with percentage vs last month, green for decrease, red for increase). Build DailySpendingChartCard with Bar/Line toggle using Recharts: bar chart (blue-600 bars, red-500 for 2x+ average), line chart (smooth curve with filled area), hover tooltips (date, amount, count), dashed amber average line. Charts animate on first render with staggered entry. Handle empty state.

tasks.title: Build Insights cards: TopCategories, BiggestExpenses, MonthComparison, and ExportCSV
tasks.description: Build TopCategoriesBreakdownCard with horizontal stacked bar (proportional category segments, lift on hover with tooltip) and sorted category list (colored dot, name, progress bar, amount, percentage). Build BiggestExpensesCard listing top 5 expenses by amount with rank circles (#1 in blue-600). Build MonthComparisonCard with side-by-side vertical bars (current vs last month, grow from bottom) and difference badge. Build ExportCsvButton that generates CSV from expenses and shows '✓ Exported' emerald text for 1.5s.

tasks.title: Build Settings dialog with currency selector, budget input, and category manager
tasks.description: Implement SettingsDialog as a centered Radix Dialog modal (max-width 420px) with dark scrim overlay. Build CurrencySelector dropdown (USD, EUR, GBP, INR, JPY), BudgetLimitInput with currency prefix (default $2,000), and CategoryManager with colored dot list, trash icons (only for non-default categories without expenses), and '+ Add category' link. Dismissible via close button, scrim click, or Escape. Currency change dispatches toast notification via NotificationContext. Settings persist to Dexie UserSettings.

tasks.title: Implement responsive breakpoints, reduced motion support, and keyboard navigation polish
tasks.description: Add responsive layout breakpoints: 768-1024px stacks dashboard to single column with reduced chart heights; 1024-1440px standard two-column; >1440px maximizes whitespace with 10+ expense items. Ensure prefers-reduced-motion replaces all chart animations with instant renders, disables bounce/cascade effects, replaces slide transitions with instant cuts. Verify full keyboard navigation (Tab, Enter, Space, Escape), chart tooltip keyboard access, ARIA roles/labels on charts, and color-not-sole-indicator compliance across all screens.

tasks.title: Wire all screens into App router, hydrate Zustand from Dexie, and end-to-end flow verification
tasks.description: Connect all page components into React Router in App.tsx. Implement app boot sequence: seed default categories, initialize UserSettings, hydrate Zustand cache slices from Dexie via useLiveQuery subscriptions. Wire SettingsDialog trigger from TopNavigationBar. Ensure month navigation updates all screens reactively. Verify the complete user flow: add expense → see on dashboard → view details → edit → check insights → export CSV → change settings.

alternatives.name: localStorage + in-memory state (Zustand/Context)
alternatives.description: All 8 data entities are stored as serialized JSON in localStorage, hydrated into an in-memory state manager (Zustand or React Context) on app load. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed on-the-fly via memoized selectors rather than persisted. QuickAddSuggestions are derived from Expense history. No external dependencies beyond the state library.
tradeoffs: PROS: Zero new infrastructure — no DB engine, no server, no migrations. Blast radius is minimal (1–2 store files + hydration hook).
PROS: Sub-millisecond reads satisfy all 150ms micro-interaction and 350ms transition constraints trivially.
PROS: Perfectly aligned with scope-exclusion constraints (no backend, no auth, no sync, no cloud).
CONS: localStorage cap is ~5MB per origin — sufficient for ~50,000 expense records at ~100 bytes each, but no graceful degradation if exceeded.
CONS: No structured querying; filtering/sorting for Insights screen must be done in JS — acceptable for personal-scale data but adds selector complexity.
CONS: Data is browser-bound and non-portable; clearing browser data destroys all records (acceptable given 'no backup/sync' scope exclusion).
CONS: Adds 1 dependency if using Zustand (zustand ~3KB gzipped); 0 if using React Context alone.
CONS: Concurrent tab writes can cause race conditions — mitigated with storage event listeners but adds ~30 lines of sync logic.

alternatives.name: IndexedDB via Dexie.js
alternatives.description: All 8 entities are stored in a structured IndexedDB database using Dexie.js as the wrapper. Each entity maps to a Dexie table with typed indexes (e.g., Expense indexed by date, categoryId, amount). Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via Dexie's live queries or cached in a lightweight Zustand store. Dexie's liveQuery() hook enables reactive UI updates without polling.
tradeoffs: PROS: Storage limit is effectively unbounded for personal use (browser quota, typically 60%+ of disk) — far exceeds localStorage.
PROS: Structured indexes enable fast range queries for Insights charts (e.g., expenses by month) without full JS-side scans.
PROS: Dexie's async API with liveQuery() integrates cleanly with React hooks — reactive updates satisfy the 600ms chart render constraint.
PROS: Dexie supports versioned schema migrations — future entity changes (e.g., adding tags to Expense) are handled cleanly.
CONS: Adds 2 new dependencies: dexie (~25KB gzipped) and optionally dexie-react-hooks (~2KB). Increases bundle size noticeably.
CONS: All DB operations are async — requires careful loading state management across 7 screens to avoid layout shift during transitions.
CONS: IndexedDB is not inspectable in all DevTools as easily as localStorage — debugging requires Dexie DevTools extension or manual console queries.
CONS: Overkill for the current 8-entity, single-user, no-sync scope — complexity cost is real with limited near-term payoff.
CONS: Affects ~5–8 data-layer files (store, hooks, entity schemas, migration file) plus any component that previously used synchronous state reads.

alternatives.name: SQLite via sql.js (WASM) with localStorage persistence
alternatives.description: A full SQLite database runs in-browser via sql.js (compiled to WebAssembly). The DB binary is serialized to localStorage or a File System Access API file on every write. All 8 entities are proper relational tables with foreign keys and SQL queries. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via SQL GROUP BY queries. The WASM module is lazy-loaded after initial paint to avoid blocking the 350ms screen transition constraint.
tradeoffs: PROS: Full SQL expressiveness — complex Insights aggregations (spending by category per month, daily rolling averages) are trivial GROUP BY queries.
PROS: Familiar relational model maps directly to the 8 entities and their relationships (Expense → Category, Budget → Category).
PROS: Portable — the DB binary can be exported as a .sqlite file, giving users a real data export path.
CONS: sql.js WASM bundle is ~1.5MB — significantly increases initial load time and violates the spirit of the 350ms transition constraint unless aggressively code-split and lazy-loaded.
CONS: Adds 1 heavy dependency (sql.js) plus a custom persistence layer (~100–150 lines) to serialize/deserialize the binary blob to localStorage on every write.
CONS: Write performance degrades as DB grows — serializing the entire binary on each expense add is O(DB size), not O(record size).
CONS: No reactivity — UI must manually re-query after mutations; requires a thin event bus or state layer on top, adding ~2–3 additional files.
CONS: Blast radius is high: affects build config (WASM asset handling), data layer (~6–10 files), and potentially webpack/vite config for WASM MIME types.
CONS: Debugging requires custom tooling — no browser DevTools panel for WASM SQLite.

alternatives.name: localStorage (raw JSON) — minimal change, no state library
alternatives.description: The absolute minimal implementation: each entity collection is stored as a JSON array under a namespaced localStorage key (e.g., 'cashpulse:expenses', 'cashpulse:categories'). A thin utility module (~60 lines) provides typed get/set/delete helpers. React component state (useState/useReducer) holds the working copy; localStorage is written on every mutation via a useEffect. No external state library. Derived entities are computed inline in components or via useMemo.
tradeoffs: PROS: Zero new dependencies — pure React + browser APIs only. Smallest possible blast radius (1 utility file + useEffect in ~3–4 components).
PROS: Trivially debuggable — all data visible in DevTools > Application > localStorage with no extensions needed.
PROS: Synchronous reads mean all micro-interaction (150ms) and transition (350ms) constraints are met with zero async overhead.
PROS: Easiest to onboard new contributors — no ORM, no schema, no migration concepts.
CONS: No centralized state — prop drilling or Context required as the app grows across 7 screens; risks becoming unmaintainable past ~10 components.
CONS: No selector memoization by default — Insights screen aggregations (CategorySpending, DailySpending, MonthSummary) recompute on every render unless manually memoized with useMemo.
CONS: Same 5MB localStorage cap and tab-sync limitations as alt-1, with less architectural guardrail.
CONS: Refactoring to a proper state manager later (if needed) requires touching every component that reads from localStorage directly — migration cost grows over time.
CONS: QuickAddSuggestion (entity-008) frequency scoring requires iterating full Expense history on every form load — acceptable now, but O(n) with no indexing.

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency to full relational SQL, all implementable in a greenfield browser-only app with no backend. PostgreSQL and Supabase were excluded because the scope explicitly rules out backend API development, user authentication, cloud storage, and data sync — a remote database would violate at least three hard/soft constraints simultaneously. Firebase/Firestore was excluded for the same reasons (cloud sync, auth dependency). A service worker + Cache API approach was excluded because it is optimized for network caching, not structured data querying, and would require a custom query layer equivalent in complexity to Dexie with none of the ecosystem benefits. The four selected alternatives represent: (1) the pragmatic production choice for a personal app at this scale, (2) the structured/scalable browser-DB choice, (3) the maximum-power SQL choice with real cost, and (4) the absolute minimal-change baseline — giving the Architecture Writer a clear cost/benefit gradient to choose from.
structured.recommendation: alt-1 (localStorage + Zustand) is the recommended choice. CashPulse is a single-user, no-sync, desktop-web-only personal tracker with 8 entities and a scope that explicitly excludes backend, cloud, and multi-device concerns. Zustand adds only ~3KB, provides centralized reactive state with selector memoization (critical for the Insights screen's derived entities), and satisfies every performance constraint with synchronous reads. The localStorage persistence layer is ~40 lines of code. This hits the sweet spot: more maintainable than raw localStorage (alt-4), far less complex than Dexie (alt-2), and avoids the WASM bundle penalty of sql.js (alt-3). If the app ever needs to grow beyond its current scope, migrating Zustand actions to call an async persistence layer (Dexie or a real API) is a well-understood, low-friction refactor.

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS v3 (Utility-First)
alternatives.description: Use Tailwind CSS as the sole styling layer. Design tokens (spacing, radii, shadows, colors) are codified in tailwind.config.js under the 'extend' key. All components — cards, chips, popovers, form inputs — are styled via utility classes directly in JSX. The 4px base spacing unit maps to Tailwind's default scale (space-1=4px). Custom values for card-radius (16px → rounded-2xl), button-radius (10px → rounded-[10px]), badge-radius (20px → rounded-full), input-radius (8px → rounded-lg), and both shadow tokens are added as theme extensions. Focus rings are enforced via ring-2 ring-blue-600 ring-offset-2 utilities. prefers-reduced-motion is handled via Tailwind's motion-reduce: variant. Breakpoints are configured at 768px (md), 1024px (lg), and 1440px (2xl).
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Zero runtime CSS-in-JS overhead — all styles are statically extracted at build time, ensuring micro-interactions and transitions stay well within 150ms budget
POSITIVE: Purge/JIT engine produces minimal CSS bundle (~5-15KB gzipped for a 7-screen app), benefiting initial load and chart render timing
POSITIVE: Breakpoint variants (md:, lg:, 2xl:) make the three-breakpoint responsive requirement declarative and co-located with markup
POSITIVE: motion-reduce: variant natively handles prefers-reduced-motion for all animation utilities in one pass

NEGATIVE: Design token enforcement is convention-based, not type-safe — a developer can use arbitrary values (e.g., rounded-[13px]) and silently break the design system; requires ESLint plugin (eslint-plugin-tailwindcss) to lint
NEGATIVE: Adds 1 new dependency (tailwindcss) plus 2 peer dependencies (postcss, autoprefixer); requires PostCSS pipeline configuration
NEGATIVE: Long className strings on complex components (e.g., the Add Expense form card) reduce JSX readability; mitigated by extracting repeated patterns into component abstractions
NEGATIVE: Inter font must be loaded separately (Google Fonts or self-hosted) and wired into fontFamily config — 1 additional config step
NEGATIVE: Custom shadow tokens (card shadow, elevated shadow) require verbatim values in config since Tailwind's defaults don't match exactly — minor but must be documented

alternatives.name: CSS Modules + CSS Custom Properties
alternatives.description: Each component gets a co-located .module.css file. A single global tokens.css file at the project root declares all design tokens as CSS custom properties (--color-primary: #2563eb; --radius-card: 16px; --shadow-card: 0 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,0.06); etc.). Components import their module and reference token variables. Animations and transitions are written in plain CSS keyframes within the module files. Focus rings are applied via a shared .focusRing class in a global utilities.module.css. Media queries for the three breakpoints are written per-component. prefers-reduced-motion is handled via @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) blocks inside each animation-bearing module.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Zero new runtime dependencies — CSS Modules are supported natively by Vite, Next.js, and Create React App without additional packages
POSITIVE: Lowest blast radius of all options; each component's styles are fully isolated, so a change to ExpenseCard.module.css cannot affect BudgetChip.module.css
POSITIVE: CSS custom properties provide a single source of truth for all design tokens; updating --radius-card in tokens.css propagates everywhere instantly
POSITIVE: No JavaScript overhead for styling at all — transitions and animations run entirely on the GPU compositor thread, guaranteeing sub-150ms micro-interactions

NEGATIVE: No co-location of responsive variants with markup — breakpoint logic is split across JSX (conditional classNames) and CSS files, increasing cognitive overhead for the three-breakpoint layout requirement

NEGATIVE: Sharing styles across components (e.g., the 2px blue-600 focus ring applied to 15+ interactive elements) requires either a global utility class or repeated declarations — both have maintenance tradeoffs

NEGATIVE: Dynamic styles based on component state (e.g., chip selected color, budget bar fill percentage) require CSS custom property overrides via inline style props or additional class variants, adding boilerplate
NEGATIVE: No built-in linting for token usage — a developer can hardcode #2563eb instead of var(--color-primary) with no warning; requires custom Stylelint rules
NEGATIVE: Approximately 7 screen files × avg 3 components = ~21 .module.css files to create and maintain alongside JSX

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS v3 + Class Variance Authority (CVA)
alternatives.description: Extends alt-tailwind by adding Class Variance Authority (CVA) as a variant management layer. CVA defines typed component variant schemas (e.g., Button variants: primary/secondary/ghost; sizes: sm/md/lg; states: default/danger). Each variant maps to a Tailwind class string. This enforces the design system at the component API level — consumers pass variant='primary' not raw class strings. The tailwind.config.js still holds all token extensions. Shadcn/ui primitives (built on Radix UI + Tailwind + CVA) can be selectively adopted for accessible primitives (Dialog, Popover, Select) that satisfy the WCAG 2.1 AA keyboard navigation and focus ring requirements out of the box.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: CVA makes design token compliance type-safe and enforced at the component API — invalid variant combinations are TypeScript compile errors, preventing silent design system drift across 7 screens

POSITIVE: Radix UI primitives (via shadcn/ui) provide battle-tested WCAG 2.1 AA keyboard navigation (Tab, Enter, Space, Escape), focus management, and ARIA attributes for Popover, Dialog, and Select — directly satisfying hard accessibility constraints without custom implementation
POSITIVE: Retains all Tailwind performance benefits (static extraction, JIT, motion-reduce: variant) from alt-tailwind
POSITIVE: Staggered chart entry animations and slide screen transitions can be composed as CVA variants (animate-in, animate-out) keeping animation logic co-located with component definitions

NEGATIVE: Adds 2 new dependencies beyond Tailwind: cva (class-variance-authority) and @radix-ui/* packages (one per primitive used — estimate 4-6 for this app: Dialog, Popover, Select, Checkbox, Tooltip, DropdownMenu)
NEGATIVE: Shadcn/ui components are copied into the project (not imported from npm) — adds ~8-12 component files to the codebase that must be maintained and customized to match CashPulse tokens
NEGATIVE: Higher initial setup cost than plain Tailwind: ~2-3 hours to configure CVA schemas for all shared components (Button, Input, Card, Badge, Chip) across the design system
NEGATIVE: Developers unfamiliar with CVA's variant/compoundVariants API face a learning curve; requires team documentation
NEGATIVE: tailwind-merge (twMerge) is also needed to safely merge className overrides without specificity conflicts — 1 additional dependency

alternatives.name: styled-components v6 (CSS-in-JS)
alternatives.description: All component styles are written as tagged template literals using styled-components. A ThemeProvider at the app root injects the full design token set (colors, spacing, radii, shadows, typography) as a typed theme object accessible in every styled component via props.theme. Dynamic styles (chip selected state, budget bar fill, category color badges) are expressed as interpolated functions. Keyframe animations use the keyframes helper. Global styles (Inter font, CSS reset, focus ring defaults) are applied via createGlobalStyle.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: ThemeProvider gives the strongest design token enforcement — all token values flow from a single typed object, and TypeScript's DefaultTheme interface catches any token reference typos at compile time

POSITIVE: Dynamic styling based on props (e.g., <BudgetBar fill={67} />, <CategoryChip color={category.color} selected={isSelected} />) is the most ergonomic of all options — no className juggling or inline style hacks
POSITIVE: Co-location of styles with component logic in a single .tsx file reduces file count compared to CSS Modules (~21 fewer .module.css files)

NEGATIVE: Runtime CSS injection adds measurable overhead — styled-components generates and injects style tags at render time, risking micro-interaction latency above the 150ms budget on lower-end hardware, especially for chart components with many styled children
NEGATIVE: Server-side rendering requires ServerStyleSheet or StyleSheetManager configuration to avoid FOUC — adds complexity if Next.js or SSR is adopted
NEGATIVE: Adds 1 large dependency (styled-components ~12KB gzipped) with a significant impact on bundle size compared to zero-runtime alternatives
NEGATIVE: CSS-in-JS styles are not statically extractable — Lighthouse CSS coverage will show higher unused CSS percentages, and critical CSS cannot be inlined at build time

NEGATIVE: The prefers-reduced-motion requirement must be manually implemented inside each animated component's template literal using a css helper block — no automatic variant system like Tailwind's motion-reduce:
NEGATIVE: Migrating away from styled-components in the future (e.g., to RSC-compatible styling) requires touching every component file — highest long-term blast radius of all options

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum of modern CSS methodologies applicable to a greenfield React/TypeScript desktop app in 2024. CSS Modules was included as the mandatory "minimal change" option (zero new dependencies). Plain Tailwind was included as the industry-standard utility-first approach with strong performance characteristics matching the sub-150ms constraint. Tailwind+CVA was included because the hard WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility constraint (keyboard nav, focus rings, ARIA) is non-trivial to implement from scratch and Radix UI primitives directly address it. styled-components was included as the canonical CSS-in-JS representative. Excluded alternatives: (1) Vanilla Extract / Linaria — zero-runtime CSS-in-JS with good DX, but adds significant build tooling complexity for a greenfield app with no existing Babel/webpack pipeline assumptions stated; (2) Emotion — functionally similar to styled-components with marginally smaller bundle but no meaningful differentiation for this use case; (3) UnoCSS — atomic CSS alternative to Tailwind but less mature ecosystem and fewer references for the team to draw on; (4) Plain global CSS — insufficient isolation for a 7-screen, 25-feature app and no token enforcement mechanism.
structured.recommendation: alt-tailwind-cva — Tailwind CSS v3 + CVA + Radix UI primitives. The hard WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility constraint is the deciding factor. Implementing fully compliant keyboard navigation, focus trapping (popovers, modals), and ARIA management from scratch is high-risk and time-consuming. Radix UI primitives solve this definitively. CVA then enforces the strict design token system (radii, shadows, spacing) at the TypeScript API level, preventing drift across 7 screens and 25 features. Tailwind's static extraction guarantees the performance budget is met. The tradeoff of ~2-3 hours extra setup and 6-8 additional dependencies is well justified by the accessibility compliance guarantee and long-term maintainability of a typed variant system.

alternatives.name: shadcn/ui (Radix UI primitives + Tailwind CSS)
alternatives.description: shadcn/ui is a collection of copy-paste components built on top of Radix UI headless primitives and styled with Tailwind CSS utility classes. Components are vendored directly into the project (no runtime library dependency), giving full ownership of markup and styles. Radix primitives provide battle-tested accessibility (ARIA, keyboard navigation, focus management) out of the box. Tailwind enables precise token mapping for the CashPulse design system (Inter, 4px spacing scale, custom radii, shadows). Popover, Dialog, Select, and Tooltip primitives are all included and WCAG 2.1 AA compliant. Animation is handled via tailwindcss-animate or CSS transitions, making prefers-reduced-motion trivial to implement with a single Tailwind variant.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Zero runtime bundle overhead — components are inlined source files, not an npm package import
POSITIVE: Radix primitives handle all WCAG 2.1 AA keyboard/ARIA requirements (Tab, Enter, Space, Escape, focus traps) with no extra work
POSITIVE: Tailwind utility classes map 1:1 to design tokens (e.g., rounded-[10px], shadow-[0_1px_3px_rgba(0,0,0,0.06)], font-inter)
POSITIVE: prefers-reduced-motion support via Tailwind's motion-reduce: variant applied globally in one config line
POSITIVE: No design opinion lock-in — every pixel is owned and overridable
NEGATIVE: Adds 2 dependencies: tailwindcss + @radix-ui/* (one package per primitive used, ~8-10 for this project)
NEGATIVE: No pre-built chart components — Recharts or Visx must be added separately for Insights screen
NEGATIVE: Initial scaffolding requires running the shadcn CLI per component (~15-20 component files generated into /components/ui/)
NEGATIVE: Tailwind class verbosity can make JSX noisy without a consistent component abstraction layer

alternatives.name: Mantine v7
alternatives.description: Mantine is a fully-featured React component library with 100+ components, a built-in theming system, and its own CSS-in-JS-free styling via CSS Modules. v7 dropped emotion in favor of plain CSS, eliminating runtime style injection overhead. It ships with a DatePicker (critical for the Add Expense date field with future-date blocking), NumberInput (ideal for the amount field with decimal formatting), Combobox, Popover, Modal, and Charts package (@mantine/charts, built on Recharts). The theme object directly accepts design tokens: fontFamily, spacing scale, radius values, shadows, and primary color shades. WCAG 2.1 AA compliance is built in across all interactive components.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Ships a NumberInput component with built-in decimal formatting, min/max constraints, and numeric-only enforcement — directly satisfies the $99,999.99 validation constraint
POSITIVE: DatePicker supports maxDate prop for blocking future dates out of the box
POSITIVE: @mantine/charts wraps Recharts with accessible defaults (role='img', aria-label support) — covers the Insights screen charts
POSITIVE: Single cohesive theming API maps all CashPulse tokens (Inter, radii, shadows, blue-600 primary) in one theme object
POSITIVE: CSS Modules in v7 means zero runtime style overhead and no hydration issues
NEGATIVE: Adds 3-5 npm packages (@mantine/core, @mantine/hooks, @mantine/dates, @mantine/charts, dayjs peer dep) — largest dependency footprint of all options

NEGATIVE: Mantine's default visual language (border radii, spacing, typography scale) requires explicit token overrides to match CashPulse's sharp, structured aesthetic — risk of design drift if overrides are incomplete
NEGATIVE: Bundle size: @mantine/core is ~120KB gzipped even with tree-shaking; heavier than shadcn/ui for equivalent components
NEGATIVE: Mantine's animation defaults (transitions, loaders) must be audited and overridden for the prefers-reduced-motion constraint
NEGATIVE: Opinionated CSS class naming can conflict with any co-existing Tailwind setup

alternatives.name: Material UI (MUI) v6
alternatives.description: MUI is the most widely adopted React component library, offering a comprehensive suite of components backed by Google's Material Design specification. It provides a robust theming system via createTheme(), TypeScript-first APIs, and strong accessibility defaults. The sx prop and theme overrides allow full design token customization. MUI's DataGrid, Select, DatePicker (via @mui/x-date-pickers), and Dialog components are production-hardened. However, MUI's visual identity is strongly Material Design, requiring significant theme overrides to achieve CashPulse's sharp financial aesthetic.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Largest ecosystem and community — highest confidence in long-term maintenance and accessibility compliance
POSITIVE: @mui/x-date-pickers provides a DatePicker with maxDate and disableFuture props, directly satisfying the future-date blocking constraint
POSITIVE: createTheme() supports full token mapping: typography.fontFamily, shape.borderRadius, shadows array, palette.primary
POSITIVE: Excellent TypeScript support with typed theme augmentation
NEGATIVE: Emotion runtime dependency adds ~7KB gzipped and introduces SSR hydration complexity

NEGATIVE: Material Design visual defaults (elevation shadows, ripple effects, rounded buttons) require extensive theme overrides to match CashPulse's design tokens — estimated 200-400 lines of theme configuration
NEGATIVE: Adds 4+ packages: @mui/material, @emotion/react, @emotion/styled, @mui/x-date-pickers
NEGATIVE: Bundle size is the largest of all options (~150KB+ gzipped for core); tree-shaking helps but component overhead is real
NEGATIVE: Ripple animations and transition defaults must be explicitly disabled or overridden for the 150ms micro-interaction constraint and prefers-reduced-motion compliance
NEGATIVE: MUI's opinionated class structure makes it harder to achieve pixel-perfect custom designs compared to headless alternatives

alternatives.name: Custom Components + Radix UI Primitives Only (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: The minimal-footprint option: use only @radix-ui/* headless primitives (Popover, Dialog, Select, Tooltip, Checkbox) for the accessibility-critical interactive components, and hand-write all visual components (cards, badges, buttons, inputs, chips) as plain React + CSS Modules. No component library is adopted. This gives maximum design fidelity to CashPulse's token system with zero visual opinion lock-in. Recharts is added separately for charts. This is the 'minimal blast radius' path — only the files you write exist, and every style decision is explicit.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Zero design opinion lock-in — every component is purpose-built for CashPulse's exact token spec (10px button radius, 16px card radius, blue-600 focus rings, etc.)
POSITIVE: Smallest possible bundle — only Radix primitives actually used are installed (~3-5 packages)
POSITIVE: No migration risk, no upstream breaking changes from a component library major version
POSITIVE: prefers-reduced-motion, focus ring styles, and animation timing are all authored once in a global CSS file with full control

NEGATIVE: Highest upfront development cost — every component (NumberInput with decimal formatting, DatePicker with future-date blocking, category chip selector, Quick Add combobox) must be built from scratch
NEGATIVE: Accessibility correctness for complex widgets (combobox, date picker) is the team's responsibility — Radix only covers primitives, not composite patterns like a full DatePicker
NEGATIVE: No built-in NumberInput — the $99,999.99 validation, decimal auto-formatting, and numeric-only enforcement must be hand-implemented (estimated 80-120 lines of input logic)
NEGATIVE: Ongoing maintenance burden: any new interactive pattern requires building from scratch rather than pulling from a library
NEGATIVE: Estimated 30-40% more component development time compared to shadcn/ui

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-opinion headless to fully-featured opinionated libraries, all implementable in a greenfield React project. MUI was included despite its design-opinion overhead because it is the most commonly evaluated option at this decision point and its tradeoffs must be explicitly documented. Chakra UI was excluded because its v3 rewrite introduced breaking changes and its CSS-in-JS runtime conflicts with the performance constraints. Ant Design was excluded because its design language is strongly enterprise/Chinese-market aesthetic and would require even more override work than MUI to match CashPulse's financial design system. Bootstrap/React-Bootstrap was excluded because it lacks the headless accessibility primitives needed for WCAG 2.1 AA compliance on complex widgets (combobox, popover, date picker) without significant custom work. The recommendation favors shadcn/ui because it uniquely satisfies the hard accessibility constraint via Radix primitives, maps cleanly to all design tokens via Tailwind, has the lowest runtime overhead, and leaves chart implementation open to the best-fit library (Recharts) without bundling an opinionated chart solution.
structured.recommendation: alt-shadcn-ui — shadcn/ui is the strongest fit for CashPulse. It satisfies the hard WCAG 2.1 AA constraint via Radix UI primitives (keyboard navigation, focus management, ARIA roles are all handled), maps precisely to every CashPulse design token via Tailwind utility classes, and has zero runtime bundle overhead since components are vendored source files. The prefers-reduced-motion constraint is handled with a single Tailwind config line. The only gap is charts, which is intentionally left open for Recharts (a deliberate separation of concerns). Compared to alt-custom-headless, shadcn/ui provides the same design control but eliminates the need to hand-build complex accessible widgets like Combobox (Quick Add suggestions) and Popover (category selector). Compared to Mantine and MUI, it avoids design-opinion override debt and runtime style injection.

alternatives.name: Dexie.js (IndexedDB wrapper) — Minimal Change
alternatives.description: Use Dexie.js as a lightweight, promise-based IndexedDB wrapper. Define schemas for all 8 entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion) as Dexie tables. Queries are written in a fluent chainable API (e.g., db.expenses.where('date').between(...).toArray()). Computed entities like MonthSummary, CategorySpending, and DailySpending are derived at query time via JavaScript aggregation rather than stored redundantly. No build-time code generation required.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency (dexie ~50KB gzipped) — no other runtime deps
IndexedDB is browser-native: zero backend, zero network latency — directly supports <150ms micro-interaction constraint
Schema migrations handled via Dexie's versioned upgrade API; straightforward for greenfield but requires discipline as entities evolve
No TypeScript schema inference out of the box — must manually type entity interfaces (mitigated by dexie@3+ with generic Table<T> support)
Aggregation queries (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) must be computed in JS — acceptable for <10K expense records typical of personal use, but no SQL GROUP BY optimization
Blast radius: 3 files — db.ts (schema definition), 1 repository/service layer file per entity group (~3 files), total ~4 files affected
No ORM-style relations — joins between Expense↔Category↔Budget must be done manually in application code

alternatives.name: Zustand + localStorage with zod validation
alternatives.description: Manage all 8 entities entirely in Zustand stores, persisted to localStorage via the zustand/middleware persist plugin. Each entity type gets a dedicated store slice (expenseStore, categoryStore, budgetStore, settingsStore). Zod schemas enforce data integrity at write time (amount ≤ 99999.99, no future dates, 2 decimal places). Computed entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion) are derived via Zustand selectors or useMemo — never stored. This approach treats the data layer as an extension of existing state management rather than a separate persistence layer.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 dependencies: zustand (~3KB) and zod (~12KB) — both are extremely common in modern React stacks and likely already planned for form validation
localStorage has a 5–10MB storage cap — sufficient for personal expense data (thousands of records) but not unlimited; IndexedDB alternatives handle larger datasets better
Synchronous localStorage reads mean zero async overhead — all reads are instant, directly supporting <150ms interaction budget
No migration strategy built-in — schema changes require manual versioned migration logic in the persist middleware's 'migrate' callback
Zod validation at write time directly enforces the data integrity constraints (amount, date) from the PRD without additional validation libraries
Blast radius: 5–7 files — one store file per entity group (~4 store files), zod schema file, persist config — all greenfield so no existing code disrupted
Data is not queryable with indexed lookups — filtering large expense lists requires full array scans in JS (acceptable for personal-scale data)

alternatives.name: Dexie.js + Dexie React Hooks (live queries)
alternatives.description: Extend alt-01 with Dexie's official React integration (dexie-react-hooks). useLiveQuery() hooks subscribe to IndexedDB changes and automatically re-render components when underlying data changes — eliminating manual cache invalidation. All 8 entities are defined as Dexie tables. Computed aggregates (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed inside useLiveQuery callbacks so the Dashboard and Insights screens always reflect live data. QuickAddSuggestion is derived from Expense frequency analysis within a live query.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 dependencies: dexie (~50KB) and dexie-react-hooks (~5KB) — both from the same maintainer, low supply-chain risk
useLiveQuery re-runs on any relevant table mutation — provides reactivity equivalent to a real-time database without a backend, directly supporting the dashboard's live budget status display
Slightly higher complexity than bare Dexie (alt-01): developers must understand the reactive query model to avoid accidental over-fetching
IndexedDB storage is effectively unlimited for practical personal use (browser quota ~60% of disk) — no 5MB cap like localStorage
Schema versioning via Dexie.version() handles future entity additions cleanly — e.g., adding fields to Expense without data loss
Blast radius: 5–8 files — db.ts schema, ~4 query hook files (one per screen's data needs), optional repository abstraction layer
Requires async data loading pattern (useLiveQuery returns undefined on first render) — components need loading states, adding ~2–3 skeleton UI components

alternatives.name: PGlite (in-browser SQLite via WASM) + Drizzle ORM
alternatives.description: Use PGlite (a WASM-compiled PostgreSQL running entirely in the browser) as the storage engine, with Drizzle ORM providing type-safe schema definitions and query building. All 8 entities are declared as Drizzle table schemas with full relational integrity (foreign keys: Expense→Category, Budget→Category). Computed entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are derived via SQL GROUP BY queries — the most correct and performant approach for aggregations. Data is persisted to IndexedDB via PGlite's persistence adapter.
tradeoffs: Adds 3 dependencies: @electric-sql/pglite (~3MB WASM bundle), drizzle-orm (~45KB), drizzle-kit (dev-only for migrations) — the WASM bundle is the largest cost and may impact initial load time
Full SQL expressiveness: GROUP BY, window functions, and JOINs make MonthSummary/CategorySpending/DailySpending trivial to compute correctly and efficiently
Drizzle provides compile-time type safety — entity interfaces are inferred from schema definitions, eliminating manual TypeScript interface maintenance
PGlite WASM initialization adds ~200–400ms on first load — must be handled with a loading state; subsequent queries are fast (sub-10ms for personal-scale data)
Most complex setup of all alternatives: requires Drizzle migration files, WASM loading strategy, and async DB initialization before any component renders
Overkill for 8 entities and personal-scale data volumes — the SQL power is not needed when JS aggregation over <10K records is fast enough
Blast radius: 8–12 files — schema file, migration files, db initialization module, query modules per screen, Drizzle config — highest blast radius of all options

structured.rationale: All four alternatives are client-side only, consistent with the hard scope exclusion of backend API development. Server-side ORMs (Prisma, TypeORM, Sequelize) were excluded because CashPulse is a frontend-only greenfield app with no backend. Firebase/Supabase were excluded as they require network calls (violating the <150ms interaction budget and the no-backend scope). Redux Persist alone was excluded because it lacks any query capability for the aggregation entities. The four selected alternatives span a spectrum from minimal (Zustand+localStorage) to powerful (PGlite+Drizzle), giving the Architecture Writer a clear cost/benefit tradeoff surface. The 'minimal change' alternative (alt-01, bare Dexie.js) is explicitly included per the rules.
structured.recommendation: alt-03 — Dexie.js + dexie-react-hooks. It hits the sweet spot for CashPulse: IndexedDB storage removes the localStorage 5MB cap, useLiveQuery() eliminates manual cache invalidation (critical for the Dashboard's live budget status), and the 2-dependency footprint is lean. The reactive model maps cleanly onto the three core screens — Dashboard and Insights screens simply declare what data they need and re-render automatically when expenses are added or edited. The async loading pattern adds minor complexity but is well-documented and results in better UX (skeleton states) that aligns with the PRD's polish requirements. PGlite (alt-04) is architecturally elegant but its WASM bundle size and initialization latency are unjustified for 8 entities at personal scale.

alternatives.name: In-Memory Store via Zustand (Client-Side State)
alternatives.description: Use Zustand (or equivalent lightweight state manager) as the single source of truth for all 8 data entities. On app load, fetch all data once and hydrate the store. Subsequent reads (dashboard, insights, chart renders) are synchronous in-memory lookups with zero network latency. Writes update the store optimistically and persist to localStorage for session continuity. No external cache infrastructure required.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: zustand (~3KB gzipped) — or zero if using React Context + useReducer
All data lives in JS heap; large expense histories (1000+ records) may increase memory pressure but are unlikely for a personal tracker
localStorage persistence adds ~2ms write overhead per mutation — negligible for the 150ms micro-interaction budget
No TTL/invalidation logic needed since this is a single-user, desktop-only app with no concurrent writers
Cold load requires one full fetch; subsequent navigations between Dashboard, Add Expense, and Insights are instant (0ms) — directly enables the 350ms screen transition target
Blast radius: 3–5 files (store definition, entity slices for Expense/Category/Budget/MonthSummary/QuickAddSuggestion, and a single data-loader hook)

alternatives.name: HTTP Cache via React Query (stale-while-revalidate)
alternatives.description: Use TanStack Query (React Query) to manage server-state caching with a stale-while-revalidate strategy. Each entity group (expenses, categories, budgets, summaries) gets a dedicated query key. Data is served from the in-memory query cache on repeat navigations (0ms), revalidated in the background. Mutations use optimistic updates with automatic rollback on failure. Pairs naturally with any REST or mock API layer.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: @tanstack/react-query (~13KB gzipped) — largest bundle cost of all alternatives
Requires wrapping the app in QueryClientProvider — 1 file change at the root
staleTime configuration per entity type needed: QuickAddSuggestions (5 min), MonthSummary/CategorySpending (1 min), Expenses (30s) — adds ~20 lines of config
Devtools add ~40KB in development but zero in production
Optimistic updates for Add Expense flow require rollback logic (~30 lines per mutation) — adds complexity but directly supports the 15-second form completion target
Background refetch may cause chart re-renders mid-animation; must gate chart entry animations behind data-stable state
Blast radius: 6–9 files (QueryClient config, 5 query hooks per entity group, 2–3 mutation hooks for Expense CRUD)

alternatives.name: Minimal Change: localStorage as Primary Data Store (No External Cache)
alternatives.description: Since backend API development is explicitly out of scope, treat localStorage as the authoritative data store for all 8 entities. Reads are synchronous (~0.1ms for typical personal-tracker data volumes). Writes are synchronous and immediately consistent. No network layer, no cache invalidation, no dependencies. A thin repository abstraction (getExpenses, saveExpense, etc.) wraps localStorage JSON serialization. This is the minimal-blast-radius option.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies — uses only browser-native Web Storage API
Synchronous reads guarantee all micro-interactions stay well within 150ms budget
localStorage is limited to ~5MB per origin; at ~500 bytes per expense record, supports ~10,000 expenses — more than sufficient for a personal tracker
No TTL or invalidation complexity — single writer (the user), single reader (the app)
Data is not portable across browsers/devices, but cloud sync is explicitly out of scope
Requires a serialization/deserialization layer for Date fields (ISO strings) — ~10 lines of utility code
Chart data (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) must be derived/computed on read — adds ~50 lines of selector logic but avoids stale derived-data bugs
Blast radius: 2–3 files (storage utility module, entity repository module, optional React context for reactivity)

alternatives.name: IndexedDB with In-Memory Read Cache (Future-Proof)
alternatives.description: Use IndexedDB (via the idb wrapper library) as the persistent store with an in-memory read-through cache layer. On app init, warm the cache by loading the current month's data into memory. All reads hit the in-memory cache (synchronous). Writes go to IndexedDB asynchronously and update the cache. This supports larger data volumes and enables future features like offline-first or background sync without architectural rework.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: idb (~1.1KB gzipped) for a promise-based IndexedDB API
IndexedDB schema migration logic required (versioned upgrades) — adds ~30 lines of setup code
Async write path (IndexedDB) means optimistic UI updates are mandatory to meet 150ms interaction budget — adds rollback complexity
Cache warming on cold load adds ~10–20ms vs localStorage (~1ms) — still well within 350ms screen transition budget
Supports up to hundreds of MB of data — significant over-engineering for a personal expense tracker with typical 1–3 years of data
StorageEvent does not fire for IndexedDB; cross-tab reactivity requires BroadcastChannel API (~15 lines) — relevant only if user opens multiple tabs
Blast radius: 5–8 files (db schema/migration, entity repositories x5, cache manager, optional BroadcastChannel handler)

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency minimal to future-proof scalable, all implementable without a backend (which is explicitly out of scope). Redis was excluded because it requires server infrastructure — incompatible with the scope constraint. HTTP cache headers (Cache-Control, ETag) were excluded because there is no server to set them. Service Worker caching was excluded because the app is desktop-only with no offline requirement stated. The four selected alternatives cover: (1) reactive client state management, (2) server-state caching patterns for when a real API is introduced, (3) the absolute minimal-change path using only browser primitives, and (4) a future-proof local persistence option. All four satisfy the hard WCAG constraint (caching is UI-layer agnostic) and all soft performance constraints, since even the slowest option (IndexedDB cold load) stays within the 350ms screen transition budget.
structured.recommendation: alt-3-localstorage-only for immediate implementation, with a planned migration path to alt-2-react-query-http-cache when a real API is introduced. Rationale: Backend API development is explicitly out of scope, making localStorage the natural authoritative store. It has zero dependencies, synchronous reads that trivially satisfy all performance budgets, and a blast radius of only 2–3 files. The thin repository abstraction (getExpenses, saveExpense) can be swapped for React Query hooks later with no changes to UI components — preserving the investment. alt-1 (Zustand) is a close second but adds a dependency without meaningful benefit over localStorage for a single-user, no-backend app. alt-4 (IndexedDB) is over-engineered for the stated data volumes and scope.

alternatives.name: Browser LocalStorage with In-Memory State (No Backend)
alternatives.description: All expense, category, budget, and settings data is persisted entirely in the browser's LocalStorage using a structured JSON schema. A lightweight state manager (e.g., Zustand or React Context + useReducer) holds in-memory state during the session, syncing to LocalStorage on every mutation. No payment provider or external API is involved — the app is fully client-side. This directly aligns with the hard scope-exclusion constraints (no backend API, no bank integration, no cloud sync). Data entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion) are serialized as keyed JSON blobs. Aggregations (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed client-side on read.
tradeoffs: Zero external dependencies added — no SDK, no API client library
Data is device-local only; clearing browser storage destroys all data (acceptable per scope-exclusion: no cloud sync)
LocalStorage is synchronous and limited to ~5MB; with typical expense volumes (thousands of records) this is sufficient but not infinite
Aggregation logic (MonthSummary, CategorySpending) must be implemented in-app — adds ~3-5 utility modules
No payment processing capability whatsoever — gap title is a misnomer for this scope; this is the correct interpretation given all scope-exclusions
QuickAddSuggestion (entity-008) frequency scoring must be computed client-side on every form load
Affected modules: state store (1), storage adapter (1), 5 aggregation utilities, all 7 screen components that read/write data = ~14 files total
references: PRD scope-exclusion: Backend API development out of scope
PRD scope-exclusion: Data backup, sync, or cloud storage out of scope

alternatives.name: IndexedDB via Dexie.js (Structured Client-Side Database)
alternatives.description: All 8 data entities are stored in an IndexedDB database managed through Dexie.js, a lightweight IndexedDB wrapper (~23KB gzipped). Each entity maps to a Dexie table with typed indexes (e.g., Expense indexed by date, categoryId; Budget indexed by categoryId). Queries are async and non-blocking, supporting efficient range queries for date-filtered dashboard views and chart aggregations without loading all records into memory. No backend, no payment provider — fully client-side. Dexie's liveQuery enables reactive UI updates when data changes.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: dexie (~23KB gzipped) — no other dependencies required
Async API requires all data-access code to be async/await — affects all 7 screen components and any synchronous utility functions
IndexedDB storage limit is browser-managed (typically hundreds of MB) — far exceeds LocalStorage and handles years of expense data
Dexie.js liveQuery enables reactive updates but requires wrapping components in useLiveQuery hook — adds ~7 hook integrations across screens
Schema migrations must be versioned in Dexie — adds 1 migration module; breaking changes require version bumps
Better performance than LocalStorage for large datasets: O(log n) indexed lookups vs O(n) JSON.parse scans
Affected modules: Dexie schema/db module (1), migration module (1), 7 screen data-access hooks, 5 aggregation queries = ~14 files
references: PRD scope-exclusion: Backend API development out of scope

alternatives.name: Mock REST API via MSW (Mock Service Worker) — Future-Backend-Ready
alternatives.description: All data operations are expressed as fetch() calls to a conventional REST API (e.g., GET /api/expenses, POST /api/expenses, GET /api/insights/monthly). Mock Service Worker (MSW) intercepts these requests at the Service Worker level and returns realistic mock responses from an in-memory store seeded with fixture data. This approach keeps the frontend API contract clean and backend-ready: when a real backend is eventually built, only the MSW handlers are removed — no application code changes. Data is not persisted across page refreshes in this variant (session-only), but MSW handlers can optionally write to LocalStorage for persistence.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new dependencies: msw (~50KB dev, ~20KB prod service worker) and optionally a fetch client like ky or axios (~8KB)
Requires registering a Service Worker (mockServiceWorker.js) — adds 1 file to public/ directory and 1 MSW setup module
All 25 features must have corresponding MSW handler definitions — adds ~10-15 handler functions across 3-4 handler files
Session-only data by default (no persistence) unless handlers are wired to LocalStorage — requires additional ~200 lines of handler persistence logic
Highest architectural overhead of all alternatives for a greenfield scope-excluded-backend project
Best choice if backend development is planned within 6 months; worst choice if app remains client-only permanently
Affected modules: MSW setup (1), handler files (4), service worker (1), API client module (1), all 7 screen data hooks refactored to fetch = ~14 files
references: PRD scope-exclusion: Backend API development out of scope (but this pattern prepares for future backend)

alternatives.name: Minimal LocalStorage Adapter (Thin Wrapper, No Framework)
alternatives.description: A single ~100-line StorageAdapter module wraps LocalStorage reads/writes with typed get/set/delete helpers keyed by entity type and ID. No state management library is added — React's built-in useState/useReducer and useContext handle in-memory state. This is the minimal-change, zero-new-dependency approach. Each screen component reads from context on mount and writes through dispatch actions that synchronously persist to LocalStorage. Aggregation for MonthSummary, CategorySpending, and DailySpending is computed via pure functions called at render time, memoized with useMemo to stay within the 600ms chart render constraint.
tradeoffs: Zero new runtime dependencies — uses only browser APIs and React built-ins already in the stack
StorageAdapter is a single file (~100 lines) — lowest blast radius of all alternatives
Context re-renders on every expense mutation may cause performance issues at scale (>500 expenses) — mitigated by useMemo and selective context splitting
No reactive query system — components must manually trigger re-reads after mutations; risk of stale UI if not carefully managed
Aggregation functions run synchronously on render — with large datasets could approach the 600ms chart render budget; acceptable for personal-scale data
Easiest to understand and debug — no abstraction layers, no async complexity
Affected modules: StorageAdapter (1), AppContext/store (1), ~7 screen components updated to use context = ~9 files total

structured.rationale: The gap is labeled 'payment processing provider' but the PRD's hard scope-exclusions explicitly rule out backend API development, bank/card integration, and any real payment processing. CashPulse is a visual expense *tracker*, not a payment processor. Therefore, all alternatives are correctly scoped to client-side data persistence strategies — the real architectural decision is how expense, budget, and settings data is stored and queried in the browser. Alternatives were selected to cover the full spectrum from zero-dependency minimal (alt-localstorage-minimal) to structured async database (alt-indexeddb) to future-backend-ready mock API (alt-mock-api-msw). Server-side databases (PostgreSQL, Firebase, Supabase) were excluded because backend API development is a hard scope-exclusion. Payment SDKs (Stripe, Braintree) were excluded as entirely irrelevant to the PRD's stated purpose. React Query / TanStack Query was considered but excluded as it adds dependency weight without benefit when there is no real network layer.
structured.recommendation: alt-localstorage-minimal for immediate implementation. CashPulse is explicitly scoped to visual and interaction design with no backend, no sync, and no multi-user support. The minimal LocalStorage adapter satisfies all data entity persistence needs with zero new dependencies, the lowest blast radius (~9 files), and the simplest mental model for a greenfield desktop-only personal tracker. If the project roadmap later includes a backend, migrating from a thin StorageAdapter to real API calls is a contained refactor. If data volume or query complexity becomes a concern, upgrading to alt-indexeddb (Dexie.js) is a single-dependency swap affecting the same ~9 files.
