alternatives.name: In-App Toast / Snackbar Notification System (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: Implement a lightweight, self-contained in-app notification layer using a React context + portal pattern. Toasts appear in a fixed overlay region (bottom-right, 16px inset) and are triggered by UI events (expense saved, budget threshold crossed, delete confirmed/reverted). No external service, no browser APIs, no backend required. State is managed in a NotificationContext that any component can push messages to. Toasts auto-dismiss after 4 seconds with a visible countdown progress bar, and support manual dismiss via keyboard (Escape) and click. All toasts meet WCAG 2.1 AA: role='status' or role='alert' for urgent messages, minimum 36×36px dismiss button, 2px blue-600 focus ring, and color is never the sole indicator (icon + text label always present). Respects prefers-reduced-motion by skipping slide-in animation and rendering instantly.
tradeoffs: Zero new runtime dependencies — implemented entirely with React context, portals, and CSS transitions already in the stack
Blast radius limited to ~4 files: NotificationContext.tsx, ToastContainer.tsx, useNotification.ts hook, and global CSS token additions
No persistence: toasts are ephemeral and lost on page refresh — not suitable for missed-action recovery
Cannot deliver notifications when the app tab is not active or the browser is closed
Budget threshold alerts are reactive only (shown when user is already in the app), not proactive
Requires discipline across all 7 screens to call the notification hook consistently — risk of inconsistent UX if adoption is partial
Push notifications and email alerts are explicitly out of scope per constraints, making this the only in-scope delivery channel

alternatives.name: Contextual Inline Banner Notifications
alternatives.description: Surface notifications as inline banners anchored directly to the relevant UI section rather than a floating overlay. For example, a budget-exceeded warning renders as a dismissible amber banner inside the Budget card on the Dashboard; a successful expense save renders a green confirmation strip at the top of the Add Expense form. Banners are part of the normal document flow, making them inherently accessible (no z-index stacking, no focus trap complexity) and naturally responsive across all three breakpoints. Each banner is a stateless component receiving props (severity, message, onDismiss) — no global context needed. Severity levels: info (blue-600), warning (amber-500), error (red-600), success (green-600) — all accompanied by text labels and icons so color is never the sole indicator.
tradeoffs: Adds ~3 new component files: InlineBanner.tsx, useBannerState.ts, and banner CSS tokens — no new dependencies

Blast radius: ~6–9 files affected — each screen (Dashboard, AddExpense, Insights, and sub-cards) needs to conditionally render banners, touching BudgetCard, ExpenseForm, InsightsSummary, and CategoryBreakdown
More visually disruptive than toasts — banners shift layout, potentially violating the 250ms layout transition constraint if not pre-allocated with min-height
Cannot aggregate multiple simultaneous notifications — each section shows at most one banner, so concurrent alerts (e.g., two categories over budget) require prioritization logic
Excellent for persistent, contextual warnings (e.g., budget status) that should remain visible until resolved — superior to toasts for non-ephemeral states
No animation required for reduced-motion compliance — banners are static layout elements by default
Requires pre-allocating vertical space in card layouts to avoid layout shift (CLS impact)
references: WAI-ARIA role='region' with aria-label for banner landmarks
Nielsen Norman Group — Inline vs. Toast Notifications — https://www.nngroup.com/articles/toast-notifications/

alternatives.name: Persistent Notification Center (Bell Icon + Drawer)
alternatives.description: Introduce a notification center accessible via a bell icon in the top navigation bar. Clicking the bell opens a slide-in drawer (or popover) listing recent system events: expense added, budget threshold reached (>80%, >100%), expense deleted with undo window, and monthly summary ready. Notifications are stored in localStorage (keyed to a session or user ID stub) so they persist across page refreshes within the same browser session. A badge counter on the bell icon shows unread count. The drawer is a focus-trapped modal-like panel with full keyboard navigation (Tab cycles through items, Escape closes). Each notification item includes a timestamp, icon, and text — no color-only indicators. The bell icon meets the 36×36px minimum click target. Drawer open/close animation completes within 250ms; respects prefers-reduced-motion with instant cut.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency if using a headless UI library for the focus-trapped drawer (e.g., @radix-ui/react-dialog ~45KB gzipped), or ~120 lines of custom focus-trap logic if avoided

Blast radius: ~8–12 files — NotificationCenter.tsx, NotificationDrawer.tsx, NotificationItem.tsx, NotificationBadge.tsx, useNotificationStore.ts, localStorage adapter, NavBar.tsx (modified), and global type definitions
localStorage has a 5MB cap and no TTL — requires manual pruning logic to avoid stale notification accumulation over time
Notifications are browser-local only — clearing localStorage or switching browsers loses history; no cross-device sync (out of scope per constraints)
Highest implementation complexity of all alternatives — focus trap, badge counter, read/unread state, and localStorage serialization all need to be built and tested
Provides the richest UX for power users who perform multiple actions quickly and want a reviewable audit trail of recent events
Push notifications and email alerts remain out of scope — this is still a purely in-app channel
references: WCAG 2.1 SC 2.1.1 Keyboard, SC 2.4.3 Focus Order

alternatives.name: Hybrid: Ephemeral Toasts + Persistent Inline Budget Banners
alternatives.description: Combine alt-01 and alt-02 into a two-tier notification strategy matched to notification urgency and persistence needs. Tier 1 — Ephemeral toasts (bottom-right overlay, 4s auto-dismiss): used for transient confirmations like 'Expense saved', 'Expense deleted — Undo' (with 3-second revert per data integrity constraint), and 'Category updated'. Tier 2 — Persistent inline banners: used exclusively for budget threshold states (>80% warning, >100% exceeded) anchored inside the BudgetCard and CategoryBreakdown components. Budget banners remain visible until the underlying budget state resolves — they are not dismissible by the user, reinforcing the financial data's importance. This separation of concerns means toasts handle action feedback (ephemeral) while banners handle state feedback (persistent), matching established UX notification taxonomy. A single NotificationContext drives toasts; budget banner visibility is derived directly from Budget and CategorySpending entity state — no separate notification store needed.
tradeoffs: Adds ~5–6 new files: ToastContext.tsx, ToastContainer.tsx, Toast.tsx, InlineBudgetBanner.tsx, useToast.ts — no new npm dependencies if CSS transitions are used natively
Blast radius: ~7–10 files — moderate, touching NavBar (none), AddExpense form, Dashboard BudgetCard, CategoryBreakdown, InsightsSummary, and global CSS
Two distinct notification patterns increase cognitive surface area for future developers maintaining the codebase — requires clear documentation of which tier to use when
Budget banners derived from entity state (not a notification store) means zero persistence complexity — they are always accurate and never stale
Toast + banner combination is the industry-standard pattern for financial dashboards (Mint, YNAB, Copilot) — high familiarity for users
Fully satisfies the 3-second delete auto-revert constraint via a toast with a visible countdown and 'Undo' CTA button
Both tiers independently respect prefers-reduced-motion: toasts render instantly, banners are static — no additional conditional logic needed
references: Nielsen Norman Group — Visibility of System Status (Heuristic #1) — https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ten-usability-heuristics/

structured.rationale: These four alternatives were selected because they are the only notification delivery channels implementable entirely within the frontend scope defined by the PRD. Push notifications and email alerts are explicitly excluded by a hard scope-exclusion constraint. Browser Notification API (Web Push) was excluded because it requires a backend service worker registration endpoint and VAPID key infrastructure — both out of scope. SMS/webhook channels were excluded for the same reason. Server-Sent Events and WebSockets were excluded as they require backend API development, which is explicitly out of scope. The four alternatives span the full blast-radius spectrum from minimal (alt-01, ~4 files) to high (alt-03, ~12 files), giving the Architecture Writer a clear cost/benefit gradient. The hybrid alt-04 was included because the PRD's data integrity constraint (3-second delete auto-revert) and budget threshold visibility needs are qualitatively different notification types that benefit from different delivery mechanisms — a single-tier system forces an awkward compromise on one or the other.
structured.recommendation: alt-04-hybrid-toast-banner — The hybrid approach is the strongest fit for CashPulse's specific notification needs. The 3-second delete auto-revert constraint (data integrity) is a natural toast use case with a countdown + Undo CTA. Budget threshold warnings are persistent state conditions that should remain visible until resolved — inline banners derived directly from Budget/CategorySpending entity state handle this with zero persistence complexity and zero staleness risk. The combined blast radius (~7–10 files) is moderate and justified by the clean separation of ephemeral action feedback (toasts) from persistent state feedback (banners). No new npm dependencies are required, keeping the bundle lean. Both tiers independently satisfy WCAG 2.1 AA and prefers-reduced-motion requirements without additional conditional logic.

alternatives.name: Client-Side In-Memory Filtering (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: All expense data is loaded once into memory on app initialization (or per-screen mount) and filtered/searched entirely on the client using JavaScript array methods. A single `useExpenseStore` hook holds the canonical list; filter predicates (by category, date range, amount range, keyword) are composed and applied reactively on each render cycle. No additional API endpoints or query parameters are needed — the existing data-fetch call for the Dashboard and Insights screens already retrieves the full Expense list.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies — uses native Array.filter/sort already available in the stack
Sub-5ms filter latency for datasets up to ~2,000 expenses, well within the 150ms micro-interaction budget
Blast radius is minimal: 2–3 files affected (expenseStore, ExpenseList component, FilterBar component)
Does not scale beyond ~5,000 records before noticeable jank; acceptable for a single-user personal tracker with realistic data volumes
Full dataset must be in memory — initial page load payload grows linearly with expense history (mitigated by pagination or date-window pre-filtering)
No server-side filtering means the backend (out of scope) cannot optimize queries, but this is acceptable given the scope-exclusion constraint on backend API development

alternatives.name: URL Query-Parameter-Driven Filtering with React Router
alternatives.description: Filter state (category, dateFrom, dateTo, amountMin, amountMax, keyword) is serialized into URL search params via React Router's `useSearchParams`. The data layer reads these params and applies filtering either client-side (same predicate logic as Alt-1) or passes them as query strings to a future API. This makes filter state bookmarkable, shareable, and survives page refresh — a meaningful UX improvement for the Insights and Dashboard screens. FilterBar components read/write params; no new state management library is required.
tradeoffs: Adds React Router `useSearchParams` usage — 1 new hook pattern, no new package if React Router is already in the stack (standard for greenfield React apps)
Blast radius: 4–6 files (FilterBar, ExpenseList, InsightsScreen, DashboardScreen, router config, expenseStore/selector)
Filter state is automatically shareable and survives hard refresh — improves usability for power users reviewing Insights
URL length grows with complex filter combinations but stays well within browser limits for this domain
Requires discipline to keep URL params as the single source of truth — local component state must not shadow params, adding a coordination constraint
Screen transition animations (350ms budget) must account for URL-change-triggered re-renders; requires memoized selectors to avoid layout thrash
Future-proof: if a backend API is added later, query params map directly to API query strings with zero refactoring

alternatives.name: Dedicated Search Index with Fuse.js Fuzzy Search
alternatives.description: Integrate Fuse.js, a lightweight (~24KB gzipped) fuzzy-search library, to build an in-memory search index over the Expense entity's text fields (description, category name, merchant). Structured filters (date range, amount, category chip selections) are applied as hard predicates before or after the fuzzy pass. This enables typo-tolerant keyword search — e.g., 'Starbcks' matching 'Starbucks' — which meaningfully improves the search UX on the Dashboard and Insights screens without any backend dependency.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: Fuse.js (~24KB gzipped) — must be evaluated against bundle size budget
Blast radius: 4–5 files (expenseStore/searchIndex, FilterBar, ExpenseList, InsightsScreen, package.json)
Index rebuild is O(n) on data change — for <2,000 records this is <10ms, safely within the 150ms interaction budget
Fuzzy matching introduces relevance scoring complexity: result ordering may feel non-deterministic to users expecting exact prefix matches
Fuse.js configuration (threshold, keys, weights) requires tuning per field — description vs. category name should have different weights
Overkill if the primary search pattern is category-chip filtering rather than free-text keyword search; validate with UX research before committing
No accessibility impact — search input already requires standard ARIA labeling regardless of underlying engine

alternatives.name: Zustand Slice with Derived Selector Filtering
alternatives.description: Centralize all filter state in a dedicated Zustand `filterSlice` (category[], dateRange, amountRange, keyword). Filtered expense lists are exposed as memoized derived selectors using Zustand's `useStore` with a selector function, preventing unnecessary re-renders. This pattern cleanly separates raw data from view-layer filter state, supports cross-screen filter persistence (e.g., a category filter set on Dashboard persists when navigating to Insights), and integrates naturally with the existing store if Zustand is already chosen as the state management solution.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency if Zustand is not already in the stack; zero additional cost if it is (common greenfield choice)
Blast radius: 5–7 files (filterSlice, expenseSlice, DashboardScreen, InsightsScreen, FilterBar, ExpenseList, store index)
Memoized selectors ensure filter operations only re-run when filter state or expense data changes — directly supports the 150ms micro-interaction budget for chip selections
Cross-screen filter persistence is a double-edged sword: users may be confused when Insights shows a filtered view they set on Dashboard; requires explicit 'Clear Filters' affordance
Zustand's devtools integration makes filter state debuggable in Redux DevTools — good for development velocity
Requires upfront slice architecture design; retrofitting later if a simpler approach was chosen first costs 1–2 days of refactoring
Does not add fuzzy search or URL persistence — those concerns remain orthogonal and can be layered on top

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency minimal change to a structured state-management architecture, all implementable without a backend API (which is explicitly out of scope). Server-side search (Elasticsearch, Algolia, PostgreSQL full-text) was excluded because backend API development is a hard scope exclusion. React Query / SWR with server filtering was excluded for the same reason. IndexedDB-based offline search was excluded as overkill for a single-user desktop web app with realistic expense volumes (hundreds to low thousands of records). The four selected alternatives cover: (1) the simplest possible implementation, (2) URL-driven stateful filtering for UX quality, (3) fuzzy text search for power users, and (4) a clean state architecture pattern — giving the Architecture Writer a clear cost/benefit ladder to choose from.
structured.recommendation: alt-2 — URL Query-Parameter-Driven Filtering with React Router. For a personal expense tracker, filter state that survives page refresh and is bookmarkable is a meaningful UX win at very low cost. React Router is a near-certain dependency in any greenfield React app, so this adds zero new packages. The blast radius (4–6 files) is manageable, and the URL-param pattern future-proofs the architecture for a backend API without any refactoring. If fuzzy keyword search proves valuable after user testing, Fuse.js (alt-3) can be layered on top of this approach without conflict. Alt-4 (Zustand slice) is the right complement for global state management but is orthogonal to the search strategy decision itself.

adrs.title: Client-Side Data Persistence via localStorage + Zustand
adrs.decision: Use Zustand as the centralized reactive state manager with localStorage persistence for all 8 data entities. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion) are computed via memoized Zustand selectors, never stored.
adrs.rationale: CashPulse is a single-user, no-backend, desktop-web-only personal tracker. Zustand adds only ~3KB, provides centralized reactive state with selector memoization critical for the Insights screen's derived aggregations, and satisfies every performance constraint with synchronous reads. localStorage persistence is ~40 lines of code. IndexedDB (Dexie) was considered but adds async complexity and ~50KB for a data volume that will never exceed a few thousand records. sql.js WASM (~1.5MB) is unjustified. Raw localStorage without a state manager risks prop-drilling and unmemoized re-renders across 7 screens.
alternatives: alt-2: IndexedDB via Dexie.js — structured indexes but async overhead and 50KB bundle unjustified at personal scale
alt-3: SQLite via sql.js (WASM) — full SQL power but 1.5MB bundle and no reactivity
alt-4: Raw localStorage with no state library — zero deps but no centralized state or selector memoization

adrs.title: Styling via Tailwind CSS v3 + CVA + Radix UI Primitives
adrs.decision: Adopt Tailwind CSS v3 as the styling layer with Class Variance Authority (CVA) for typed component variants and Radix UI headless primitives for accessible interactive widgets (Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip). Design tokens are codified in tailwind.config.js. shadcn/ui components are vendored into the project for accessible primitives styled with Tailwind.
adrs.rationale: The hard WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility constraint (keyboard navigation, focus trapping, ARIA management) is the deciding factor. Radix UI primitives solve this definitively for Dialog (Settings), Popover (Expense Detail), Select (Category Filter, Currency), and Tooltip (Chart hover). CVA enforces the strict design token system at the TypeScript API level, preventing drift across 7 screens. Tailwind's static extraction guarantees zero runtime CSS overhead, meeting the 150ms micro-interaction budget. styled-components was rejected for runtime injection overhead. CSS Modules lack co-located responsive variants. MUI requires extensive theme overrides to match CashPulse's sharp aesthetic.
alternatives: alt-css-modules: CSS Modules + Custom Properties — zero deps but no co-located responsive variants and no accessible primitives
alt-tailwind: Plain Tailwind without CVA — no typed variant enforcement
alt-styled-components: styled-components — runtime CSS injection risks 150ms budget on chart-heavy screens

adrs.title: Component Library: shadcn/ui (Vendored Radix + Tailwind)
adrs.decision: Use shadcn/ui as the component foundation. Components are copied into the project as owned source files, not imported from npm. Radix UI primitives provide WCAG 2.1 AA keyboard/ARIA compliance. Recharts is added separately for chart components on the Insights screen.
adrs.rationale: shadcn/ui uniquely satisfies the hard accessibility constraint via Radix primitives while giving full design ownership — every pixel maps to CashPulse tokens via Tailwind utilities. Zero runtime bundle overhead since components are vendored source. No design-opinion lock-in unlike Mantine or MUI. The only gap is charts, which Recharts fills with accessible defaults (role='img', aria-label). MUI was rejected for its Material Design override debt (~200-400 lines) and Emotion runtime. Mantine was rejected for its 120KB+ bundle and CSS class conflicts with Tailwind.
alternatives: alt-mantine: Mantine v7 — ships DatePicker and Charts but 120KB+ bundle and design override debt
alt-mui: MUI v6 — largest ecosystem but Material Design overrides and Emotion runtime
alt-custom-headless: Custom + bare Radix — maximum control but 30-40% more dev time for DatePicker, NumberInput

adrs.title: Data Access Pattern: Zustand Store Slices with Zod Validation
adrs.decision: Use Zustand store slices as the data access layer with Zod schemas enforcing data integrity at write time. Each entity group (expenses, categories, budget, settings) gets a dedicated store slice. Derived entities are computed via memoized selectors. No ORM is used — the data layer is a thin typed abstraction over localStorage-persisted Zustand state.
adrs.rationale: Traditional ORMs (Prisma, Drizzle, TypeORM) require a server-side database, which is out of scope. Client-side alternatives like Dexie.js add async complexity unjustified for personal-scale data. Zustand slices with Zod validation provide type-safe writes (amount ≤ 99999.99, no future dates, 2 decimal places), centralized reactive state, and memoized selectors for derived aggregations. The pattern is simple, debuggable, and migrateable to a real API layer later.
alternatives: alt-01: Dexie.js — IndexedDB wrapper with async queries, adds 50KB for unnecessary structured indexing
alt-03: Dexie.js + React Hooks — reactive queries but async loading states add skeleton UI complexity
alt-04: PGlite + Drizzle ORM — full SQL in browser but 3MB WASM bundle is unjustified

adrs.title: Caching Strategy: localStorage as Primary Store (No External Cache)
adrs.decision: Use localStorage as the authoritative data store with synchronous reads. No external caching layer (Redis, HTTP cache, React Query) is needed. A thin repository abstraction wraps localStorage JSON serialization. Derived data (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) is computed on read via memoized selectors.
adrs.rationale: Backend API development is explicitly out of scope, making localStorage the natural authoritative store. Synchronous reads trivially satisfy all performance budgets (150ms micro-interactions, 350ms transitions). The 5MB localStorage cap supports ~10,000 expense records — more than sufficient for a personal tracker. React Query adds 13KB without benefit when there is no network layer. IndexedDB is over-engineered for the stated data volumes.
alternatives: alt-1-in-memory-zustand: Zustand only (no persistence) — data lost on refresh
alt-2-react-query-http-cache: React Query — 13KB dependency with no network layer to cache
alt-4-indexeddb-with-cache-layer: IndexedDB + cache — over-engineered for personal-scale data

adrs.title: Notification Strategy: Hybrid Toasts + Inline Budget Banners
adrs.decision: Implement a two-tier notification system. Tier 1: Ephemeral toasts (bottom-center, auto-dismiss) for transient action confirmations (expense saved, deleted with undo, currency updated). Tier 2: Persistent inline banners inside BudgetCard for budget threshold states (>80% warning, >100% exceeded). Budget banners are derived from entity state, not a notification store.
adrs.rationale: The PRD specifies distinct notification types: ephemeral confirmations (toast for 'Currency updated to EUR', '✓ Saved!') and persistent budget warnings (On Track / Heads Up / Over Budget badges). The hybrid approach matches each type to its natural delivery mechanism. Toasts handle the 3-second delete auto-revert constraint with a countdown + Undo CTA. Budget banners derived from entity state are always accurate and never stale. No new npm dependencies required. A full notification center (bell icon + drawer) is over-engineered for a personal tracker with no push notifications.
alternatives: alt-01-in-app-toast: Toasts only — budget warnings would auto-dismiss and be missed
alt-02-inline-banner: Banners only — ephemeral confirmations would cause layout shift
alt-03-notification-center: Bell + drawer — over-engineered for single-user personal tracker

adrs.title: Search and Filtering: URL Query-Parameter-Driven with React Router
adrs.decision: Serialize filter state (category, date range, keyword) into URL search params via React Router's useSearchParams. Filtering is performed client-side on the in-memory Zustand store. Filter state survives page refresh and is bookmarkable.
adrs.rationale: URL-driven filtering provides meaningful UX value (bookmarkable, refresh-safe) at zero additional dependency cost since React Router is already in the stack. The blast radius (4-6 files) is manageable. Client-side filtering over Zustand state is sub-5ms for personal-scale data. Fuse.js fuzzy search (24KB) can be layered on later if user testing validates the need. The URL-param pattern also future-proofs for a backend API — query params map directly to API query strings.
alternatives: alt-1: In-memory Array.filter only — no URL persistence, filter lost on refresh
alt-3: Fuse.js fuzzy search — 24KB dependency, overkill unless free-text search is validated
alt-4: Zustand filter slice — good complement but orthogonal to URL persistence

adrs.title: Payment Provider Gap: Not Applicable — Client-Side Data Only
adrs.decision: No payment provider is integrated. CashPulse is an expense tracker, not a payment processor. All expense data (including payment method labels like 'Visa •••4821') is user-entered text stored in localStorage via Zustand. The payment method field is an enum selector (Cash, Debit Card, Credit Card, UPI/Other) with no external integration.
adrs.rationale: The PRD explicitly excludes backend API development, bank/card integration, and automatic transaction import. The 'payment provider' gap is a misnomer for this scope — CashPulse records payment method as a user-selected label, not a financial transaction. No SDK, no API client, no external service is needed.
alternatives: alt-indexeddb: IndexedDB for structured storage — rejected as over-engineered for this scope
alt-mock-api-msw: MSW mock API — adds complexity without near-term backend plans

decisions.rationale: localStorage + Zustand is the pragmatic choice for a single-user, no-backend, desktop-web personal tracker. Zustand (~3KB) provides centralized reactive state with selector memoization critical for derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending). Synchronous reads satisfy all performance constraints trivially. The 5MB localStorage cap supports ~50,000 expense records. Dexie.js adds unnecessary async complexity; sql.js WASM is a 1.5MB bundle penalty; raw localStorage lacks centralized state management.

Tailwind CSS v3 + CVA + Radix UI primitives satisfies the hard WCAG 2.1 AA constraint via battle-tested accessible primitives, enforces design tokens at the TypeScript API level via CVA variants, and has zero runtime CSS overhead via static extraction. The motion-reduce: variant natively handles prefers-reduced-motion. styled-components was rejected for runtime injection overhead; CSS Modules lack co-located responsive variants.

shadcn/ui provides WCAG 2.1 AA compliant Radix primitives (Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip) with zero runtime bundle overhead since components are vendored source. Full design ownership via Tailwind utilities maps precisely to CashPulse tokens. Recharts fills the chart gap separately. MUI and Mantine were rejected for design-opinion override debt and larger bundle sizes.

Zustand + localStorage with Zod validation is the natural data access pattern given the data-store decision (ADR-001). Zod schemas enforce data integrity at write time (amount ≤ 99999.99, no future dates, 2 decimal places) directly from the PRD constraints. No ORM is needed — traditional ORMs require server-side databases which are out of scope. Dexie.js adds async complexity; PGlite + Drizzle adds a 3MB WASM bundle.

With no backend API, localStorage is the authoritative store. Synchronous reads satisfy all performance budgets. The thin repository abstraction (getExpenses, saveExpense) can be swapped for React Query hooks later if a backend is introduced. React Query adds 13KB without benefit when there is no network layer. IndexedDB is over-engineered for personal-scale data volumes.

CashPulse is an expense tracker, not a payment processor. The PRD explicitly excludes backend API development, bank integration, and automatic transaction import. Payment method is a user-selected enum label (Cash, Debit Card, Credit Card, UPI/Other) stored as a string in localStorage. The minimal localStorage adapter with zero new dependencies is the correct interpretation of this gap given all scope exclusions.

The PRD specifies both ephemeral confirmations (expense saved, currency updated) and persistent budget warnings (On Track / Heads Up / Over Budget). The hybrid approach matches each notification type to its natural delivery mechanism. Toasts handle the 3-second delete auto-revert with countdown + Undo. Budget banners derived from entity state are always accurate. No new npm dependencies. A notification center is over-engineered for a personal tracker.

URL query-parameter-driven filtering provides bookmarkable, refresh-safe filter state at zero additional dependency cost (React Router is already in the stack). Client-side filtering over Zustand state is sub-5ms for personal-scale data. Fuse.js fuzzy search can be layered on later if validated. The URL-param pattern future-proofs for a backend API.

implementationPatterns.title: Zustand Store Slices with localStorage Persistence
implementationPatterns.rule: All data reads and writes go through Zustand store slices. Each entity group (expenses, categories, budget, settings) has a dedicated slice. Use zustand/middleware persist with localStorage as the storage engine. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion) are computed via memoized selectors using Zustand's selector pattern — never stored. Store actions are the only write path; direct localStorage manipulation outside the store is prohibited.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-store and gap-orm-strategy decisions. Centralizes all state mutations through Zustand actions, ensuring reactivity across 7 screens and preventing stale UI. Memoized selectors prevent unnecessary re-renders on the Insights screen's aggregation-heavy views.
forbids: Direct localStorage.getItem/setItem calls outside Zustand persist middleware
Storing derived/computed entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) in localStorage

implementationPatterns.title: Zod Validation at Data Write Boundary
implementationPatterns.rule: Every data mutation (addExpense, updateExpense, updateBudget, addCategory, updateSettings) must pass through a Zod schema before writing to the Zustand store. Schemas enforce all PRD data integrity constraints: amount is a positive number ≤ 99999.99 with max 2 decimal places; date is not in the future; note is max 100 characters; paymentMethod is one of the defined enum values; categoryId references an existing category. Validation errors are caught at the store action level and surfaced via the toast notification system.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-orm-strategy decision (Zustand + Zod). Zod at the write boundary is the client-side equivalent of validation-zod-at-boundary from the baseline catalog, adapted for a no-backend architecture where the Zustand store action is the 'boundary'.
forbids: Writing to Zustand store without Zod validation
Inline validation logic in UI components — all validation rules live in schema files

implementationPatterns.title: Tailwind via Design Tokens Only — No Arbitrary Values
implementationPatterns.rule: All styling uses Tailwind utility classes mapped to design tokens defined in tailwind.config.js. The config extends the default theme with CashPulse tokens: colors (blue-600, gray-900, emerald-500, red-500, amber-500, violet-500, orange-500, etc.), fontFamily (Inter with tabular-nums variant), spacing (4px base unit), borderRadius (card: 16px, button: 10px, badge: 20px/full, input: 8px), and boxShadow (card: '0 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,0.06)', elevated: '0 8px 24px rgba(0,0,0,0.10)'). Use CVA for component variant definitions. Arbitrary Tailwind values (e.g., rounded-[13px]) are prohibited unless the exact token value requires bracket notation (e.g., rounded-[10px] for button radius).
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-styling-approach decision (Tailwind + CVA). Ensures strict adherence to NFR-007 design consistency. CVA variant schemas make token compliance a TypeScript compile-time check rather than a code review concern.
forbids: Arbitrary Tailwind values that don't correspond to a defined design token
Inline style attributes for properties expressible as Tailwind utilities
Direct hex color codes in className strings — use token aliases

implementationPatterns.title: Error Envelope via Toast Notifications
implementationPatterns.rule: All user-facing errors are surfaced through the toast notification system with a consistent structure: { severity: 'error' | 'warning' | 'info' | 'success', message: string, action?: { label: string, onClick: () => void } }. Validation errors from Zod are caught in store actions and dispatched as warning toasts with the first validation message. Unexpected errors (e.g., localStorage quota exceeded) are caught by an ErrorBoundary and dispatched as error toasts. Toast messages always include a text label and icon — color is never the sole indicator (WCAG 2.1 AA). Toasts use role='status' for informational messages and role='alert' for errors.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-notification-channel decision (hybrid toasts + banners). Replaces the baseline api-error-rfc7807 pattern since there is no HTTP API. The toast envelope provides a consistent error surface across all 7 screens without requiring each component to implement its own error UI.
forbids: Swallowing errors silently — all caught errors must produce a toast or log entry
Using window.alert() or console.error() as the primary error surface

implementationPatterns.title: No Authentication — Single-User Client-Side App
implementationPatterns.rule: CashPulse has no authentication, authorization, or session management. All data belongs to the single browser profile. No auth middleware, no JWT, no session cookies, no user ID. The UserSettings entity stores preferences (currency, default budget) without any user identity. This is a deliberate architectural decision aligned with scope exclusions, not a deferral.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-auth-strategy (resolved as 'none' by scope-exclusion). Replaces the baseline auth-middleware-required pattern. Explicitly documenting the absence of auth prevents future contributors from adding auth-shaped code (e.g., user ID fields, middleware wrappers) without a deliberate architectural revision.
forbids: Adding userId fields to any entity schema
Importing or configuring auth libraries (next-auth, clerk, supabase-auth)
Adding route guards or middleware that check authentication state

implementationPatterns.title: Structured Console Logging in Development
implementationPatterns.rule: Use a thin logging utility that wraps console.log/warn/error with structured context: { timestamp, level, module, message, data? }. In production builds, only warn and error levels are emitted. In development, all levels are emitted. The logger is imported from a single @/lib/logger module. Store actions log mutations at 'debug' level. Error boundaries log at 'error' level. No external logging service (Sentry, DataDog) is configured — this is a client-side personal app.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Replaces the baseline logging-structured-pino pattern since there is no server-side runtime. Pino is a Node.js logger inappropriate for a browser-only app. Structured console logging provides debuggability during development without adding dependencies.
forbids: Bare console.log calls outside the logger utility
Installing server-side logging libraries (pino, winston, bunyan)

implementationPatterns.title: Zustand as Single Source of Truth — No Server State
implementationPatterns.rule: All application state lives in Zustand stores. There is no server state, no React Query cache, no SWR. Components read state exclusively via Zustand selectors (useStore with selector function) to prevent unnecessary re-renders. Cross-screen state (e.g., selected month, active filters) is held in a dedicated UI slice. Entity state (expenses, categories, budget, settings) is held in entity slices. React component-local state (useState) is reserved only for ephemeral UI state (input focus, popover open, animation phase).
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-store and gap-caching-strategy decisions. Replaces the baseline state-server-only pattern since there is no server. Zustand selectors with shallow equality checks prevent the re-render cascades that would violate the 150ms micro-interaction budget on the Dashboard and Insights screens.
forbids: Using React Query or SWR for data that lives in localStorage
Storing entity data in React component state (useState) — use Zustand store
Prop-drilling entity data more than 1 level — use Zustand selector instead

implementationPatterns.title: Filter State in URL Search Params
implementationPatterns.rule: All user-facing filter state (category filter, date range, search keyword) is serialized into URL search params via React Router's useSearchParams. Filter components read from and write to URL params as the single source of truth. Zustand selectors accept filter params as arguments to compute filtered results. Changing filters updates the URL, which triggers a re-render with the new filtered data. This makes filter state bookmarkable, shareable, and refresh-safe.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-search-strategy decision (URL query-parameter-driven filtering). Separates filter state (URL) from entity state (Zustand) cleanly. Future-proofs for a backend API where query params map directly to API query strings.
forbids: Storing filter state in Zustand or React state when it should be in URL params
Hardcoding filter values that bypass URL params

implementationPatterns.title: Ephemeral Toasts for Actions, Inline Banners for Budget State
implementationPatterns.rule: Use the toast system (NotificationContext + ToastContainer portal) for ephemeral action confirmations: expense saved, expense deleted (with 3-second undo countdown), currency updated, category added/removed. Use inline banners inside BudgetCard for persistent budget threshold states: 'On Track' (emerald), 'Heads Up' (amber, >80%), 'Over Budget' (red, >100%). Budget banners are derived directly from computed MonthSummary state — they are not dispatched as notifications. Toasts auto-dismiss after the PRD-specified duration (2s for currency, 3s for delete undo, 1.5s for export). All toasts include icon + text label (never color alone).
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-notification-channel decision (hybrid toast + banner). Matches each notification type to its natural delivery mechanism per the PRD's interaction matrices.

implementationPatterns.title: Radix Primitives for Focus Management + Motion-Reduce Variant
implementationPatterns.rule: All interactive overlays (Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip, DropdownMenu) use Radix UI primitives from shadcn/ui for automatic focus trapping, keyboard navigation (Tab, Enter, Space, Escape), and ARIA attribute management. Focus rings are applied globally via Tailwind's ring-2 ring-primary ring-offset-2 utilities. All animations use Tailwind's motion-reduce: variant to respect prefers-reduced-motion — animated classes are paired with motion-reduce:transition-none or motion-reduce:animate-none. Charts include role='img' with descriptive aria-label. All interactive elements have minimum 36×36px click targets enforced via min-h-9 min-w-9.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-component-library and gap-styling-approach decisions. Satisfies the hard WCAG 2.1 AA constraint (NFR-003) and the soft prefers-reduced-motion constraint (NFR-004). Radix primitives eliminate the need to hand-implement focus traps and ARIA patterns for 5+ overlay components.
implementationPatterns.example:
// Button with focus ring and motion-reduce
<Button
  className="bg-primary text-white rounded-button h-[52px] w-full
    transition-transform duration-150 active:scale-[0.98]
    motion-reduce:transition-none motion-reduce:transform-none
    focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-primary focus-visible:ring-offset-2"
>
  Save Expense
</Button>
forbids: Custom focus trap implementations when a Radix primitive exists for the pattern
Animations without a motion-reduce: counterpart
Interactive elements smaller than 36×36px (min-h-9 min-w-9)

implementationPatterns.title: CVA Variant Schemas for Shared Components
implementationPatterns.rule: All shared UI components (Button, Badge, Card, Chip, Input) define their visual variants using Class Variance Authority (CVA). Variant schemas are co-located with the component file. Consumers pass typed variant props (e.g., variant='primary', size='lg', severity='warning') — not raw className strings. Use tailwind-merge (cn utility) to safely merge className overrides without specificity conflicts. Component files live in components/ui/ and are the only place where raw Tailwind class strings for design tokens appear.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-styling-approach decision (Tailwind + CVA). CVA makes design token compliance a TypeScript compile-time check. The cn() utility (clsx + twMerge) prevents className conflicts when consumers need to add layout-specific classes.

stackConfig.backend: None (client-side only — backend explicitly out of scope)
stackConfig.styling: Tailwind CSS v3 + Class Variance Authority (CVA) + tailwind-merge

fields.name: id
fields.description: Primary key, generated client-side (crypto.randomUUID)

fields.name: amount
fields.description: Expense amount; must be > 0 and ≤ 99999.99; stored with exactly 2 decimal places (Zod-enforced)

fields.name: description
fields.description: Merchant name or short description of the expense

fields.name: categoryId
fields.description: Foreign key to Category entity

fields.name: date
fields.description: Date of the expense; must not be a future date (Zod-enforced)

fields.name: paymentMethod
fields.description: Enum: CASH | DEBIT_CARD | CREDIT_CARD | UPI_OTHER

fields.name: note
fields.description: Optional user note; max 100 characters

fields.name: createdAt
fields.description: ISO 8601 timestamp of record creation, set client-side at save time

fields.name: id
fields.description: Primary key, generated client-side

fields.name: name
fields.description: Display name of the category; unique(name)

fields.name: color
fields.description: Hex color string (e.g. #3B82F6) used for donut segments, border accents, and dot indicators; must match pattern #[0-9A-Fa-f]{6}

fields.name: icon
fields.description: Icon identifier string (e.g. Lucide icon name or emoji) rendered in category chip and expense row circle

fields.name: isDefault
fields.description: True for system-seeded categories that cannot be deleted; false for user-created categories

fields.name: sortOrder
fields.description: Display order in category selector grid and legend; default categories seeded 0–N, user categories appended

fields.name: id
fields.description: Primary key

fields.name: month
fields.description: ISO month string YYYY-MM (e.g. 2026-03); unique(month) — one budget record per calendar month

fields.name: monthlyLimit
fields.description: User-defined spending cap for the month; must be > 0

fields.name: currency
fields.description: ISO 4217 currency code: USD | EUR | GBP | INR | JPY

fields.description: Singleton record; always a fixed well-known UUID (e.g. 'settings-singleton')

fields.name: currency
fields.description: Active display currency: USD | EUR | GBP | INR | JPY; default USD

fields.name: defaultMonthlyBudget
fields.description: Fallback monthly budget used when no month-specific Budget record exists; default 2000.00

fields.description: ISO month string YYYY-MM; primary key and natural identifier for this derived aggregate

fields.description: Count of Expense records for the month

fields.name: dailyAverage
fields.description: totalSpent / number of days elapsed in month (or total days if month is past)

fields.name: budgetStatus
fields.description: Enum: ON_TRACK (< 80% spent) | HEADS_UP (80–100%) | OVER_BUDGET (> 100%)

fields.name: remainingAmount
fields.description: budgetLimit − totalSpent; can be negative when over budget

fields.description: Part of composite PK; references Category

fields.description: ISO month string YYYY-MM; part of composite PK. unique(categoryId, month)

fields.name: totalAmount
fields.description: Sum of Expense.amount for this category in this month

fields.name: percentage
fields.description: totalAmount / MonthSummary.totalSpent × 100; used for donut segments and stacked bar proportions

fields.name: transactionCount
fields.description: Count of Expense records for this category in this month

fields.name: date
fields.description: Calendar date; primary key for this derived aggregate

fields.name: totalAmount
fields.description: Sum of Expense.amount for the day; used for bar/line chart data points

fields.name: transactionCount
fields.description: Count of Expense records for the day; shown in chart hover tooltip

additions.description: List expenses with optional filters. Query params: month (YYYY-MM), categoryId (uuid), paymentMethod (CASH|DEBIT_CARD|CREDIT_CARD|UPI_OTHER), search (string — matches description/note/amount), page (integer, default 1), limit (integer, default 50). Returns paginated array of Expense objects joined with Category (id, name, color, icon). Supports feat-003 Recent Expenses List, feat-024 Search Expenses. Response 200: { data: Expense[], total: integer, page: integer, limit: integer }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope. Expense shape: { id: uuid, amount: decimal, description: string, categoryId: uuid, category: { id: uuid, name: string, color: string, icon: string }, date: date, paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string, createdAt: timestamptz }.

Create a new expense. Request body: { amount: decimal (>0, ≤99999.99, 2dp), description: string (max 255), categoryId: uuid, date: date (not future), paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string (max 100) }. Supports feat-005 Add/Edit Expense Form. Response 201: Expense (full object with joined category). Response 400: ErrorEnvelope (Zod validation failures). Response 404: ErrorEnvelope (categoryId not found).
Retrieve a single expense by UUID. Supports feat-004 Expense Detail Popover. Response 200: Expense (full object with joined category). Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.

Replace an existing expense. Request body identical to POST /api/expenses. Supports feat-004 Edit button (pre-filled form) and feat-005 Add/Edit Expense Form. Response 200: updated Expense. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.
Delete an expense by UUID. Supports feat-004 Delete with inline confirmation. Response 204: no body. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.

List all categories ordered by sortOrder ascending. Supports feat-002 Donut Chart legend, feat-003 category filter, feat-005 category selector grid, feat-009 Top Categories, feat-014 Settings category manager. Response 200: { data: Category[] }. Category shape: { id: uuid, name: string, color: string, icon: string, isDefault: boolean, sortOrder: integer }.

Create a new user-defined category. Request body: { name: string (max 50, unique), color: string (hex #[0-9A-Fa-f]{6}), icon: string (max 50) }. isDefault is always false for user-created categories; sortOrder auto-assigned as max+1. Supports feat-015 Inline Category Creation. Response 201: Category. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope (validation or duplicate name).

Delete a user-created category (isDefault=false only). Supports feat-014 Settings category manager trash icon. Response 204: no body. Response 403: ErrorEnvelope (attempt to delete default category). Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.

Retrieve the Budget record for a specific month (YYYY-MM). Supports feat-001 Budget Summary Dashboard, feat-014 Settings monthly budget input. Response 200: { id: uuid, month: string, monthlyLimit: decimal, currency: string }. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope (no month-specific budget; caller should fall back to UserSettings.defaultMonthlyBudget).

Upsert the Budget record for a specific month (YYYY-MM). Request body: { monthlyLimit: decimal (>0), currency: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY' }. Creates if absent, replaces if present. Supports feat-014 Settings monthly budget input. Response 200: Budget. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.

Retrieve the singleton UserSettings record. Supports feat-014 Settings Dialog (currency selector, default monthly budget). Response 200: { id: uuid, currency: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY', defaultMonthlyBudget: decimal }.

Update the singleton UserSettings record. Request body: { currency?: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY', defaultMonthlyBudget?: decimal (>0) }. Supports feat-014 Settings Dialog, feat-018 Currency Change Toast (triggers toast on currency change), feat-022 Multi-Currency Support. Response 200: updated UserSettings. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.

Retrieve the computed MonthSummary aggregate for a given month (YYYY-MM). Supports feat-001 Budget Summary Dashboard (spent, limit, remaining, status), feat-007 Spending Insights Period Summary (totalSpent, dailyAverage, transactionCount), feat-011 Month-over-Month Comparison (call for current and previous month). Response 200: { month: string, totalSpent: decimal, transactionCount: integer, dailyAverage: decimal, budgetLimit: decimal, budgetStatus: 'ON_TRACK'|'HEADS_UP'|'OVER_BUDGET', remainingAmount: decimal }. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope (month has no expense data; return zeroed summary instead — 404 only if month is entirely unknown).

Retrieve CategorySpending breakdown for a given month (YYYY-MM), sorted by totalAmount descending. Supports feat-002 Donut Chart segments and legend, feat-009 Top Categories stacked bar and sorted list. Response 200: { month: string, data: CategorySpending[] }. CategorySpending shape: { categoryId: uuid, category: { id: uuid, name: string, color: string, icon: string }, month: string, totalAmount: decimal, percentage: decimal, transactionCount: integer }.

Retrieve DailySpending data points for every calendar day in the given month (YYYY-MM). Days with no expenses are included with totalAmount=0 and transactionCount=0 to ensure complete chart x-axis. Supports feat-008 Daily Spending Bar/Line Chart (data points, average line). Response 200: { month: string, dailyAverage: decimal, data: DailySpending[] }. DailySpending shape: { date: date, totalAmount: decimal, transactionCount: integer }.

Retrieve top QuickAddSuggestion chips (max 4), ranked by frequency desc then lastUsedAt desc. Supports feat-006 Quick Entry Suggestions. Returns empty array (not 404) when no frequent expenses exist, driving the hidden-section behavior. Response 200: { data: QuickAddSuggestion[] }. QuickAddSuggestion shape: { id: uuid, description: string, amount: decimal, categoryId: uuid, category: { id: uuid, name: string, color: string, icon: string }, paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', frequency: integer, lastUsedAt: timestamptz }.

Export expenses as a CSV file download. Query params: month (YYYY-MM, optional — omit for all months), categoryId (uuid, optional). Returns CSV with columns: date, description, category, amount, paymentMethod, note. Supports feat-016 Export CSV button on Insights screen. Response 200: text/csv with Content-Disposition: attachment; filename='cashpulse-export.csv'. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.

List all RecurringTemplate records ordered by createdAt desc. Supports feat-023 Recurring Expense Templates. Response 200: { data: RecurringTemplate[] }. RecurringTemplate shape: { id: uuid, description: string, amount: decimal, categoryId: uuid, category: { id: uuid, name: string, color: string, icon: string }, paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string, createdAt: timestamptz }.

Create a new RecurringTemplate. Request body: { description: string (max 255), amount: decimal (>0, ≤99999.99), categoryId: uuid, paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string (max 100) }. Supports feat-023. Response 201: RecurringTemplate. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope (categoryId not found).
Delete a RecurringTemplate by UUID. Supports feat-023. Response 204: no body. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.

Instantiate a new Expense from a RecurringTemplate, using today's date (or a provided date). Request body: { date?: date (not future, defaults to today) }. Supports feat-023 quick-log from template. Response 201: Expense (full object). Response 400: ErrorEnvelope. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope (template not found).

List CategoryBudget records. Query params: month (YYYY-MM, required), categoryId (uuid, optional). Supports feat-025 Per-Category Budget Limits. Response 200: { data: CategoryBudget[] }. CategoryBudget shape: { id: uuid, categoryId: uuid, category: { id: uuid, name: string, color: string, icon: string }, month: string, monthlyLimit: decimal }. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope.

Upsert a per-category budget limit for a given categoryId and month (YYYY-MM). Request body: { monthlyLimit: decimal (>0) }. Supports feat-025 Per-Category Budget Limits settings UI. Response 200: CategoryBudget. Response 400: ErrorEnvelope. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope (categoryId not found).
Remove a per-category budget limit for a given categoryId and month. Supports feat-025. Response 204: no body. Response 404: ErrorEnvelope.

props.expense: { id: string; amount: number; description: string; category: { id: string; name: string; color: string; icon: string }; date: string; paymentMethod: 'CASH' | 'DEBIT_CARD' | 'CREDIT_CARD' | 'UPI_OTHER'; note?: string } | null

props.categories: Array<{ id: string; name: string; color: string; icon: string }>

props.items: Array<{ id: string | null; label: string; color?: string }>

dataBindings.transform: mapStatus({ ON_TRACK: 'On Track', HEADS_UP: 'Heads Up', OVER_BUDGET: 'Over Budget' })
mapPaymentMethod({ CASH: 'Cash', DEBIT_CARD: 'Debit Card', CREDIT_CARD: 'Credit Card', UPI_OTHER: 'UPI/Other' })

navigationTargets.trigger: expense-row: onClick (opens expense detail popover anchored to row)
popover-actions: onEdit (navigates to Add Expense pre-filled with expense data)
save-button: onClick (after successful save, navigates to dashboard)
date-picker-field: onFocus / onClick (opens calendar sheet below input)
export-csv-button: onExport (stays on screen, triggers CSV download via /api/expenses/export)

dataBindings.transform: mapPaymentMethod({ CASH: 'Cash', DEBIT_CARD: 'Debit Card', CREDIT_CARD: 'Credit Card', UPI_OTHER: 'UPI/Other' })

navigationTargets.trigger: detail-popover-actions: onEdit (navigates to Add Expense with pre-filled expense data)
detail-popover-actions: onDelete (after delete confirmation, closes popover and returns to dashboard)
calendar-day-grid: onDaySelect (closes sheet and returns selected date to Add Expense form)
filter-dropdown-list: onSelect (applies category filter and closes sheet, returning to dashboard with filtered expense list)
filter-dropdown-root: onClickOutside (dismisses sheet without changing filter)

structured.themeStrategy: Single-theme (light-only). CashPulse is a greenfield client-side app with no dark-mode requirement stated. All tokens are defined as static values in tailwind.config.js under `theme.extend`. Semantic aliases (e.g. `color.budget.*`, `color.chart.*`, `color.nav.*`) are layered on top of the primitive palette (gray-*, blue-*, emerald-*, etc.) so that component CVA variants reference semantic names rather than raw Tailwind primitives. This keeps future theming opt-in: a dark-mode pass would only need to remap the semantic layer without touching component code. Category colors (violet-500, orange-500, and any user-defined hex values) are applied as dynamic inline styles at runtime since they are data-driven and cannot be statically tokenised.

tasks.title: Build Dashboard left column: BudgetSummaryCard and CategoryDonutChartCard

tasks.title: Build ExpenseDetailPopover with edit/delete actions and inline delete confirmation

Edit button navigates to /add-expense?edit={id} with pre-filled form. Delete button with inline confirmation: text changes to 'Confirm delete?' with 3-second auto-revert timer. On confirm, expense is deleted from store and success toast shown. Keyboard accessible with focus trapping.

tasks.description: Implement the AddExpensePage with single-column form (max-width 520px): HeroAmountInput (32px, auto-focused, numeric-only with decimal auto-formatting), CategorySelectorGrid (3x2 chips with colored selection states), InlineCategoryCreation ('+ New Category' link expanding to text input + 6-color picker + Save mini-button), DatePickerField with mini calendar (Radix Popover, no future dates), PaymentMethodSelector (Cash/Debit Card/Credit Card/UPI-Other chips), NoteInput (100 char max with counter at 80+), and full-width SaveButton (disabled when amount $0 or no category, shows '✓ Saved!' emerald state then navigates to /). Support edit mode via ?edit=id query param pre-filling all fields.

tasks.title: Build Quick Entry Suggestions chips on Add Expense screen
tasks.description: Implement the QuickEntrySuggestions component: a horizontal scrollable row of 3-4 suggestion chips below the save button, computed from the QuickAddSuggestion memoized selector (frequent past expenses). Each chip shows emoji + merchant + amount (e.g., '☕ Starbucks $4.50'). Clicking a chip auto-fills the entire form with cascade animation (50ms per field delay). Hidden entirely when no frequent expenses exist. Respects prefers-reduced-motion by disabling cascade animation.

tasks.title: Build Insights page: Period Summary, Daily Spending Chart, and Month Comparison
tasks.description: Implement PeriodSummaryHeader with three StatCell components (Total Spent, Daily Average, Transactions) separated by vertical dividers, each with value, label, and trend indicator (up/down arrow with percentage vs last month, green for decrease, red for increase). Build DailySpendingChartCard with Bar/Line toggle using Recharts: bar chart (blue-600 bars, red-500 for 2x+ average), line chart (smooth curve with filled area), hover tooltips (date, amount, count), dashed amber average line, staggered entry animation. Build MonthComparisonCard with side-by-side vertical bars (current vs last month) growing from bottom, and comparison badge ('You spent $X less/more').

tasks.title: Build Insights page: Top Categories Breakdown and Biggest Expenses list
tasks.description: Implement TopCategoriesBreakdownCard with horizontal stacked bar (proportional category segments that lift on hover with tooltip) and sorted category list (colored dot, name, individual progress bar, amount, percentage). Build BiggestExpensesCard listing top 5 expenses by amount with rank circle (#1 highlighted blue-600), merchant name, category, date, and amount. Include InsightsEmptyState ('Not enough data yet' with chart icon) shown when no spending data exists.

tasks.title: Assemble InsightsPage with Export CSV and responsive layout
tasks.description: Create the InsightsPage component composing TopNavBar, PeriodSummaryHeader, DailySpendingChartCard, TopCategoriesBreakdownCard, BiggestExpensesCard, MonthComparisonCard, and ExportCsvButton. Implement ExportCsvButton that generates CSV from expense data (date, description, category, amount, paymentMethod, note columns), triggers download, and shows '✓ Exported' emerald text for 1.5 seconds. Wire up responsive layout and empty state logic.

tasks.title: Build Settings Dialog with currency, budget, and category management
tasks.description: Implement SettingsDialog as a centered Radix Dialog modal (max-width 420px, dark scrim overlay) with: CurrencySelector dropdown (USD, EUR, GBP, INR, JPY), BudgetInput numeric input with currency prefix (default $2,000), CategoryManager with colored dot list, trash icons (only for non-default categories), and '+ Add category' link that opens inline creation row. Dismissible via close button, scrim click, or Escape. Currency change triggers toast notification ('Currency updated to EUR'). All amounts update with new currency symbol.

tasks.title: Wire up App routing, error boundary, and global providers
tasks.description: Finalize App.tsx with React Router routes (/ → DashboardPage, /add-expense → AddExpensePage, /insights → InsightsPage), wrap with NotificationProvider and ToastContainer portal. Add ErrorBoundary component that catches unexpected errors and dispatches error toasts. Ensure SettingsDialog is accessible from all pages via the gear icon. Wire up URL search params for category filter and search. Verify all navigation flows: tab switching, month navigation, edit expense redirect, save-and-return.

tasks.title: Accessibility audit, reduced motion support, and responsive polish
tasks.description: Audit and enhance all components for WCAG 2.1 AA compliance: ensure all interactive elements have ARIA roles/labels, charts have role='img' with descriptive aria-label, keyboard navigation works with Tab/Enter/Space/Escape throughout. Apply motion-reduce: variants to all animations (chart animations → instant render, bounce/cascade → disabled, slide transitions → instant cuts). Verify responsive breakpoints across all three pages. Ensure color is never the sole indicator (all status badges have text labels, chart tooltips accessible via keyboard).
